    - `monitor-dashboard-page-header.stories.tsx`
    - `monitor-dashboard-instance-card.stories.tsx`
- Reason:
  - after introducing `components/monitor-dashboard-widgets` as the shared public contract surface, Storybook titles should name the actual governed component contracts rather than broad labels like `Panel`, `PageHeader`, or `InstanceCard`
  - explicit titles improve searchability, reduce ambiguity, and make contract audits easier
- Cross-app comparison:
  - these stories already point at shared public components:
    - `DashboardPanel`
    - `DashboardPageHeader`
    - `DashboardInstanceCard`
  - the mismatch was only in Storybook taxonomy, not in runtime implementation
- Decision:
  - keep the existing component APIs and stories
  - align Storybook titles to the real public component names
- Migration:
  - updated titles to:
    - `Business/Monitor/DashboardPanel`
    - `Business/Monitor/DashboardPageHeader`
    - `Business/Monitor/DashboardInstanceCard`
- Storybook:
  - no new stories
  - existing monitor widget stories now advertise the same names as the public components they verify

### Log and ops-analysis public-contract alignment
- Candidate:
  - Storybook stories importing shared business widgets and config sections directly from app-local paths:
    - `app/log/(pages)/analysis/dashBoard/widgets/*`
    - `app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/dashBoard/widgets/*`
    - `app/ops-analysis/components/*`
    - `app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/dashBoard/components/viewConfig/sections/*`
- Reason:
  - these stories were already acting as component contracts, but they bypassed the shared components surface and imported from product implementation directories
  - that weakened the Storybook-first rule because the source of truth for shared UI behavior still depended on app-local paths instead of explicit public component entrypoints
- Cross-app comparison:
  - log analysis widgets are shared within the log-analysis dashboard domain
  - ops-analysis dashboard widgets are shared within the ops-analysis dashboard domain
  - ops-analysis config sections are reused configuration surfaces rather than one-off page fragments
  - the common issue across all three families was contract exposure, not duplicate rendering logic
- Decision:
  - do not fork or physically relocate implementations in this slice
  - add explicit components-level public entrypoints for each shared family:
    - `components/log-analysis-widgets`
    - `components/ops-analysis-widgets`
    - `components/ops-analysis-config-sections`
  - repoint Storybook to those public entrypoints so the contract boundary becomes explicit
- Migration:
  - added barrel entrypoints for the three families above
  - updated log-analysis and ops-analysis stories to import from the new components public surfaces
  - left app runtime usage untouched to keep the migration low-risk and incremental
- Storybook:
  - Storybook now consumes these shared business families through `components/*` public entrypoints instead of direct `app/*` implementation paths
  - this narrows the gap between “shared in practice” and “shared by governed contract”
  - follow-up cleanup also re-exported the small set of story-only domain types needed by these families through the same public entrypoints, so `src/stories` no longer imports from `@/app/*` at all

### Monitor dashboard widget public-contract alignment
- Candidate:
  - monitor dashboard shared widget family currently implemented under:
    - `app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/*`
  - Storybook stories importing those widgets directly from the app path
- Reason:
  - these widgets already behave like governed shared business components:
    - multiple Storybook entries exist for them
    - they are reused across monitor dashboard object views
  - but their Storybook contract path still pointed into `src/app`, which weakened the architectural signal of “shared component public API lives under components”
- Cross-app comparison:
  - this family is business-shared within the monitor domain rather than framework-global
  - the problem was not duplicate runtime implementations; the problem was that the public Storybook contract bypassed the shared-components surface and imported straight from app-local implementation paths
- Decision:
  - do not duplicate or fork the widget implementations
  - establish a formal shared public entrypoint under `components/monitor-dashboard-widgets`
  - point Storybook at that public contract instead of the app implementation path
- Migration:
  - added `components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/index.ts` as the shared public export surface for monitor dashboard widgets
  - updated monitor widget stories to import from `@/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets`
  - preserved the existing runtime implementations and app-internal usage for now, keeping this slice low-risk and contract-first
- Storybook:
  - Storybook now consumes the monitor dashboard widget family through the shared components public surface rather than through `src/app`
  - this makes the governed contract explicit while leaving room for a later physical implementation move if needed

### Opspilot Storybook contract alignment
- Candidate:
  - shared OpsPilot component contracts whose Storybook coverage existed but was obscured by alias file names:
    - `components/opspilot-studio-card`
    - `components/opspilot-skill-card`
    - `components/opspilot-entity-card`
    - `components/opspilot-approval-card`
  - scheduler input group barrel:
    - `components/opspilot-scheduler-inputs/index.ts`
- Reason:
  - governance audits that compare shared components to Storybook entries should not rely on human memory of alternate story file names
  - the four OpsPilot card-style components already had stories, but the story filenames hid the one-to-one contract relationship
  - `opspilot-scheduler-inputs` is a shared business component group exported through a barrel, and its subcomponents had individual stories without any grouped overview that represented the shared scheduler-input surface as a cohesive module
- Cross-app comparison:
  - the card components are reused across OpsPilot list views and already represent stable business contracts
  - the scheduler inputs are reused together in scheduling flows, with existing individual stories for:
    - `CronEditor`
    - `MonthDayPicker`
    - `TimeListField`
  - what was missing was explicit Storybook discoverability at the shared module level, not another abstraction layer
- Decision:
  - keep the existing components as-is
  - make Storybook contract mapping explicit by aligning story filenames with component names where the component contract already exists
  - add a grouped Storybook overview for the scheduler input module rather than inventing a fake aggregate runtime component
- Migration:
  - renamed story files to align with shared component names:
    - `studio-card.stories.tsx` -> `opspilot-studio-card.stories.tsx`
    - `skill-card.stories.tsx` -> `opspilot-skill-card.stories.tsx`
    - `entity-card.stories.tsx` -> `opspilot-entity-card.stories.tsx`
    - `approval-card.stories.tsx` -> `opspilot-approval-card.stories.tsx`
  - added `stories/opspilot-scheduler-inputs.stories.tsx` as a grouped overview using the barrel exports
- Storybook:
  - improved discoverability and contract auditability for existing shared OpsPilot components
  - added a business-domain overview story for scheduler inputs without broadening the runtime API surface

### Section-header internal convergence
- Candidate:
  - repeated titled section rows inside shared components:
    - `components/integration-step-callout/index.tsx`
    - `components/k8s-collector-install/index.tsx`
    - existing framework primitive `components/section-header/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - these shared components were independently re-implementing the same framework pattern:
    - leading icon
    - concise section title
    - spacing block above a subordinate content surface
  - the differences were only icon choice, icon color/size, and bottom margin
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `IntegrationStepCallout` is used by monitor/log k8s flows as the reusable “prerequisites / step note” heading
  - `K8sCollectorInstall` is used as the reusable install/verify step surface across integration flows
  - both were already shared components, which made their internal duplicate title-row implementations a stronger governance target than isolated page-local `h3` usage
- Decision:
  - do not introduce another title-row component
  - strengthen `SectionHeader` slightly so the existing framework primitive can absorb these repeated shared-component internals
- Migration:
  - added `titleClassName` to `SectionHeader`
  - migrated `IntegrationStepCallout` to render its title row through `SectionHeader`
  - migrated both titled blocks inside `K8sCollectorInstall` to render through `SectionHeader`
  - preserved each surface's existing icons, spacing, and downstream public APIs
- Storybook:
  - updated `stories/section-header.stories.tsx` with a custom title-tone example to cover the expanded framework usage
  - no new component story; this was an internal convergence into an existing framework primitive

### Introduction banner variant cleanup
- Candidate:
  - the `banner` variant in `components/introduction/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - `Introduction` is a shared framework component for explanatory intro cards used by alarm and cmdb settings pages
  - the `banner` variant suggested a second visual contract, but it had no product consumers and only existed in Storybook
  - keeping an unconsumed variant works against the governance goal of having Storybook reflect real, supported component contracts instead of speculative ones
- Cross-app comparison:
  - real app usage across alarm and cmdb consistently used the card-like explanatory pattern
  - no app used `Introduction` as a banner-style page hero
  - audit also confirmed that `TopSection` already covers the separate “detail/manage page header” semantic, so forcing `Introduction` to keep a second pseudo-header variant would blur component boundaries rather than improve reuse
- Decision:
  - keep `Introduction` focused on the explanatory-card contract
  - remove the unused `banner` variant instead of preserving a dead alternative API
- Migration:
  - removed `variant` from `IntroductionProps`
  - deleted the unused banner rendering branch and related stylesheet rules
  - removed the banner-only Storybook example
- Storybook:
  - updated `stories/introduction.stories.tsx` to reflect the real supported contract only

### Chart-series-state hook boundary cleanup
- Candidate:
  - the shared chart state module in `components/chart-series-state/index.ts`
- Reason:
  - this module does not render UI and does not define a Storybook-level component contract
  - it only provides reusable chart-series state helpers:
    - series key extraction
    - dimension detail aggregation
    - visible-series toggle state
    - generated color allocation
  - keeping it under `src/components` blurred the boundary between reusable UI primitives and reusable chart logic
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor chart components and mlops chart components both consumed the same state logic
  - Storybook used it only through a demo wrapper, which confirms the reusable asset is the hook behavior rather than a standalone component surface
  - no app needed a separate rendered component named `chart-series-state`
- Decision:
  - classify it as a shared hook, not a shared component
  - move it to `hooks/useChartSeriesState.ts`
  - keep the Storybook demo only as a behavioral example for downstream chart components, not as a component contract source
- Migration:
  - moved `chart-series-state` to `hooks/useChartSeriesState.ts`
  - updated monitor chart components, mlops chart components, and the Storybook demo to import from the new hook path
  - removed the empty `components/chart-series-state` directory
- Storybook:
  - no new shared component story
  - existing demo story now points at the hook location through its demo wrapper

### Log-chart support modules boundary cleanup
- Candidate:
  - misclassified chart support modules in:
    - `components/log-chart-colors/index.tsx`
    - `components/log-chart-style/index.ts`
- Reason:
  - neither module expressed a UI component contract
  - `log-chart-colors` is a theme-aware hook that returns palette tokens for charts
  - `log-chart-style` is a pure utility module for gradient and area-fill helpers
  - keeping hook/util modules inside `src/components` made the component inventory noisy and weakened the Storybook-first governance boundary
- Cross-app comparison:
  - both modules were consumed by shared log chart components (`log-donut-chart`, `log-kpi-card`) and by many log dashboard widgets
  - they had zero standalone Storybook value because there is no user-facing component API to validate
  - the repeated pattern across those consumers was dependency on shared chart theming helpers, not dependency on a rendered UI primitive
- Decision:
  - do not keep them as shared components
  - reclassify them into the correct architectural layers:
    - `hooks/useChartColors.ts`
    - `utils/chartStyle.ts`
  - remove the misleading component directories entirely
- Migration:
  - moved `log-chart-colors` to `hooks/useChartColors.ts`
  - moved `log-chart-style` to `utils/chartStyle.ts`
  - updated log dashboard widgets plus `log-donut-chart` and `log-kpi-card` to import from the new locations
  - removed the empty `components/log-chart-colors` and `components/log-chart-style` directories
- Storybook:
  - none; no component contract changed and no new shared component was introduced

### Stale component directory cleanup
- Candidate:
  - empty component directories left behind after prior convergences:
    - `components/integration-detail-top-section`
    - `components/node-manager-detail-intro`
    - `components/oneline-ellipsis-intro`
    - `components/opspilot-detail-top-section`
    - `components/routed-top-section`
    - `components/system-manager-action-search-bar`
- Reason:
  - these directories no longer contained source files, but they still polluted the component tree and falsely suggested remaining live components
  - keeping empty component directories works against the governance goal of having no garbage components
- Cross-app comparison:
  - all of them were leftovers from earlier framework/business convergences
  - none had runtime references or Storybook references remaining
- Decision:
  - remove the empty directories entirely instead of leaving archival husks in `src/components`
- Migration:
  - deleted the empty directories listed above
  - re-verified that no matching component references remain in `src`
- Storybook:
  - none; this was cleanup after prior story removals

### Routed-top-section inline convergence
- Candidate:
  - parallel routing-only top-section helper in:
    - `components/routed-top-section/index.tsx`
    - `components/routed-detail-layout-shell/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - `RoutedTopSection` had no remaining independent consumers and existed only to map pathname metadata into `TopSection`
  - `RoutedDetailLayoutShell` was its only runtime consumer, so keeping both created an unnecessary extra framework layer
- Cross-app comparison:
  - route-driven title/description mapping is still required by node-manager and opspilot detail layouts
  - the mapping semantics stay local to `RoutedDetailLayoutShell`; no separate composition point remains
- Decision:
  - inline the routing helper logic into `RoutedDetailLayoutShell`
  - remove `RoutedTopSection` and its standalone Storybook entry
- Migration:
  - updated `components/routed-detail-layout-shell/index.tsx` to resolve pathname matches directly
  - removed `components/routed-top-section/index.tsx`
  - removed `stories/routed-top-section.stories.tsx`
- Storybook:
  - none added; routed behavior remains covered through `RoutedDetailLayoutShell`

### Top-section integration variant convergence
- Candidate:
  - parallel top-section implementations in:
    - `components/top-section/index.tsx`
    - `components/integration-detail-top-section/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - both components expressed the same semantic contract:
    - optional leading visual
    - title plus single-line descriptive copy
    - framed hero-like section at the top of a page or detail view
  - the differences were presentational only: image icon support, sizing, border treatment, and text scale
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `top-section` serves general detail and settings pages across multiple apps
  - `integration-detail-top-section` served monitor/log integration detail pages with a larger image icon and bordered surface
  - no behavior or business-domain logic differed between them
- Decision:
  - converge the integration-specific variant into `TopSection`
  - remove the parallel business component instead of preserving two top-section contracts with overlapping meaning
- Migration:
  - extended `components/top-section/index.tsx` with `iconSrc`, `variant`, and richer ReactNode support for title/content
  - migrated `integration-detail-layout-shell` to use `TopSection`
  - removed `integration-detail-top-section` and its story
- Storybook:
  - updated `stories/top-section.stories.tsx` with the integration variant
  - removed `stories/integration-detail-top-section.stories.tsx`

### Detail-intro framework convergence
- Candidate:
  - repeated detail summary intro components in:
    - `components/oneline-ellipsis-intro/index.tsx`
    - `components/node-manager-detail-intro/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - both components were rendering the same detail-intro semantics:
    - prominent one-line title
    - optional supporting summary text
    - optional icon for entity identity
    - overflow-safe display inside side-menu intro regions
  - the differences were orientation and whether icon/description were shown
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `opspilot` uses a horizontal text summary with title + description
  - `node-manager` uses a centered vertical icon + title summary
  - both live inside the same detail layout intro slot and differ only in presentation variant
- Decision:
  - replace the parallel intro components with one framework intro component
  - remove the old non-business duplicates entirely instead of preserving thin wrappers
- Migration:
  - added `components/detail-intro/index.tsx`
  - migrated `opspilot-detail-layout-shell`, `node-manager-detail-layout-shell`, `node-manager cloudregion mainlayout`, `opspilot studio/detail`, and `opspilot knowledge/detail`
  - removed `oneline-ellipsis-intro` and `node-manager-detail-intro` plus their stories
- Storybook:
  - added `stories/detail-intro.stories.tsx`
  - registered as `Framework/Content/DetailIntro`

### Menu-resolution helper convergence
- Candidate:
  - repeated menu-source resolution logic in:
    - `components/layout/sub-layout/index.tsx`
    - `cmdb/(pages)/assetData/components/asset-data-layout/index.tsx`
    - `opspilot/(pages)/studio/detail/layout.tsx`
- Reason:
  - these layouts were independently re-implementing the same underlying menu matching behavior:
    - find the closest matching menu path for the current route
    - resolve visible child menu items
    - filter out non-menu or directory-only entries
  - the variation was in downstream business filtering, not in the traversal semantics
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `sub-layout` resolves the active layer for generic side-menu rendering
  - `asset-data-layout` resolves the nearest ancestor menu set, then applies model-specific filtering
  - `studio/detail` resolves the nearest ancestor menu set, then applies bot-type filtering
  - route-specific filtering logic remains local to each app and should stay local
- Decision:
  - do not introduce a new shared component here
  - extract shared menu-resolution helpers underneath the existing layout components to remove repeated traversal logic first
- Migration:
  - expanded `utils/menuHelpers.ts` with shared helpers for deepest matched items, first-layer siblings, and closest ancestor child menus
  - migrated `sub-layout`, `asset-data-layout`, and `studio/detail/layout.tsx` to consume those helpers
  - preserved all page-local filtering and existing layout component APIs
- Storybook:
  - none for this slice; no component contract changed

### Routed-detail-layout-shell framework extraction
- Candidate:
  - repeated routed-detail layout composition in:
    - `node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/mainlayout/layout.tsx`
    - `opspilot/(pages)/studio/detail/layout.tsx`
    - `opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/detail/layout.tsx`
- Reason:
  - these layouts were repeating the same framework composition:
    - `DetailLayoutShell`
    - `RoutedTopSection` driven by pathname-to-title mapping
    - optional intro summary block
    - back navigation wiring and optional custom menus/progress
  - only the route map content and intro summary source differed
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `node-manager` uses fixed menu items and an icon intro
  - `opspilot studio` uses filtered menu items and a text summary intro
  - `opspilot knowledge` uses a text summary intro plus task-progress slotting
  - each layout keeps its own route definitions, back behavior, and menu derivation
- Decision:
  - extract a framework routed-detail shell instead of repeating `DetailLayoutShell + RoutedTopSection` by hand
  - keep domain layouts thin so route metadata and domain-specific intro sources stay local
- Migration:
  - added `components/routed-detail-layout-shell/index.tsx`
  - migrated the three layouts above to consume the shared framework shell
  - preserved all page-local route maps, progress behavior, and custom menu logic
- Storybook:
  - added `stories/routed-detail-layout-shell.stories.tsx`
  - registered as `Framework/Layout/Detail/RoutedShell`

### Detail-layout-shell framework extraction
- Candidate:
  - repeated detail-page shell wrappers in:
    - `components/opspilot-detail-layout-shell/index.tsx`
    - `components/integration-detail-layout-shell/index.tsx`
    - `components/node-manager-detail-layout-shell/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - all three wrappers were repeating the same layout composition:
    - `WithSideMenuLayout`/`SubLayout` as the base
    - top detail section
    - optional intro summary block
    - back navigation wiring
    - optional custom menu items and progress slotting
  - differences lived in domain-specific top-section and intro components, not in the shell contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `opspilot` adds summary text and optional progress/task UI
  - `integration` supplies a custom top section and custom menus
  - `node-manager` supplies a custom intro card and standard top section
  - each domain keeps its own summary semantics, titles, and menu derivation
- Decision:
  - extract a framework detail-layout shell beneath the business wrappers instead of keeping parallel wrapper implementations
  - retain business wrappers as thin semantic adapters so domain APIs stay stable
- Migration:
  - added `components/detail-layout-shell/index.tsx`
  - migrated the three business shell components above to compose the shared framework shell
  - migrated `node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/mainlayout/layout.tsx` and `system-manager/(pages)/channel/detail/layout.tsx` to consume the shared framework shell directly
  - preserved all page-facing business props and downstream usage sites
- Storybook:
  - added `stories/detail-layout-shell.stories.tsx`
  - registered as `Framework/Layout/Detail/Shell`

### Panel-shell framework extraction
- Candidate:
  - repeated titled panel shells with scrollable bodies in:
    - `components/system-manager-application-menu/source-menu-tree.tsx`
    - `system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/menu/config/page.tsx`
    - `monitor/(pages)/search/queryPanel.tsx`
- Reason:
  - all three surfaces were independently composing the same structural panel pattern:
    - optional titled header
    - scrollable body region
    - optional footer action row
    - framed overflow-hidden container with app-specific surface styling
  - the differences were header content, footer presence, widths, and surface colors, not panel semantics
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `source-menu-tree` uses a compact titled card with only header + body
  - `menu/config` uses the same shell for a workspace editor main panel with editable header actions
  - `monitor/search/queryPanel` uses the same shell as a left utility sidebar with footer actions and collapsible width
  - drag-drop behavior, query state, and menu editing logic remain local and should stay local
- Decision:
  - extract a framework panel shell instead of preserving ad hoc header/body/footer wrappers
  - keep the API compositional: `header`, `children`, optional `footer`, plus class hooks for outer/header/body/footer styling
- Migration:
  - added `components/panel-shell/index.tsx`
  - migrated `source-menu-tree`, `application/manage/menu/config/page.tsx`, and `monitor/search/queryPanel.tsx`
  - preserved each surface's own interaction logic and sizing behavior
- Storybook:
  - added `stories/panel-shell.stories.tsx`
  - registered as `Framework/Layout/PageScaffold/PanelShell`

### Search-action-bar framework promotion
- Candidate:
  - repeated right-aligned search-plus-actions toolbars in:
    - `system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/role/UserTab.tsx`
    - `system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/role/OrganizationTab.tsx`
    - `system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/data/page.tsx`
    - `system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/menu/page.tsx`
    - `node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/variable/page.tsx`
    - `opspilot/(pages)/settings/manage/page.tsx`
    - `opspilot/(pages)/skill/detail/rules/page.tsx`
    - `opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/page.tsx`
- Reason:
  - these surfaces were repeating the same base toolbar contract:
    - right-aligned `Input.Search`
    - stable 240px-256px width
    - optional one-or-more trailing actions
    - identical bottom spacing and horizontal alignment
  - the differences were placeholder copy, search handler wiring, button content, and one custom search trigger icon
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `system-manager` originally carried a business wrapper, but its API was purely generic and contained no system-manager-specific behavior
  - `node-manager` uses the same layout with a slightly wider search input
  - `opspilot` reuses the same layout for settings, rules, and search-only knowledge browsing
  - page-local tables, permissions, routing, and modal behavior remain local and should stay local
- Decision:
  - promote the pattern to a framework component instead of preserving the old system-manager-only wrapper
  - remove the redundant business component to avoid duplicate abstractions with identical contracts
  - keep the framework API narrow: `searchProps`, optional `actions`, optional outer `className`, optional `searchClassName`
- Migration:
  - added `components/search-action-bar/index.tsx`
  - migrated the pages above plus `system-manager/(pages)/user/structure/page.tsx` to consume the shared framework toolbar
  - removed `components/system-manager-action-search-bar` and its story after promotion
- Storybook:
  - added `stories/search-action-bar.stories.tsx`
  - registered as `Framework/Toolbar/SearchActionBar`

- Candidate:

  - `loginLogs` uses the same end-aligned audit-toolbar layout with different inputs and an extra export action

  - `log` includes collector text in the title and a single category tag
  - tree filtering, routing, and fetch behavior remain page-local and should stay local

### System-manager role tab action-search-bar convergence
- Candidate:
  - repeated role-tab search/action toolbars in:
    - `system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/role/UserTab.tsx`
    - `system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/role/OrganizationTab.tsx`
- Reason:
  - both tabs were independently composing the same management-toolbar pattern:
    - right-aligned search box
    - primary add action
    - batch delete action
    - identical spacing and search width
  - the differences were only permission keys and click handlers
- Cross-app comparison:
  - both tabs live under the same role-management surface
  - both use the same search affordance and button arrangement
  - both differ only in business nouns: user vs organization
  - modal content and table columns remain domain-specific and should stay local
- Decision:
  - extract a system-manager business toolbar instead of duplicating the search/action wrapper
  - keep the API narrow: `onSearch`, `searchPlaceholder`, optional `actions`, optional `className`

### Routed top-section framework convergence
- Candidate:
  - repeated path-mapped top-section selection in:
    - `opspilot/(pages)/studio/detail/layout.tsx`
    - `opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/detail/layout.tsx`
    - `node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/mainlayout/layout.tsx`
- Reason:
  - after extracting `OpsPilotDetailTopSection`, the underlying pattern proved to be cross-app rather than OpsPilot-only:
    - resolve current pathname
    - match a route-specific title/description pair
    - render a shared `TopSection`
    - fall back to a default route description
  - node-manager cloudregion was still duplicating the same path-to-copy resolution inline
- Cross-app comparison:
  - OpsPilot studio and knowledge use exact detail-route matching for shell copy
  - node-manager cloudregion uses exact section-route matching for node/environment/variable views
  - all three share the same matching semantics and differ only in the route map and translated copy they pass in
- Decision:
  - promote the behavior to a framework component instead of leaving an OpsPilot-branded wrapper in place
  - keep route definitions and translation ownership inside each page so domain copy stays local
- Migration:
  - added `components/routed-top-section/index.tsx`
  - migrated OpsPilot studio, OpsPilot knowledge, and node-manager cloudregion to the shared framework component
  - removed the now-redundant `opspilot-detail-top-section` wrapper
- Storybook:
  - replaced the business-only story with `stories/routed-top-section.stories.tsx`
  - registered as `Framework/Layout/RoutedTopSection`

### OpsPilot routed detail top-section convergence

  - the repeated pattern was not page-specific rendering, but a stable OpsPilot detail behavior:

    - fall back to a default top-section state

  - both need exact-path matching with a default fallback
  - `studio` has four route variants
  - `knowledge` has three route variants and separate task-progress behavior that remains page-local

  - extract a business component for OpsPilot routed detail headers instead of keeping parallel `switch` blocks
  - keep translation lookup and route item definitions in each page so copy ownership stays local

  - updated studio/knowledge detail layouts to pass route definitions into the shared component
  - removed duplicated path-switching JSX from both pages

  - registered as `Business/OpsPilot/DetailTopSection`

    - `log/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/k8s/commonIssuesDrawer.tsx`

  - both app-local wrappers were manually constructing the same shared `K8sCommonIssuesDrawer` issue schema in-page
  - the wrappers still need to own business copy selection, but the preset structure itself belongs closer to the shared component contract than to each page
- Cross-app comparison:
  - both monitor and log wrappers render the same shared drawer component and differ only in:
    - localized title/labels
    - issue preset content
  - the repeated work was the `K8sCommonIssue[]` object assembly, not runtime drawer behavior
- Decision:
  - keep monitor/log wrappers as business entry points
  - move the preset-building logic into the shared component namespace so app wrappers no longer own duplicated schema assembly
- Migration:
  - added `web/src/components/k8s-common-issues-drawer/presets.tsx`
  - updated:
    - `web/src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/k8s/commonIssuesDrawer.tsx`
    - `web/src/app/log/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/k8s/commonIssuesDrawer.tsx`
  - this is a low-risk runtime convergence step: less duplication, same business behavior, same Storybook contract
- Storybook:
  - no title change in this slice
  - existing `Business/Integrations/K8s/CommonIssuesDrawer` story remains the contract surface for drawer behavior

### Integration import and batch-edit modals stay in business scope
- Candidate:
  - `IntegrationBatchEditModal` from `web/src/components/integration-batch-edit-modal/index.tsx`
  - `IntegrationExcelImportModal` from `web/src/components/integration-excel-import-modal/index.tsx`
  - `IntegrationInstanceEditModal` from `web/src/components/integration-instance-edit-modal/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - all three are genuinely shared, but each still bundles integration-specific business semantics rather than only modal infrastructure
  - `IntegrationBatchEditModal` owns column-driven batch field enablement, auth-type/password coupling, node/group semantics, and edited-field extraction rules
  - `IntegrationExcelImportModal` owns Excel parsing, import-column mapping, auth/private-key normalization, uniqueness checks, and group/node option resolution
  - `IntegrationInstanceEditModal` still expresses the specific workflow of editing integration instance identity/group and switching between single-item edit and batch group reassignment
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `IntegrationBatchEditModal` and `IntegrationExcelImportModal` are used by:
    - `monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/automatic.tsx`
    - `log/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/automatic.tsx`
    - `node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/node/controllerInstall/installConfig/index.tsx`
  - `IntegrationInstanceEditModal` is used by:
    - `monitor/(pages)/integration/asset/editInstance.tsx`
    - `log/(pages)/integration/receive/editInstance.tsx`
  - across usages, reuse is driven by a shared integration data model and workflow, not by a domain-agnostic overlay contract
- Decision:
  - keep all three in `Business/Integrations`
  - do not promote them to framework scope until the business-specific parsing, auth, batch-edit, and instance-management semantics have been factored into lower-level primitives
- Migration:
  - kept runtime ownership unchanged
  - aligned Storybook naming so this business-shared group reads consistently in the catalog
- Storybook:
  - retained in `Business/Integrations`
  - renamed for consistency:
    - `BatchEditModal` -> `IntegrationBatchEditModal`
    - `ExcelImportModal` -> `IntegrationExcelImportModal`
    - `IntegrationInstanceEditModal` -> `InstanceEditModal`

### Async config-edit modal shell reclassification
- Candidate:
  - `IntegrationConfigEditModal` from `web/src/components/integration-config-edit-modal/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - this component no longer contains integration-domain rendering logic
  - it now acts as a generic async modal shell:
    - open with source context
    - load remote config
    - derive default values
    - render injected form items
    - handle loading, empty state, validation, submit, and success/failed feedback
  - the integration-specific behavior lives entirely in the wrapper callbacks: `loadConfig`, `getFormItems`, `getDefaultValues`, and `submitConfig`
- Cross-app comparison:
  - used by:
    - `monitor/(pages)/integration/asset/updateConfig.tsx`
    - `log/(pages)/integration/receive/updateConfig.tsx`
  - the two wrappers load different config sources and build different forms, yet both rely on the same modal lifecycle contract
  - this is strong evidence that the reusable value is the shell, not any integration-specific field model
- Decision:
  - classify `IntegrationConfigEditModal` as a framework overlay/modal primitive
  - keep app-level `updateConfig.tsx` wrappers in business scope because they still own collector/plugin semantics and request payload shaping
- Migration:
  - updated `web/src/stories/integration-config-edit-modal.stories.tsx`
  - left runtime imports stable to avoid churn while moving Storybook ownership to the framework catalog
- Storybook:
  - moved:
    - `Business/Integrations/IntegrationConfigEditModal` -> `Framework/Overlays/AsyncConfigModal`

### Integration instance edit modal stays in business scope
- Candidate:
  - `IntegrationInstanceEditModal` from `web/src/components/integration-instance-edit-modal/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - although it is shared between `monitor` and `log`, the contract still encodes integration-instance semantics directly:
    - edit instance name
    - edit owning group
    - switch between single-item edit and batch group reassignment
  - that is more than a generic modal shell; the business workflow is still part of the component contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - used by:
    - `monitor/(pages)/integration/asset/editInstance.tsx`
    - `log/(pages)/integration/receive/editInstance.tsx`
  - both wrappers vary only by labels, source instance shape, and mutation handlers
  - reuse is real, but the shared meaning remains specifically about integration instances rather than a general-purpose editing primitive
- Decision:
  - keep `IntegrationInstanceEditModal` under `Business/Integrations`
  - do not flatten business semantics into framework scope just because the wrappers line up
- Migration:
  - no runtime change
  - no Storybook taxonomy change in this slice; the important action here is preserving the correct ownership boundary

### Automatic configuration workspace shell reclassification
- Candidate:
  - `IntegrationAutomaticConfigurationShell` from `web/src/components/integration-automatic-configuration-shell/index.tsx`

  - it owns generic structure only:

    - configuration section titles
    - slot-based form area
    - monitored-object table section with import and batch-action affordances
    - confirm action placement

  - all domain-specific meaning is supplied through props such as `formItems`, `tableNode`, labels, menu items, and confirm handlers

  - used by:
    - `monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/automatic.tsx`
    - `log/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/automatic.tsx`
  - both pages inject different data models, columns, modal flows, and labels while relying on the exact same workspace contract
  - the stable reusable value is the shell itself, not any particular monitored-object semantics
- Decision:
  - classify `IntegrationAutomaticConfigurationShell` as a framework layout primitive
  - keep the app-level automatic configuration pages in business scope because they still own plugin config loading, table behavior, batch operations, and submission logic
- Migration:
  - updated `web/src/stories/integration-automatic-configuration-shell.stories.tsx`
  - runtime imports remain unchanged to avoid unnecessary churn while governance ownership moves to the framework catalog
- Storybook:

    - `Business/Integrations/AutomaticConfigurationShell` -> `Framework/Layout/ConfigurationWorkspace`

  - `K8sAccessAssetFields` from `web/src/components/k8s-access-asset-fields/index.tsx`

  - the shared contract is stable across apps, but it remains specifically about k8s asset onboarding rather than general-purpose layout or feedback

- Candidate:

  - despite the file name, the component has become a generic completion-state surface: primary illustration, status icon, title, description, optional sub-description, and an action row
  - it contains no integration-domain state, no k8s-specific rendering, and no navigation semantics beyond the callbacks passed in through `actions`

  - differences are limited to:
    - page copy

    - icon token overrides
    - destination callbacks
  - the visual and interaction contract is otherwise identical across apps
- Decision:
  - classify `IntegrationAccessComplete` as a framework feedback/result primitive
  - keep the app-level `accessComplete.tsx` wrappers in business scope because they still own route decisions and domain copy

  - `IntegrationSettingRow` is a simple two-slot layout for `control + description`

### Event level display primitives reclassification
- Candidate:
  - `EventLevelTag` from `web/src/components/event-level-tag/index.tsx`
  - `EventLevelIndicator` from `web/src/components/event-level-indicator/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - both components now serve as shared semantic display atoms across multiple apps rather than event-page-private widgets
  - `EventLevelTag` is used by `alarm`, `monitor`, `log`, plus shared drawers and related-alert tables
  - `EventLevelIndicator` is shared by both `monitor` and `log` alert detail surfaces
  - each component only expresses severity presentation through `label`, `color`, and optional icon treatment; there is no workflow, fetching, or app-specific behavior inside either implementation
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `EventLevelTag` appears in:
    - `alarm/(pages)/incidents/*`
    - `alarm/(pages)/settings/alertAssign/components/operateModal.tsx`
    - `monitor/(pages)/event/alert/page.tsx`
    - `monitor/(pages)/view/monitorAlarm.tsx`
    - `log/(pages)/event/alert/page.tsx`
    - shared components such as `alarm-table`, `alarm-detail-drawer`, `related-alerts-panel`, and `event-detail-drawer/header.tsx`
  - `EventLevelIndicator` appears in both:
    - `monitor/(pages)/event/alert/information.tsx`
    - `log/(pages)/event/alert/information.tsx`
  - the stable shared contract is severity display, not a business event workflow
- Decision:
  - classify both as framework data-display primitives
  - keep `EventCloseAction`, `EventNotificationForm`, and `EventDetailDrawer` in business scope because they still encode event workflow semantics, permissions, or domain copy contracts
- Migration:
  - updated:
    - `web/src/stories/event-level-tag.stories.tsx`
    - `web/src/stories/event-level-indicator.stories.tsx`
  - no runtime component change was needed; the governance correction is Storybook taxonomy and design-system ownership
- Storybook:
  - moved:
    - `Business/Events/LevelTag` -> `Framework/DataDisplay/SeverityTag`
    - `Business/Events/LevelIndicator` -> `Framework/DataDisplay/SeverityIndicator`
  - Storybook naming is now semantic rather than import-path-driven, so the framework catalog speaks in design-system terms even while runtime file names stay stable for migration safety

### Event status semantic-tag reclassification
- Candidate:
  - `EventStatusTag` from `web/src/components/event-status-tag/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - this tag is already reused across both `monitor` and `log` event-alert flows
  - its contract is narrow and stable: a semantic on/off status pill driven by `label` and `active`
  - keeping it under `Business/Events` in Storybook understated its real role and made it look domain-private even though the implementation is already cross-app shared
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `monitor/(pages)/event/alert/page.tsx`
  - `monitor/(pages)/event/alert/alertDetail.tsx`
  - `monitor/(pages)/view/monitorAlarm.tsx`
  - `log/(pages)/event/alert/page.tsx`
  - `log/(pages)/event/alert/alertDetail.tsx`
  - all usages apply the same semantic pattern: render a compact status tag from a localized label plus active/inactive state
  - there is no event-domain-specific behavior, data fetching, or layout contract left in the component itself
- Decision:
  - classify `EventStatusTag` as a framework data-display primitive rather than a business event component
  - keep `EventDetailDrawer` in `Business/Events`, because that shell still carries event-detail workflow semantics and timeline/information tab structure
- Migration:
  - updated `web/src/stories/event-status-tag.stories.tsx`
  - no runtime code change was required because the implementation was already shared correctly; the governance fix here is the Storybook contract and taxonomy
- Storybook:
  - moved from `Business/Events/StatusTag`
  - to `Framework/DataDisplay/StatusTag`

### Monitor stacked-capacity panel Storybook formalization
- Candidate:
  - `StackedBarPanel` from `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/stacked-bar-panel.tsx`
- Reason:
  - this component is already promoted into the monitor shared widget barrel and is used by the `k8s-cluster` dashboard for resource-capacity breakdown
  - despite already being a shared business widget, it had no Storybook contract, which meant one of the monitor dashboard’s higher-signal comparison panels was still governed only by page usage
- Cross-app comparison:
  - current evidence shows it is a shared monitor business panel with stable semantics: stacked used/requested/free capacity rows, oversold highlighting, legend treatment, and optional guide-aware header
  - it is not yet a framework chart primitive because the contract is business-facing and capacity-specific
- Decision:
  - keep `StackedBarPanel` as `Business/Monitor`
  - add Storybook coverage as its explicit contract before broader reuse expands
- Migration:
  - added `web/src/stories/monitor-stacked-bar-panel.stories.tsx`
  - documented normal capacity and oversold states without changing runtime behavior
- Storybook:
  - `Business/Monitor/StackedBarPanel`
  - covered balanced capacity and oversold warning states

### Ops-analysis config panel formalization
- Candidate:
  - `SingleValueSettingsSection`
  - `GaugeSettingsSection`
- Reason:
  - after converging metric-field selection, value-format editing, threshold colors, and value mappings, both composed panels now read as stable business configuration surfaces rather than ad hoc assembly points
  - the remaining differences are intentional widget semantics, not accidental UI drift:
    - single value adds compare-period behavior
    - gauge adds min/max and shape controls
- Cross-app comparison:
  - within ops-analysis, `SingleValueSettingsSection` already spans dashboard and topology flows, making it a higher-level business contract than the lower-level config atoms
  - `GaugeSettingsSection` is the stable dashboard-side gauge configuration shell built on the same shared atoms
  - no evidence yet supports promoting either panel to framework scope, because both still encode widget-specific business meaning
- Decision:
  - keep both panels in `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config`
  - document them in Storybook as composed panel contracts so future UX iteration can be governed at panel level, not only at atom level
- Migration:
  - added:
    - `web/src/stories/ops-analysis-single-value-settings-panel.stories.tsx`
    - `web/src/stories/ops-analysis-gauge-settings-panel.stories.tsx`
  - panel stories now compose the shared config atoms and expose the main state differences directly
- Storybook:
  - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/SingleValueSettings`
  - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/GaugeSettings`
  - covered compare-unavailable, readonly, semicircle, and circle gauge states

### Ops-analysis value-format config convergence
- Candidate:
  - the duplicated unit and number-format configuration block inside `SingleValueSettingsSection` and `GaugeSettingsSection`
- Reason:
  - both panels carried the same semantic editor for value formatting: structured unit selection, custom suffix fallback, conversion factor, and decimal precision
  - this is a real business contract, not incidental spacing, and it already influences rendering behavior across single value, gauge, and bar-gauge widgets
- Cross-app comparison:
  - today the exact editing pattern is repeated across two separate ops-analysis config panels
  - the model is still tied to ops-analysis numeric widget semantics (`unitId`, `unit`, `conversionFactor`, `decimalPlaces`), so it is better treated as a shared business config component than a framework primitive
- Decision:
  - extract the value-format block into a shared ops-analysis config component
  - keep the surrounding panels composed from this block plus their widget-specific controls

  - both panels now share one value-format editing contract
- Storybook:
  - added `web/src/stories/ops-analysis-value-format-config.stories.tsx`
  - covered structured-unit, custom-suffix, wider gauge-layout, and readonly states

### Ops-analysis metric field-selector convergence

  - the duplicated display-field selector block inside `SingleValueSettingsSection` and `GaugeSettingsSection`

  - leaving that block duplicated meant dashboard single-value and gauge configuration could drift independently even though they already represent the same underlying metric-selection contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - this pattern is reused across two separate ops-analysis configuration surfaces today
  - the prop model is still business-specific, because it depends on ops-analysis field trees, data-source fetching, and selected widget field semantics
  - that makes it a business shared component, not a framework primitive
- Decision:
  - extract the repeated selector block into a shared ops-analysis config component
  - keep the larger `SingleValueSettingsSection` and `GaugeSettingsSection` as composed business panels

  - both panels now compose the same field-selector contract instead of owning divergent local copies

  - covered ready, loading, no-data-source, and readonly states

### Ops-analysis config-rule editor formalization
- Candidate:
  - `ValueMappingsConfigSection`
  - `ThresholdColorConfigSection`
- Reason:
  - both components already sit at the center of ops-analysis numeric configuration flows and are reused across dashboard view configuration and topology node configuration
  - they define stable editing semantics rather than page-specific layout: rule typing, match condition editing, result text/color mapping, threshold insertion/removal, and immutable base-threshold behavior
  - before this slice, their contract was only implied by form integration code and a local showcase fragment
- Cross-app comparison:
  - inside ops-analysis, both components are shared by multiple parent editors, including gauge/single-value dashboard configuration and topology single-value node configuration
  - outside ops-analysis, there is not yet strong evidence of the same prop contract or editing semantics elsewhere in the repo, so extracting them to framework level would be premature
  - the right convergence step today is to govern them as business configuration primitives in Storybook
- Decision:
  - keep both components in `Business/OpsAnalysis`, not `Framework`
  - treat them as reusable config-rule editors with their own Storybook contract
- Migration:

    - `web/src/stories/ops-analysis-value-mappings-config.stories.tsx`
    - `web/src/stories/ops-analysis-threshold-color-config.stories.tsx`

- Storybook:
  - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/ValueMappings`
  - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/ThresholdColors`

### Ops-analysis metric widget contract formalization
- Candidate:
  - `ComSingle`

  - `ComBarGauge`

  - their behavior is not incidental page markup; it is defined by repeatable semantic dimensions such as unit scaling, threshold coloring, compare state, gauge shape, and value mapping
  - before this slice, that contract still lived mainly inside the local widget showcase instead of the global Storybook taxonomy
- Cross-app comparison:
  - across `ops-analysis`, all three are first-class registry-backed widget types, so they already act like governed business components
  - there is no safe evidence yet that they should be collapsed into framework components, because their prop model is still tightly coupled to ops-analysis dashboard semantics (`selectedFields`, `compare`, `gaugeShape`, dashboard value mappings)
  - the shared layer here is the Storybook contract, not a new framework abstraction
- Decision:
  - keep `Single`, `Gauge`, and `BarGauge` as `Business/OpsAnalysis` components
  - formalize their real state space in Storybook rather than leaving them governed only by the catch-all local showcase

    - `web/src/stories/ops-analysis-gauge.stories.tsx`

  - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Gauge`
  - `Business/OpsAnalysis/BarGauge`

### Ops-analysis widget contract formalization and markdown-shell convergence
- Candidate:
  - `ComTopN`, `ComStateTimeline`, and `ComText` under `web/src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/dashBoard/widgets/`
  - the duplicated markdown parsing logic embedded inside `ComText`
- Reason:
  - `ops-analysis` no longer has duplicated business wrappers in its widget registry, but several mature widget contracts still only lived inside the local `widgetShowcase.stories.tsx` demo or had no global Storybook entry at all
  - `ComText` also carried a second markdown-rendering path even though the repo already has a governed framework component, `MarkdownRenderer`, with its own Storybook contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `ComTopN`, `ComStateTimeline`, and `ComText` are first-class business widgets in `web/src/app/ops-analysis/components/widgetRegistry.ts`, not one-off page fragments
  - `ComText` overlapped directly with `web/src/components/markdown/index.tsx`, which already serves markdown rendering in job, log, cmdb, and opspilot surfaces
  - this means the stable split is now clear:
    - framework layer owns markdown parsing and sanitization
    - business layer owns the ops-analysis text-panel shell and widget-specific empty state
- Decision:
  - Promote `TopN`, `StateTimeline`, and `TextPanel` into global Storybook business categories
  - Remove the duplicated markdown parsing logic from `ComText` and converge it onto `MarkdownRenderer`
  - Keep these as business widgets rather than over-extracting them into framework components, because their semantics are still dashboard-widget specific
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/dashBoard/widgets/comText.tsx` to render through `MarkdownRenderer`
  - Added global Storybook entries:
    - `web/src/stories/ops-analysis-top-n.stories.tsx`
    - `web/src/stories/ops-analysis-state-timeline.stories.tsx`
    - `web/src/stories/ops-analysis-text-panel.stories.tsx`
- Storybook:
  - `Business/OpsAnalysis/TopN`
  - `Business/OpsAnalysis/StateTimeline`
  - `Business/OpsAnalysis/TextPanel`
  - These now replace the implicit reliance on the catch-all local showcase for the governed contracts covered in this slice

  - `flowScatter` resolved to `ComScatter`
  - `flowTable` resolved to `ComTable`

  - Remove this final pure-wrapper tail and point the affected widget keys directly at the shared base widgets
  - Document the previously untracked shared contracts for `ComPie` and `ComScatter` in Storybook

  - Added `web/src/stories/log-analysis-scatter.stories.tsx`
  - Covered compact pie, pie-to-bar fallback, and labeled scatter states

### Log base chart-shell convergence and Storybook formalization
- Candidate: the remaining pure wrapper chart entries around `ComBar`, `ComBarLine`, `ComLine`, and `ComSankey` inside `web/src/app/log/(pages)/analysis/dashBoard/widgets/*`
- Reason: after KPI/donut/table cleanup, several log widget entry files still only forwarded props into the same shared chart shells. That kept repeated entry components in business directories even though the actual reusable contract already lived in the base chart widgets
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `httpBarLine`, `kafkaBarLine`, and `mongodbBarLine` all resolved to the same `ComBarLine`
  - `kafkaBar`, `mongodbBar`, `rabbitmqBar`, and `windowsEventBar` all resolved to `ComBar`
  - `flowTrend` resolved to `ComLine`
  - `flowSankey` resolved to `ComSankey`
  - business-specific wrappers that still layer real behavior, such as `flowBar` and `mysqlDualLine`, were intentionally left untouched
- Decision:
  - Remove the pure passthrough business-local chart wrappers
  - Keep widget-type keys stable, but map them directly to the shared base chart shells
  - Bring the base chart shells into Storybook so shared chart behavior is governed explicitly instead of being inferred from widget directories
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/app/log/(pages)/analysis/dashBoard/components/widgetWrapper.tsx` so repeated chart widget keys now point directly to `ComBar`, `ComBarLine`, `ComLine`, and `ComSankey`
  - Removed duplicated passthrough wrapper files from the affected widget directories
  - Updated affected widget `index.ts` barrels to stop exporting deleted wrapper files
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/log-analysis-bar.stories.tsx`
  - Added `web/src/stories/log-analysis-bar-line.stories.tsx`
  - Added `web/src/stories/log-analysis-line.stories.tsx`
  - Added `web/src/stories/log-analysis-sankey.stories.tsx`

### Log analysis table-shell convergence and Storybook formalization
- Candidate: the log analysis dashboard table entry wrappers around `web/src/app/log/(pages)/analysis/dashBoard/widgets/comTable.tsx`
- Reason: multiple log business widgets still carried local `*Table.tsx` files that only returned the same shared log analysis table shell. That duplicated entry components across widget directories and kept the real table-shell contract undocumented in Storybook
- Cross-app comparison:
  - Apache, Elasticsearch, File Integrity, Kafka, MongoDB, Nginx, PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, Syslog, and Windows Event table entries all resolved to the same `ComTable`
  - the actual shared shell is `ComTable`, which standardizes row indexing, scroll-height behavior, and `CustomTable` integration
  - business-specific divergence remains only where data normalization is real, such as `httpRequestTable` and `dockerLogTail`, so those wrappers were intentionally kept
- Decision:
  - Remove the pure passthrough business-local table wrappers
  - Keep widget-type keys stable, but map the repeated ones directly to `ComTable`
  - Treat `ComTable` as the Storybook-documented table shell for these log analysis widgets
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/app/log/(pages)/analysis/dashBoard/components/widgetWrapper.tsx` so repeated table widget keys now point directly to `ComTable`
  - Removed duplicated passthrough table files from affected widget directories
  - Updated affected widget `index.ts` barrels to stop exporting deleted passthrough files
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/log-analysis-table.stories.tsx`
  - Covered indexed and non-indexed table-shell states so Storybook now documents the shared log analysis table contract directly

### Log donut widget-entry convergence onto shared `LogDonutChart`
- Candidate: the log analysis dashboard donut/pie entry components under `web/src/app/log/(pages)/analysis/dashBoard/widgets/*/*Pie.tsx` and `*Donut.tsx`
- Reason: a broad set of log pie widget files had become pure passthrough shells to the shared `LogDonutChart`, duplicating component entrypoints across business directories without adding any distinct rendering or normalization behavior. That leaves the real contract in the shared component while business folders keep stale, unnecessary wrappers
- Cross-app comparison:
  - Apache, Elasticsearch, Flow, HTTP basic donut, Kafka, MongoDB, Nginx, PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, Syslog, and Windows Event donut/pie entries all resolved to identical `LogDonutChart` behavior
  - a smaller subset still has real business divergence and was intentionally kept local, including `mysqlDonut`, `redisDonut`, `dockerSeverityDonut`, and `httpStatusCategoryDonut`, because they normalize incoming raw data before rendering the shared donut shell
  - the actual shared UI contract already lives in `web/src/components/log-donut-chart/index.tsx` and is already represented in Storybook
- Decision:
  - Remove the pure passthrough business-local donut wrappers
  - Keep widget-type registry keys stable, but map the repeated ones directly to `LogDonutChart`
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/app/log/(pages)/analysis/dashBoard/components/widgetWrapper.tsx` so repeated donut widget keys now point directly to `LogDonutChart`
  - Removed duplicated passthrough donut/pie files from affected widget directories
  - Updated affected widget `index.ts` barrels to stop exporting deleted passthrough files
- Storybook:
  - No new story file was needed because the shared behavior already belongs to `web/src/stories/log-donut-chart.stories.tsx`
  - This convergence sharpens the rule that donut widget changes must be expressed through the shared Storybook contract, not through business-local passthrough wrappers

### Log KPI widget-entry convergence onto shared `LogKpiCard`

These files duplicated component surface area across many business directories without adding semantic value, which weakens ownership boundaries and makes Storybook less trustworthy as the single source of truth

  - the actual shared contract already lives in `web/src/components/log-kpi-card/index.tsx` and is already documented in Storybook
  - only a smaller subset of KPI entries still carry meaningful business divergence today, such as `httpKpiCard`, `syslogKpiCard`, and `postgresqlKpiCard`, which keep custom metric calculation logic

  - Remove the pure passthrough business-local KPI components instead of preserving duplicate wrapper files

  - Removed the duplicated passthrough KPI files from the affected widget directories
  - Updated the affected widget `index.ts` barrels to stop exporting deleted passthrough files

### Monitor detail-card extraction and Storybook formalization
- Candidate: `DetailMetricRow` and `DetailPanelCard`, previously buried inside `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/objects/common/dashboard-components.tsx`
- Reason: these two monitor detail-shell pieces were already functioning as shared business components, but they were hidden inside an object-dashboard helper file rather than exposed as first-class shared widgets. That left one of the most reused detail-view contracts outside Storybook and easy to drift while editing object dashboards
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `DetailPanelCard` is reused across Apache, Ping, Tomcat, Docker, RabbitMQ, Zookeeper, K8s node, and K8s pod dashboards
  - `DetailMetricRow` is reused both by `DetailPanelCard` and by MongoDB’s custom detail panels
  - the stable shared layer is the detail contract itself: title/subtitle shell, empty-state behavior, right-aligned value column, sparkline/progress/status visualization slot, and optional guide tooltip on row labels
  - dashboard-specific metric selection, wording, and prepared row data remain page-owned
- Decision:
  - Promote both into monitor shared widgets instead of leaving them as implicit helpers inside the common dashboard orchestration file
  - Keep the existing object-dashboard wrapper exports in place so migration stays incremental and low-risk

    - `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/detail-metric-row.tsx`
    - `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/detail-panel-card.tsx`
  - Updated common dashboard helpers and MongoDB custom detail panels to source the shared row/panel contract from the shared widgets layer

  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-detail-panel-card.stories.tsx`
  - Covered sparkline, progress, status-dot, mixed-row, and empty-detail states

### Monitor collection-status contract expansion and Elasticsearch health-card convergence
- Candidate: `CollectionStatusCard` in `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/collection-status-card.tsx` and the Elasticsearch-local `cluster-health-card.tsx`
- Reason: Elasticsearch still carried a page-local health status card whose structure was effectively the same as the shared monitor status card: title-with-guide header, prominent semantic value, segmented timeline, and legend. The real difference was only semantic configuration: warning tone support, custom title/guide copy, custom legend labels, and empty-timeline messaging
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor common dashboards, MySQL, MongoDB, and K8s cluster already reuse `CollectionStatusCard` for collection health
  - Elasticsearch used a parallel local implementation with the same shell but a different status taxonomy (`正常 / 警告 / 严重`) and empty-state copy
  - the stable shared layer is the status-shell contract itself; the variable layer is title, tone mapping, guide copy, legend labels, and timeline-empty presentation
- Decision:
  - Expand the existing shared business component instead of keeping a second status-card implementation
  - Add warning-tone support and configurable status semantics so the same business shell can cover both collection-health and cluster-health variants
- Migration:
  - Extended `CollectionStatusCard` with configurable `title`, `statusTone`, `legendItems`, and `emptyTimelineText`
  - Removed `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/objects/elasticsearch/cluster-health-card.tsx`
  - Migrated Elasticsearch dashboard health overview to the shared `CollectionStatusCard`
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/monitor-collection-status-card.stories.tsx`
  - Added cluster-health and empty-timeline variants so Storybook now documents both the original collection-status grammar and the expanded cluster-health semantic mode

### Monitor KPI/instance selector Storybook formalization
- Candidate: `StatCard` and `InstanceSelector` from `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/*`
- Reason: these are two of the most reused monitor dashboard primitives that still lacked standalone Storybook contracts. `StatCard` is the KPI shell behind monitor value tiles with compare/trend/footer patterns, while `InstanceSelector` is the shared instance-switching entry reused by dashboard headers and instance identity cards. Leaving both undocumented kept dashboard-level style decisions scattered across object pages instead of anchored in one shared contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - within monitor, `StatCard` is reused by the common simple-dashboard shell and multiple object dashboards such as MySQL, MongoDB, RabbitMQ, and K8s-oriented views
  - `InstanceSelector` is reused by shared header widgets and object dashboard entry shells where users switch monitored instances without leaving the page
  - the stable shared layer is already clear: `StatCard` owns title/icon/value/unit/compare/footer/extra/trend presentation, and `InstanceSelector` owns searchable inline selection, optional label, loading state, and compact toolbar placement
  - object-specific metric semantics, icons, trend series, selector options, and callback wiring remain page-owned
- Decision:
  - Keep both as business shared monitor widgets
  - Formalize their state space in Storybook rather than creating more page-local wrappers or duplicating selector/KPI markup in object dashboards
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required in this slice
  - This Storybook contract now becomes the reference point for the next round of monitor KPI and toolbar style convergence across object dashboards
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-stat-card.stories.tsx`
  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-instance-selector.stories.tsx`
  - Covered compare-improving, compare-regressing, no-trend, and empty-trend KPI states plus default, loading, and label-less selector states

It is already one of the most broadly reused monitor dashboard widgets, but it had no Storybook contract despite being central to the dashboard visual system

  - the stable shared layer is the trend-panel shell itself: header + legend grammar, title-with-guide, subtitle, top/bottom extension slots, and interactive range-selection wiring
  - dashboard-specific metric queries, copy, and series data remain product/page-owned

### Monitor distribution-panel Storybook formalization
- Candidate: `RingChartPanel` and `HorizontalBarPanel` from `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/*`
- Reason: these two panels are the shared distribution/ranking primitives across a wide set of monitor object dashboards, covering ring-based composition views and horizontal ranking/proportion views. They already encode stable interaction and layout rules, but without Storybook their expected states remained implicit in individual dashboards
- Cross-app comparison:
  - within monitor, `RingChartPanel` is reused by host, website, ping, rabbitmq, nginx, mongodb, mysql, and k8s dashboards
  - `HorizontalBarPanel` is reused by k8s node/cluster, host, docker, elasticsearch, mysql, nginx, redis, website, zookeeper, and others
  - the stable shared layer is the panel contract itself: guide-aware title row, empty state handling, center/value information layout for ring panels, and ranked-vs-distribution-vs-trend row behavior for horizontal bar panels
- Decision:
  - Keep both as business shared monitor widgets
  - Capture their shared state space in Storybook instead of leaving ranking and distribution presentation rules embedded in dashboard pages
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required in this slice
  - Verified existing monitor dashboards are already converged on these shared panel primitives
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-ring-chart-panel.stories.tsx`
  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-horizontal-bar-panel.stories.tsx`
  - Covered normal, custom-info, and empty ring-panel states plus distribution, ranked-tiered, and sparkline-row bar-panel states

### Monitor dashboard panel-shell Storybook formalization
- Candidate: `DashboardPanel` and `DetailPanel` from `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/*`
- Reason: these are the shared content shells behind monitor dashboard sections and detail blocks, and they already centralize title styling, optional guide tooltip behavior, subtitle treatment, and inner body layout. Despite that, they had no Storybook coverage, which meant dashboard section chrome still depended on callers reading existing pages rather than a documented contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - within monitor, `DashboardPanel` appears across object dashboards such as MySQL and MongoDB for grouped chart/data sections
  - `DetailPanel` is reused for metric-detail blocks where structured rows need a stable title and body scaffold
  - the stable shared layer is the panel shell itself: guide-aware title row, optional subtitle, outer container semantics, and optional body wrapper
- Decision:
  - Keep both as business shared monitor widgets
  - Put the shared shell states into Storybook instead of creating more local wrappers around each dashboard section
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required in this slice
  - Verified monitor dashboards are already converged on these shared shells rather than duplicating panel markup
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-dashboard-panel.stories.tsx`
  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-detail-panel.stories.tsx`
  - Covered guide/no-guide, subtitle/no-subtitle, and wrapped-body states so Storybook now captures the shared section-shell behavior directly

### Monitor dashboard header-shell Storybook formalization
- Candidate: `DashboardPageHeader` and `DashboardInstanceCard` from `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/*`
- Reason: these two widgets define the primary interaction shell at the top of monitor dashboards: mode switching, time-range controls, refresh entry points, instance identity display, and instance switching. They are already stable shared building blocks, but without Storybook they remained easy to drift while editing individual dashboards
- Cross-app comparison:
  - within monitor, the same header/instance-card pattern appears across K8s cluster dashboards and the common object dashboard framework
  - the stable shared layer is the header interaction grammar itself: title, dashboard-vs-metrics mode toggle, optional time selector, back action, instance selector, and metadata display
  - object-specific titles, meta chips, selector options, and callback wiring remain product/page-owned
- Decision:
  - Keep both as business shared monitor widgets
  - Document the shared top-shell contract in Storybook instead of leaving it implicit inside full dashboard pages
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required in this slice
  - Confirmed current monitor dashboards are already reusing these widgets rather than duplicating top-shell markup
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-dashboard-page-header.stories.tsx`
  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-dashboard-instance-card.stories.tsx`
  - Covered dashboard mode, metrics mode without inline time selector, and absolute-range toolbar variants so Storybook now captures the shared monitor header/instance-shell states

### Monitor collection-status card Storybook formalization
- Candidate: `CollectionStatusCard` from `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/collection-status-card.tsx`
- Reason: this card is the lead KPI/status block across monitor’s shared simple-dashboard shell and multiple object dashboards, but it had no Storybook contract despite already encoding the shared visual grammar for health label, timeline segments, legend, and title help
- Cross-app comparison:
  - within monitor, the same card is reused by the common dashboard shell plus object dashboards such as MySQL, MongoDB, and K8s cluster
  - the stable shared layer is the status-card presentation itself: title with guide, tri-state label styling, condensed timeline, and legend
  - business-specific status copy and computed timelines stay in each dashboard data-preparation layer
- Decision:
  - Keep it as a business shared component under monitor dashboard infrastructure
  - Add Storybook coverage so future dashboard work reuses the same status-card contract instead of drifting visually or semantically
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required in this slice
  - Verified existing monitor dashboards are already converged on the same shared card
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/monitor-collection-status-card.stories.tsx`
  - Covered healthy, degraded, and no-data states so Storybook now documents the main monitor status-card state space directly

### Monitor dashboard guide-title Storybook formalization
- Candidate: `TitleWithGuide` from `web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/guide-tooltip.tsx`
- Reason: this title-plus-tooltip widget is already the shared semantic header pattern across monitor object dashboards, KPI cards, chart panels, and detail rows, but it had no Storybook coverage at all. That left one of the most reused dashboard-explanation patterns undocumented even though it directly affects information scent and cross-dashboard visual consistency
- Cross-app comparison:
  - within monitor, the same widget is reused across the common simple-dashboard shell and multiple object dashboards such as MySQL and MongoDB
  - the stable shared layer is the guide-title interaction itself: title text, optional info icon, and tooltip content rows
  - product-specific guide copy stays in each dashboard config; the component contract is the presentation and interaction shell
- Decision:
  - Keep it as a business shared component under monitor dashboard infrastructure
  - Formalize the contract in Storybook instead of cloning ad hoc title+tooltip markup in more dashboards

  - No runtime caller changes were needed in this slice
  - Confirmed current dashboard pages are already converged on the same shared widget

  - Covered guide-present, guide-absent, and tooltip-content states so Storybook now documents the monitor dashboard title-help pattern directly
