### Automatic integration table-workspace extraction
- Candidate: `monitor/(...)/configure/automatic.tsx` and `log/(...)/configure/automatic.tsx`
- Reason: monitor and log both maintained a large automatic-integration workspace that mixed product-specific data preparation with the same UI shell: configuration form header, monitored-object table section, import button, batch-operation dropdown, table validation field, permission-gated confirm action, and shared batch-edit / Excel-import modals
- Cross-app comparison:
  - both pages fetch node options, build a row-editable `CustomTable`, support batch edit, support Excel import, track selected rows, validate the table through a hidden form field, and submit through a permission-gated confirm button
  - the real differences are in config generation and persistence: monitor derives plugin UI from JSON config and submits `updateNodeChildConfig`, while log derives columns from collect-type hooks and submits `batchCreateInstances`
  - the stable shared layer is the automatic-integration editing workspace itself, not the product-specific row schema or payload mapping
- Decision:
  - Extract a shared business shell for the automatic integration workspace instead of duplicating the same layout and action chrome in both pages
  - Keep config assembly, table validation details, and submit payload generation inside each product page so the abstraction stops at the proven shared UI boundary
- Migration:
  - Added `web/src/components/integration-automatic-configuration-shell/index.tsx`
  - Migrated monitor and log automatic configuration pages to the new shared shell
  - Preserved each page’s own node-fetching logic, table columns, validation rules, and submit handlers
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/integration-automatic-configuration-shell.stories.tsx`
  - Covered the normal workspace, empty state, and modal-slot composition so Storybook now acts as the contract source for the shared automatic-integration editor shell

### CMDB asset-data layout rename and boundary clarification
- Candidate: shared `web/src/components/layout/sub-layout/index.tsx` versus app-local `cmdb/(pages)/assetData/components/sub-layout`
- Reason: the repository still had two different layout components both called `sub-layout`. That created the same governance problem we already saw with `EntityList`: one is the shared navigation/layout shell used across multiple apps, while the CMDB asset-data variant is a business adapter that derives a filtered menu set and plugs in a custom side menu
- Cross-app comparison:
  - the shared `layout/sub-layout` is a framework-level shell already used by `monitor`, `log`, `system-manager`, `node-manager`, `mlops`, `opspilot`, and CMDB
  - the CMDB asset-data local layer depends on asset-data-specific menu filtering and relationship-aware side-menu rendering
  - the real duplication was semantic naming, not component responsibility: the local component is an app-specific adapter on top of the shared shell, not another reusable layout primitive
- Decision:
  - Do not absorb the asset-data adapter into the shared layout API yet; its menu derivation and relation-aware side menu are still CMDB-specific
  - Rename the local component and its props so shared and business layers are unambiguous in both imports and code review
- Migration:
  - Moved `web/src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/components/sub-layout/*` to `web/src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/components/asset-data-layout/*`
  - Renamed the component to `AssetDataLayout`
  - Renamed the local props type to `AssetDataLayoutProps`
  - Updated the asset-data detail layout to import the renamed business adapter
- Storybook:
  - No Storybook entry added in this slice because the renamed component remains app-local and business-owned
  - The shared `layout/sub-layout` remains the Storybook-tracked contract for reusable navigation shells

### K8s guided wizard shell convergence
- Candidate: `monitor/(...)/configure/k8s/k8sConfiguration.tsx`, `log/(...)/configure/k8s/k8sConfiguration.tsx`, and `cmdb/(...)/components/k8s/k8sGuidedTask.tsx`
- Reason: after aligning the K8s access fields, install step, and completion surface, the remaining cross-app drift sat at the wizard-shell layer. Monitor and log were already on shared `StepWizardFlow`, while CMDB still maintained its own local `Steps` container and manual step switching for an otherwise equivalent three-step K8s onboarding flow
- Cross-app comparison:
  - all three flows are the same business sequence: access config -> collector install -> access complete
  - monitor and log persist step state through shared `StepWizardFlow`
  - CMDB differed mainly in how step one handed saved values into step two, not in the shell pattern itself
  - the stable shared layer is the step container, progress indicator, reset/back flow, and cross-step state handoff; step content remains product-owned
- Decision:
  - Reuse shared `StepWizardFlow` instead of keeping a CMDB-only step shell
  - Keep each product’s step content local, but converge the outer wizard pattern so the K8s onboarding family shares one navigational contract
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/k8s/k8sGuidedTask.tsx`
  - Replaced manual `Steps` state management with shared `StepWizardFlow`
  - Preserved CMDB’s `K8sTaskForm` save-and-handoff behavior through `onAfterSave`, and kept the existing collector/install completion steps intact
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/step-wizard-flow.stories.tsx`
  - Added a guided-setup story that demonstrates external form handoff into later wizard steps, so Storybook now documents the exact flow pattern used by the K8s onboarding pages

### OpsPilot entity list rename and boundary clarification
- Candidate: shared `web/src/components/entity-list/index.tsx` versus app-local `opspilot/components/entity-list`
- Reason: the repository still had two different components named `EntityList`. That violated the governance goal even though the underlying intent was different: the shared component is a reusable entity-card gallery shell, while the OpsPilot local component is a business container with built-in fetching, infinite scroll, mutation flows, pin behavior, and modal orchestration
- Cross-app comparison:
  - the shared `entity-list` is already used by `mlops`, `node-manager`, `system-manager`, and parts of `opspilot` as a presentation-first reusable shell
  - the OpsPilot local implementation owns endpoint wiring, pagination, mutation side effects, and product-specific create flows; those are not stable cross-app props yet
  - the real issue was not “missing abstraction”, but a misleading duplicate name that made the component system harder to govern
- Decision:
  - Do not force the OpsPilot container into the shared `entity-list` API yet; that would over-abstract app-specific data and mutation behavior
  - Rename the OpsPilot local component to a business-specific name so shared and business layers are clearly separated
- Migration:
  - Moved `web/src/app/opspilot/components/entity-list/index.tsx` to `web/src/app/opspilot/components/entity-management-grid/index.tsx`
  - Updated the OpsPilot skill and studio pages to import the renamed business component
- Storybook:
  - No Storybook entry added in this slice because the renamed component remains app-local and business-owned
  - The shared `entity-list` continues to be the Storybook-tracked reusable contract

### K8s collector install step extraction
- Candidate: `monitor/(...)/configure/k8s/collectorInstall.tsx`, `log/(...)/configure/k8s/collectorInstall.tsx`, and `cmdb/(...)/components/k8s/collectorInstall.tsx`
- Reason: after the K8s access-asset forms were aligned, the next repeated business shell was the collector-install verification step. Monitor, log, and CMDB were all wiring the same shared `K8sCollectorInstall` presentation with overlapping local state for verification progress, success/failure transitions, delayed completion, and install-command handling
- Cross-app comparison:
  - all three steps render the same install-command surface and verification status workflow
  - monitor and log start from a ready-made install command, while CMDB first generates the command and then enters the same verification loop
  - the real differences are limited to product copy, success delay, verify API function, and whether install actions/content are injected
  - product-specific common-issues content remains local through each page’s own `CommonIssuesDrawer`, while CMDB simply omits that branch
- Decision:
  - Extract the shared verification step into a business component instead of leaving the state machine duplicated
  - Extend the business component boundary to support both ready-made and generated install-command flows by injecting install actions/content and letting the shared step own the verification state machine
  - Keep verify API calls and issue-drawer content injected from the product pages so the abstraction stops at the stable shared workflow boundary
- Migration:
  - Added `web/src/components/k8s-collector-install-step/index.tsx`
  - Migrated monitor, log, and CMDB collector-install pages to the new shared business step
  - Preserved local API integrations, localized copy, success timing, issue drawers, and CMDB’s command-generation actions
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/k8s-collector-install-step.stories.tsx`
  - Covered the waiting/failure path, a success path, and a generated-command variant so Storybook now documents the shared install-verification workflow instead of only the lower-level presentational shell

### K8s access asset field extraction
- Candidate: `monitor/(...)/configure/k8s/accessConfig.tsx` and `log/(...)/configure/k8s/accessConfig.tsx`
- Reason: monitor and log each maintained their own K8s access-configuration skeleton for choosing new-vs-existing asset, collecting organization / existing cluster input, and choosing cloud region. The post-selection fields diverge by product, but this first asset-access segment was already structurally identical and sat directly on shared form primitives (`IntegrationSettingRow`, `GroupTreeSelect`)
- Cross-app comparison:
  - both flows render the same access-type radio group
  - both branch between “new cluster name + organizations” and “existing cluster select”
  - both end the shared segment with cloud-region selection
  - monitor continues into upload interval configuration, while log continues into runtime profile and path configuration; those remain app-owned
- Decision:
  - Extract the common first-step asset-access section into a shared business component
  - Keep the later monitor/log-specific fields local so the abstraction stops at the real shared boundary instead of absorbing unrelated product logic
- Migration:
  - Added `web/src/components/k8s-access-asset-fields/index.tsx`
  - Migrated both monitor and log K8s access-config pages to the new shared business component for the access-asset / cluster / organization / cloud-region section
  - Left product-specific trailing fields in place to preserve behavior and avoid over-abstracting
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/k8s-access-asset-fields.stories.tsx`
  - Covered both new-asset and existing-asset flows so Storybook now acts as the contract source for this shared business section, not just the underlying field primitives

### Chart series state Storybook formalization
- Candidate: `chart-series-state`
- Reason: `useChartSeriesState` is the shared chart-state primitive behind monitor and MLOps dimension-aware charts, but it had no Storybook presence at all despite already governing series-key detection, color allocation, dimension-detail detection, and legend visibility toggling for multiple shared chart wrappers
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor line and bar charts use the hook to derive `chartKeys`, `colors`, dimension details, and visible-series toggling
  - MLOps line charts rely on the same state primitive for dimension-aware tooltip and legend coordination
  - the stable shared layer is the series-state contract itself, not the app-local chart wrappers that render thresholds, annotations, or event overlays
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Charts`
  - Do not add another higher-level wrapper just because monitor and MLOps charts share this hook; their runtime behaviors still diverge meaningfully
  - Put the shared state primitive itself into Storybook so its contract becomes explicit and testable
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller edits were required
  - Verified that monitor and MLOps are already converged on the same shared series-state logic rather than duplicating it app-locally
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/chart-series-state.stories.tsx`
  - Documented dimension-aware series detection, no-details behavior, and custom key matching so Storybook now covers the shared chart-state rules that power multiple chart families

### Custom table drag contract fix and story expansion
- Candidate: `custom-table`
- Reason: `CustomTable` is one of the most reused shared data-display primitives in `web/`, spanning `cmdb`, `monitor`, `log`, `system-manager`, `alarm`, `job`, `node-manager`, `ops-analysis`, `opspilot`, and `mlops`. Production usage already relies on row dragging, selectable pagination summaries, and field-setting overlays, but Storybook only covered a plain table and a basic action-column case. While auditing those real runtime modes, a shared bug surfaced: the row-drag callback arguments were emitted in the opposite order from the documented contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor, CMDB, and ops-analysis configuration flows use `rowDraggable` to reorder schemas, display columns, or metric rows
  - many operational tables use row selection together with paginated footer summaries
  - CMDB and view-management screens use the shared field-setting affordance to control visible columns instead of building app-local table settings chrome
  - the stable shared layer is the enhanced table shell itself: resizable columns, shared pagination chrome, optional row selection summary, optional row drag, and optional field-setting modal
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/DataDisplay`
  - Fix the shared drag callback contract at the source rather than compensating in app code
  - Expand Storybook so the source of truth reflects the real advanced table modes already exercised in production
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/components/custom-table/index.tsx`
  - Corrected `onRowDragEnd` to emit `(targetTableData, sourceIndex, targetIndex)` in the same order promised by the shared prop contract
  - No caller edits were required in this slice because existing single-argument handlers keep working, and multi-argument handlers now receive the intended indices
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/custom-table.stories.tsx`
  - Added row-selection-with-pagination, row-draggable, and field-setting stories so Storybook now documents the table modes most likely to diverge across apps if left implicit

### Group tree select contract stabilization
- Candidate: `group-tree-select`
- Reason: `GroupTreeSelect` is a broadly reused shared selector across `cmdb`, `monitor`, `log`, `job`, `system-manager`, `ops-analysis`, `alarm`, `mlops`, and `opspilot`, but its Storybook reliability and contract coverage lagged behind production. The missing piece was not a new abstraction; it was a stable Storybook data source for organization trees plus explicit coverage for runtime modes already used across apps
- Cross-app comparison:
  - many forms use the default multi-select ownership flow
  - alarm, system-manager, and some rule/config flows use single-select mode
  - multiple record and settings screens rely on search, clearing, or locked-selection behavior layered onto the same shared selector
  - the stable shared layer is the organization-tree selector itself, not the surrounding forms or permission rules
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/DataEntry`
  - Do not fork app-local organization selectors
  - Stabilize Storybook by mocking `userInfo` context at the Storybook layer, then document the real selector modes already exercised in production
- Migration:
  - Added a Storybook alias for `@/context/userInfo` so organization-aware shared components render against deterministic mock group data in Storybook instead of depending on runtime API availability
  - No app caller edits were required in this slice
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/.storybook/main.ts` and added `web/.storybook/mocks/user-info.tsx`
  - Updated `web/src/stories/group-tree-select.stories.tsx`
  - Added searchable/clearable, locked-values, and filtered single-select stories so Storybook now reflects the practical selector modes used by shared and app-level forms

### Time selector story contract expansion
- Candidate: `time-selector`
- Reason: `TimeSelector` is a high-reuse shared control across `log`, `monitor`, `alarm`, `system-manager`, `ops-analysis`, and `opspilot`, but Storybook still mostly framed it as a basic recent-time selector plus refresh control. Production usage already relies on two additional shared behaviors that were not explicit in the contract: preloaded absolute time ranges and imperative `ref` readback
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `log` search and dashboard pages keep a `TimeSelectorRef` and read back the current range through `getValue()`
  - `ops-analysis` unified filter flows and `opspilot` studio log pages use the same shared control in `onlyTimeSelect` mode, including absolute-range defaults
  - monitor dashboards add custom refresh-frequency lists on top of the same base shell
  - the stable shared layer is the combined time-range selector + refresh cadence control, including relative selection, absolute selection, and imperative readback
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/DataEntry`
  - No runtime API change was needed in this slice because the component already supports these production modes
  - Strengthen Storybook so the shared contract visibly includes the absolute-range default state and ref-driven integration path
- Migration:
  - No caller edits were required
  - Validated that current `log`, `monitor`, `ops-analysis`, and `opspilot` flows are already sharing the same component rather than maintaining app-local time selector variants
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/time-selector.stories.tsx`
  - Added a preset absolute-range story and a ref-readback story so Storybook now documents the real integration patterns used by pages that synchronize queries or trigger fetches from the current selector state

### Permission wrapper contract hardening
- Candidate: `permission`
- Reason: `PermissionWrapper` is one of the most reused shared guard components in `web/`, spanning `cmdb`, `opspilot`, `ops-analysis`, `node-manager`, `alarm`, `log`, and multiple shared business components. Its Storybook contract was too thin, and the shared implementation had a stale-render risk because the custom memo comparator ignored `instPermissions` and `children`, both of which vary in real production usage
- Cross-app comparison:
  - many pages only rely on route-level `requiredPermissions`
  - CMDB, OpsPilot document flows, and other record-level action menus also pass `instPermissions` to gate the same shared action shell using instance-specific capabilities
  - some callers depend on custom `fallback` rendering, while others simply dim the original child action under the default no-auth tooltip
  - the stable shared layer is the permission gate itself: route permission check, optional instance permission check, optional fallback rendering, and disabled-tooltip presentation
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Guards`
  - Do not create app-specific permission wrappers for record-level actions
  - Harden the existing shared component so it reliably rerenders when real permission-related props or guarded content change
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/components/permission/index.tsx`
  - Removed the unsafe custom `React.memo` comparator so updates to `instPermissions`, `children`, and other varying props are no longer skipped by the shared guard layer
  - No caller edits were required in this slice because the public API stays the same
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/permission.stories.tsx`
  - Added custom-fallback and instance-permission-denied stories so Storybook now reflects the real cross-app guard variants instead of only one basic unauthorized case

### Operate modal visibility contract alignment
- Candidate: `operate-modal`
- Reason: `OperateModal` is one of the highest-reuse shared shell components in `web/`, but real production callers are still split across two visibility contracts: modern `open` and legacy `visible`. Leaving that split implicit in Ant Design passthrough props makes the shared API easy to misuse and weakens Storybook as the source of truth
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `OperateModal` is imported broadly across `monitor`, `cmdb`, `job`, `system-manager`, `node-manager`, `mlops`, `log`, and `opspilot`
  - current production call sites use both `open={...}` and `visible={...}` on the same shared modal shell
  - the stable shared layer is the styled modal container itself: header, subtitle, footer, and visibility control. Business forms and selectors remain app-owned
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Feedback`
  - Do not introduce another modal wrapper for legacy pages
  - Harden the existing shared shell so the visibility contract is explicit and migration-friendly: `open` is preferred, `visible` remains supported as a compatibility alias
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/components/operate-modal/index.tsx`
  - Added explicit `open` and `visible` props, with `open` taking precedence and `visible` serving as a fallback
  - No caller edits were required in this slice, which keeps change risk low while allowing app pages to migrate gradually toward `open`
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/operate-modal.stories.tsx`
  - Added a legacy-`visible` story so Storybook now documents both the preferred contract and the compatibility path still exercised in production

### Chart legend interaction contract hardening
- Candidate: `chart-legend`
- Reason: `ChartLegend` is already the shared legend primitive across `log` and `ops-analysis` dashboard charts, but its shared contract still had two governance gaps: interaction semantics were mouse-only `div`/`tr` click targets, and inactive-state swatch styling diverged between table and list layouts
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `log` line/bar/bar-line/pie widgets all route legend selection through the same shared component and dispatch the result back into ECharts selection state
  - `ops-analysis` dashboard charts reuse the same shared legend primitive for vertical list mode
  - the stable shared layer is one legend interaction grammar with three presentation variants: vertical list, horizontal list, and table-with-title
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Charts`
  - Do not fork app-local legend variants
  - Harden the shared primitive itself so all consuming apps inherit the same keyboard-accessible interaction model and inactive visual treatment
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/components/chart-legend/index.tsx`
  - Replaced clickable non-semantic containers with explicit buttons and `aria-pressed` across table, horizontal, and vertical modes
  - Unified inactive swatch coloring through one shared helper so the visual contract stays consistent across layouts
  - No app-level caller changes were required because the shared prop API remains stable
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/chart-legend.stories.tsx`
  - Added dense long-label table and horizontal stories so Storybook now documents the real layout pressure seen in dashboard legends instead of only the short-label happy path

### Chart surface story contract expansion
- Candidate: `chart-surface`
- Reason: `ChartSurface` is the shared base container behind chart primitives and business chart components, but Storybook only covered the default spinner and plain empty state while production already uses custom loading content and can pass through richer empty-state props
- Cross-app comparison:
  - shared business components such as `log-donut-chart` and `log-kpi-card` already provide custom loading indicators through `loadingContent`
  - chart primitives and wrappers rely on the same base surface to normalize loading/data/empty transitions
  - The stable shared layer is the state shell itself: data-present render, custom loading chrome, and configurable empty presentation
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Charts`
  - No runtime API change was needed in this slice because the current surface already supports the proven production variants
  - Strengthen Storybook so the contract explicitly includes the richer empty-state path, not only the default compact empty state
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required
  - Validated that shared chart/business components are already exercising custom loading behavior through the base surface
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/chart-surface.stories.tsx`
  - Added a decorated empty-state story so Storybook now documents the configurable empty-state path alongside default loading and custom loading content

### Chart-with-dimension panel story contract expansion
- Candidate: `chart-with-dimension-panel`
- Reason: the shared panel shell is already used by monitor and MLOps chart wrappers both with and without dimension sidebars, but Storybook only documented the sidebar variants and skipped the plain chart-only container mode that production also relies on
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor bar/line and MLOps line wrappers all use the same chart shell
  - some flows render a filter or table sidebar, while others fall back to a plain chart column and pass an explicit `containerClassName`
  - The stable shared layer is one chart shell that can host either chart-plus-sidebar or chart-only layouts without forking app-local wrappers
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Charts`
  - No runtime API change was needed; the missing piece was Storybook coverage for the no-sidebar mode already exercised in production
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required
  - Validated that current monitor/MLOps wrappers already rely on both sidebar and chart-only modes through the same shared shell
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/chart-with-dimension-panel.stories.tsx`
  - Added a chart-only story so Storybook now documents the plain shell mode alongside filter-sidebar and table-sidebar variants

### Time-series area chart canvas story contract expansion
- Candidate: `time-series-area-chart-canvas`
- Reason: the shared area-chart canvas is already the base primitive for monitor and MLOps line-chart flows, but Storybook only covered a default time-axis chart and a brush variant, leaving two real runtime modes under-documented: non-selectable numeric step axes and pre-tooltip overlay rendering
- Cross-app comparison:
  - MLOps training charts use the shared canvas with `allowSelect={false}` and a numeric `step` x-axis rather than time-based drag selection
  - monitor line charts layer gap/threshold overlays before and after tooltip handling, so the shared contract needs to visibly represent overlay ordering, not just plain series rendering
  - The stable layer is one chart-canvas primitive with selectable vs non-selectable modes, optional brush, and optional pre/post-series overlay slots
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Charts`
  - No runtime API expansion was needed in this slice because the shared component already supports these production modes
  - Strengthen Storybook so the source of truth reflects both MLOps step-axis usage and monitor overlay usage
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required
  - Validated that monitor and MLOps are already sharing the same primitive and expanded contract coverage at the story layer instead of creating app-local chart canvas variants
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/time-series-area-chart-canvas.stories.tsx`
  - Added a non-selectable step-axis story and a pre-tooltip overlay story so Storybook now documents the real production canvas modes beyond the default and brush examples

### Code editor contract alignment
- Candidate: `code-editor`
- Reason: `CodeEditor` is already the shared editor/viewer surface for node-manager guidance flows and monitor integration configuration screens, but production callers were still relying on two behaviors the shared component did not explicitly own in its contract: a legacy `showCopy` prop and a `loading` state passed into read-only command/config previews
- Cross-app comparison:
  - node-manager guidance/config pages use the editor as a read-only command or template viewer and already pass `loading` while async content is being prepared
  - monitor configuration pages primarily use the modern `headerOptions={{ copy, fullscreen }}` path, but one manual-config screen still uses `showCopy`
  - The stable shared layer is one technical editor shell with optional header actions and optional loading chrome, not separate app-local wrappers
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Editors`
  - Harden the shared contract to explicitly support the proven compatibility path instead of forcing a broad caller rewrite in one slice
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/components/code-editor/index.tsx` so `loading` is rendered through the shared shell rather than leaking to underlying editor props
  - Added legacy `showCopy` compatibility while keeping `headerOptions` as the preferred modern API
  - No runtime caller edits were required in this slice; existing node-manager and monitor flows now sit on an explicit shared contract
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/code-editor.stories.tsx`
  - Added loading and legacy-`showCopy` stories so Storybook now documents the real shared usage modes instead of only the newest header-options path

### Chart series tooltip story contract expansion
- Candidate: `chart-series-tooltip`
- Reason: `ChartSeriesTooltip` is already the shared tooltip shell behind monitor and MLOps chart primitives, but Storybook only covered a standard detail-rich row set and a compact metric-row case, leaving two real runtime edges under-documented: scrollable long tooltips and rows that only show value content
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor line/bar charts can render many series rows at once and already pass runtime `maxHeight`/`maxWidth`
  - monitor and MLOps flows both sometimes produce rows where description collapses down heavily or is effectively absent, while the numeric/value signal remains the useful part
  - The stable layer is the shared tooltip row grammar: title, colored marker, optional description, optional value, and sorted rows
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Charts`
  - No runtime API expansion was needed in this slice because the shared component already supports these variants; the missing piece was contract visibility in Storybook
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required
  - Validated existing monitor/MLOps usage and expanded the shared contract coverage at the story layer rather than forking tooltip variants in app code
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/chart-series-tooltip.stories.tsx`
  - Added a scrollable long-details story and a value-only row story so Storybook now represents the real production tooltip modes instead of only the most descriptive cases

### Content drawer content-state alignment
- Candidate: `content-drawer`
- Reason: `ContentDrawer` is already shared between direct OpsPilot content preview flows and a CMDB config-file wrapper, but its base contract still treated `content=""` as if no `content` prop had been provided at all, which blurred the difference between an intentionally empty preview and a missing content input
- Cross-app comparison:
  - OpsPilot knowledge preview flows pass raw string content directly into the shared drawer through `useContentDrawer`
  - CMDB builds a richer wrapper with metadata and its own explicit no-content message inside `children`
  - The stable shared layer is the drawer shell plus its plain-text content/fallback behavior; rich metadata remains app-owned
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Overlays`
  - Harden the shared plain-content contract so string content is rendered whenever the prop is present, even when it is an empty string
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/components/content-drawer/index.tsx` to distinguish “content prop not provided” from “provided but empty”
  - Added a default lightweight empty fallback in the shared shell for no-content scenarios when callers do not provide their own custom content
  - Existing CMDB wrapper behavior is preserved because it passes `children`; OpsPilot direct string previews now sit on a clearer content contract
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/content-drawer.stories.tsx`
  - Added loading and empty-content stories so Storybook now documents the full shared text-preview state model instead of only populated content

### Step wizard flow story contract expansion
- Candidate: `step-wizard-flow`
- Reason: `StepWizardFlow` is already the shared shell behind node-manager controller installation plus monitor/log onboarding wizards, but Storybook only documented a minimal demo and did not make the production state-transition contract explicit
- Cross-app comparison:
  - node-manager controller install and monitor/log k8s/flow onboarding all use the same three-step shell
  - the stable shared behavior is not just visual step switching; it is the context contract for carrying state forward with `next(...)` and returning to the first step with `reset(...)`
  - current production evidence does not show cross-app use of arbitrary `goTo`, so broadening that surface would be speculative rather than governance-driven
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Navigation`
  - Do not expand runtime API in this slice
  - Strengthen Storybook so it documents the real shared behavior proven by onboarding flows: accumulated state updates and restart/reset
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller changes were required
  - Validated that node-manager, monitor flow, monitor k8s, and log k8s all already align on the same three-step stateful wizard shell
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/step-wizard-flow.stories.tsx`
  - Added an accumulated-state onboarding flow story that demonstrates function-style `next(...)` updates and `reset(...)` restart behavior, matching the real production usage pattern

### Selection preview layout contract hardening
- Candidate: `selection-preview-layout`
- Reason: the shared selection workflow shell is already reused by job host selection, monitor event asset selection, and monitor integration instance selection, but the shared contract still left two production-relevant gaps undocumented and underpowered: an empty selected-state produced a blank preview pane, and clear/remove interactions were rendered as plain clickable text/icon chrome instead of explicit controls
- Cross-app comparison:
  - all three current consumers use the same two-column grammar: a searchable selection surface on the left and a removable selected-items preview on the right
  - all three flows naturally hit the zero-selected state during first open and after clearing selections
  - the stable layer is the preview interaction shell itself, not the table/tree content inside the primary pane
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Selection`
  - Harden the existing shared contract instead of adding app-local empty states or button wrappers in each caller
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/components/selection-preview-layout/index.tsx` so the preview pane shows a default empty state when no items are selected
  - Converted clear/remove interactions to explicit button controls while keeping the public API unchanged
  - Added optional `emptyText` for future business-specific overrides without forcing one now
  - Existing job and monitor callers inherit the stronger behavior without page-level rewrites
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/selection-preview-layout.stories.tsx`
  - Added an empty-selection story so Storybook now proves the zero-state contract rather than only populated preview cases

### Tree selector panel story contract expansion
- Candidate: `tree-selector-panel`
- Reason: `TreeSelectorPanel` is already the shared left-rail selector shell for monitor event pages, monitor integration pages, and log integration pages, but Storybook only documented a basic tree and missed the production-heavy variants that actually define the contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor event/template/view/asset flows use the plain sidebar version with loading states and leaf selection
  - monitor integration list/object flows also use draggable sorting with object-group payload generation
  - log integration list/receive flows use the `panel` surface plus a visible `all` root item as the default selection anchor
  - The stable layer is one selector shell with a few real variants: plain vs panel surface, optional `all` root preservation, draggable ordering, and loading/empty states
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Navigation`
  - No behavioral abstraction change was needed in this slice; the stronger move is to make Storybook reflect the real multi-app contract so future edits converge on one proven surface
- Migration:
  - No runtime caller migrations were required
  - Verified shared usage across monitor and log and expanded the contract coverage at the story layer rather than spawning app-local selector variants
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/tree-selector-panel.stories.tsx`
  - Added panel stories with a real `all` root item, draggable panel ordering, loading state, and empty state so Storybook now mirrors the production selector modes instead of only the simplest tree case

### Operate drawer visibility contract alignment
- Candidate: `operate-drawer`
- Reason: `OperateDrawer` is already a shared shell reused across alarm, monitor, log, node-manager, ops-analysis, and adjacent admin surfaces, but production still mixes legacy `visible` control and newer `open` control while Storybook only documented the `open` contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - alarm settings and several monitor flows already pass `open`
  - node-manager guidance drawers, log search/event drawers, and multiple older management flows still pass `visible`
  - The stable layer is one shared right-side operation drawer shell; the variance is only the control prop shape left behind by gradual migration
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Feedback`
  - Harden the shared contract to explicitly support both visibility inputs during migration instead of forcing a risky multi-app caller rewrite in one pass
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/components/operate-drawer/index.tsx` so `open` remains the preferred prop and takes precedence, while legacy `visible` is still honored
  - No runtime caller edits were required in this slice; existing surfaces across multiple apps now share one explicit compatibility contract
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/operate-drawer.stories.tsx`
  - Added a legacy `visible` alias story so Storybook reflects both the modern contract and the compatibility path still exercised in production

### Introduction responsive contract hardening
- Candidate: `introduction`
- Reason: `Introduction` is already a true shared page-intro component reused across alarm settings and CMDB management pages, but its contract still carried a hard-coded `min-width: 800px`, which could force overflow and inconsistent spacing in narrower shells even though the callers all treat it as a full-width shared intro block
- Cross-app comparison:
  - alarm settings pages (`correlationRules`, `alertAssign`, `alertEnrichment`, `shieldStrategy`, `operationLog`) all use the same intro-card pattern above the main work surface
  - CMDB pages (`management`, `operationLog`, `customReporting`, `autoDiscovery`) use the identical shared intro shell with different copy density
  - The stable layer is the intro block styling and layout responsiveness; only title/message content changes per app
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Content`
  - Harden the shared layout contract rather than letting each app work around width issues locally
- Migration:
  - Removed the component-level fixed `min-width` style from `web/src/components/introduction/index.module.scss`
  - Updated `web/src/components/introduction/index.tsx` so `minWidth` remains supported but is constrained by `min(100%, <value>)`, preventing narrow-shell overflow without changing caller APIs
  - No page-level migrations were required; all existing alarm and CMDB consumers inherit the safer responsive behavior
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/introduction.stories.tsx`
  - Added a narrow-container story so Storybook now verifies the responsive contract instead of only wide-shell usage

### Horizontal category bar chart contract expansion
- Candidate: MLOps `horizontalBarChart` versus shared `horizontal-category-bar-chart`
- Reason: the MLOps training metric single-value bar kept a second app-local horizontal bar implementation even though log dashboards were already standardized on `Framework/Charts/Category/HorizontalBarChart`
- Cross-app comparison:
  - log widgets (`dockerBarChart`, `mysqlInstanceBar`, `redisNodeCompareBar`, and adjacent bars) already share the same horizontal category bar shell for ranked/category metrics
  - MLOps uses the same base visual grammar, but adds three contract variants the shared component did not yet document well: hidden category axis, explicit value-axis range, and compact single-metric labels including negative values
  - The stable framework layer is still one chart primitive; the MLOps-specific layer is only metric name selection and unit/range formatting
- Decision:
  - Keep `horizontal-category-bar-chart` as the shared `Framework/Charts` primitive
  - Convert the MLOps wrapper into a thin business adapter instead of preserving a duplicate Recharts implementation
- Migration:
  - Expanded `web/src/components/horizontal-category-bar-chart/index.tsx` to support category-axis hiding, explicit value-axis domain, value tooltip formatting, and single-metric label styling
  - Re-implemented `web/src/app/mlops/components/charts/horizontalBarChart.tsx` on top of the shared framework component
  - Preserved MLOps-specific precision and unit formatting inside the wrapper rather than pushing training semantics into the framework contract
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/horizontal-category-bar-chart.stories.tsx`
  - Added single-metric positive and negative range stories so Storybook now documents the MLOps-compatible framework contract instead of only the log-ranked variants

### MLOps training metric chart de-duplication
- Candidate: MLOps `simpleLineChart` and the unused local `barChart`
- Reason: the training metric detail view still carried an app-local area-chart shell that duplicated `Framework/Charts/TimeSeries/AreaChartCanvas` plus tooltip chrome, and it also kept an extra local `barChart` file with no remaining callers
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor and mlops line-chart flows already rely on the shared `time-series-area-chart-canvas` primitive for interactive area/line rendering
  - the stable layer is the chart rendering shell, tooltip framing, grid, and series wiring
  - the varying layer in MLOps remains business-owned: `step` as the x-axis label and metric-unit formatting for training runs
- Decision:
  - Keep the training metric wrapper app-local as `Business/MLOps`
  - Remove the framework duplication by re-implementing the wrapper on top of the shared primitive instead of maintaining a second raw Recharts shell
  - Delete the dead local `barChart` file because it no longer represents a real app contract
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/app/mlops/components/charts/simpleLineChart.tsx` to render through `TimeSeriesAreaChartCanvas`
  - Switched the wrapper to `ChartSeriesTooltip` so MLOps metric hover states now match the shared chart-system tooltip contract
  - Removed `web/src/app/mlops/components/charts/barChart.tsx` after verifying there are no remaining imports
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/chart-series-tooltip.stories.tsx`
  - Added a centered metric-row variant that matches the MLOps training metric tooltip pattern, so the shared tooltip contract now covers both detail-rich and metric-compact usages

### Entity list contract hardening
- Candidate: `entity-list`
- Reason: `EntityList` is already a true shared framework component used by MLOps, System Manager, Node Manager, and OpsPilot tool surfaces, but its runtime accessibility behavior and Storybook stories lagged behind production expectations
- Cross-app comparison:
  - MLOps datasets, system-manager application/security/channel pages, node-manager collector/controller/cloudregion pages, and opspilot tool pages all rely on the same card-list shell for dense entity browsing
  - The stable layer is the search/filter/action/card-grid shell, not the business data itself
  - The shared component had platform-wide gaps: clickable cards were not keyboard reachable, icon-only affordances lacked explicit labels, and Storybook still documented stale props that no longer matched the real API
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/DataDisplay`
  - Strengthen the existing shared contract rather than creating a parallel list shell
- Migration:
  - Updated `web/src/components/entity-list/index.tsx` so clickable cards and the add-new tile are keyboard reachable and expose proper button semantics
  - Replaced icon-only `div` triggers with accessible buttons for card actions and overflow menus
  - Added a shared focus-visible treatment in `web/src/components/entity-list/index.module.scss`
  - No caller rewrites were required; all current consumers benefit immediately from the hardened shared behavior
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/entity-list.stories.tsx`
  - Removed stale story args that no longer exist on the public API and replaced them with real toolbar/filter/action scenarios that match production usage

### Chart with dimension panel
- Candidate: `chart-with-dimension-panel`
- Reason: monitor and mlops chart wrappers were both repeating the same framework shell around shared chart primitives: `ChartSurface` plus a primary chart slot plus optional dimension filter or dimension table side panels
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `monitor/components/charts/lineChart`, `monitor/components/charts/barChart`, and `mlops/components/charts/lineChart` already shared the same panel grammar even though their tooltip logic, overlays, and data transforms differ
  - The stable layer is the layout contract for chart + side panel composition
  - The varying layer remains app-owned: series wiring, threshold/event overlays, brush behavior, and metric formatting
- Decision:
  - Promote the shell as a shared `Framework/Charts` component
  - Keep business and domain chart behavior in existing wrappers, but stop duplicating the surrounding panel layout
- Migration:
  - Added `web/src/components/chart-with-dimension-panel/index.tsx`
  - Migrated monitor bar chart, monitor line chart, and mlops line chart to the shared shell
  - Preserved all existing tooltip, overlay, threshold, and brush behavior in app-local wrappers
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/chart-with-dimension-panel.stories.tsx`
  - Stories cover both filter-sidebar and table-sidebar layouts so the shared shell is documented independently of any one app's chart data

### Chart dimension controls contract hardening
- Candidate: `chart-dimension-filter` and `chart-dimension-table`
- Reason: both components are already shared chart-system primitives, but the filter still relied on mouse-only list items and the table's columns-mode contract still depended on `details.value1` existing, which made the public API weaker than the Storybook surface implied
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor and mlops both consume these primitives through shared chart wrappers
  - The stable layer is the dimension toggle/table contract itself, not any one app's metric names
  - The broken assumption was structural, not business-specific: keyboard access in the filter and first-series column inference in the table
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Framework/Charts`
  - Harden the existing shared primitives rather than forking app-specific variants
- Migration:
  - `chart-dimension-filter` now renders keyboard-reachable toggle buttons with `aria-pressed`
  - `chart-dimension-table` now derives column-mode detail fields from the first available populated series details instead of hard-coding `value1`
  - No caller changes were required; current chart wrappers inherit the stronger contract automatically
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/chart-dimension-filter.stories.tsx`
  - Updated `web/src/stories/chart-with-dimension-panel.stories.tsx` with a no-`value1` table case so the shared contract covers the generalized details mapping

### Alarm action contract alignment
- Candidate: `alarm-action`
- Reason: the shared alarm action shell was already in Storybook, but runtime code still reached back into `next-auth`, alarm/incident API hooks, and alarm common context from inside the shared component, which meant the Storybook contract was not the real production contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - The same action shell is reused from alarm list bulk actions, alarm row actions, alarm detail drawer, incident detail actions, gantt detail, link modal detail, and related-alert drill-ins
  - The stable shared layer is the action UI and status gating logic
  - The varying layer is page-owned runtime data: current username, assignee options, and the action executor for alarm vs incident flows
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Business/Alarm` shared component
  - Remove component-internal app fallbacks and make runtime dependencies explicit props so Storybook remains the single truthful contract surface
- Migration:
  - `web/src/components/alarm-action/index.tsx` now depends only on explicit `currentUsername`, `assigneeOptions`, and `operateAction`
  - `AlarmTable`, `AlarmDetailDrawer`, and `RelatedAlertsPanel` now forward `alarmActionProps` instead of relying on shared-layer hook access
  - Migrated alarm list, incident detail, gantt detail, and link modal callers to build and pass explicit action contracts for alarm and incident flows
  - This removes duplicate hidden implementations of the same runtime wiring pattern across alarm surfaces
- Storybook:
  - Existing `web/src/stories/alarm-action.stories.tsx` already represented the explicit contract and now matches production
  - Updated `alarm-detail-drawer` and `related-alerts-panel` stories to pass the same explicit action contract used at runtime

### Log donut chart
- Candidate: `dockerDonutChart`
- Reason: log dashboard pie/donut widgets for apache, nginx, rabbitmq, mongodb, postgresql, kafka, elasticsearch, windows-event, syslog, flow, docker severity, mysql latency buckets, redis error categories, and http status categories were all routing through the same donut shell, but the shared implementation was hidden under a docker widget path and had no Storybook contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - This is a log-domain shared business component rather than a framework pie primitive
  - Most wrappers pass already-normalized `{name,value}`-like rows, while mysql, redis, docker severity, and http status wrappers perform app-specific normalization first
  - The stable layer is the donut shell itself: center total, responsive radius calculation, right-side legend formatting, loading state, and mapped key/value contract
- Decision:
  - Promote the donut shell to an explicit `Business/Log` shared component
  - Keep upstream normalization logic in thin wrappers where query output shapes still differ by source
- Migration:
  - Moved the shared donut implementation to `web/src/components/log-donut-chart/index.tsx`
  - Migrated all log pie/donut wrappers that previously imported `widgets/docker/dockerDonutChart` to the shared component path
  - Preserved `widgets/docker/index.ts` export compatibility so widget registry wiring stays stable
  - Inlined the donut tooltip numeric formatter so the shared component no longer depends on `@/app/log/utils/common`
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/log-donut-chart.stories.tsx`
  - Stories cover direct mapped rows, custom field mapping, and long legend labels so the shared contract is explicit

### Log chart colors
- Candidate: `useChartColors`
- Reason: log chart shells and shared business components were all depending on the same theme/color hook, but that hook still lived under `widgets/docker/`, which left shared components depending on an app-local widget path
- Cross-app comparison:
  - This is not a Storybook-facing UI component; it is shared style infrastructure inside the log chart family
  - The same token set is used by shared log business components (`log-kpi-card`, `log-donut-chart`) and a wide set of app-local log chart widgets
  - The stable contract is the theme-derived color token object, not any single widget presentation
- Decision:
  - Promote it to a shared log chart infrastructure module
  - Keep it outside Storybook because helpers/hooks are not themselves standard shared UI components
- Migration:
  - Moved the hook implementation to `web/src/components/log-chart-colors/index.tsx`
  - Migrated shared business components and log chart widgets to import the shared path instead of the old docker-widget path
  - This removes an app-local dependency edge from the shared component graph
- Storybook:
  - No standalone story added
  - Shared components that consume this hook remain represented through their own stories

### Log chart style helpers
- Candidate: `chartStyle`
- Reason: multiple log chart widgets were reusing the same gradient and area-style helpers, but those helpers still lived under the app-local widget tree even after adjacent shared chart infrastructure had been promoted
- Cross-app comparison:
  - This is shared log chart infrastructure, not a Storybook-facing component
  - The same helper set powers bar gradients and line area treatments across generic log widgets, docker widgets, redis/http specializations, and other log chart variants
  - The stable contract is a tiny set of visual helper functions (`createHorizontalBarGradient`, `createVerticalBarGradient`, `createSoftLineArea`)
- Decision:
  - Promote it to a shared log chart helper module
  - Keep it outside Storybook because helpers are implementation infrastructure rather than a component contract surface
- Migration:
  - Moved the helper module to `web/src/components/log-chart-style/index.ts`
  - Migrated dependent log chart widgets to the shared helper path
  - This removes another app-local dependency edge from the chart/shared-component graph
- Storybook:
  - No standalone story added
  - Its behavior remains indirectly covered through the stories of shared log/chart components that render these treatments

### Log KPI card
- Candidate: `comKpiCard`
- Reason: log dashboard KPI widgets such as apache, nginx, rabbitmq, mongodb, file-integrity, syslog, postgresql, windows-event, elasticsearch, kafka, docker, redis, mysql, flow, and http already converge on the same KPI shell, but that shell was still hidden inside an app-local widget file and had no Storybook contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - This is not yet a cross-app framework primitive; it is a log-domain business component with real reuse across many log integrations
  - Most widgets use the default sum-or-latest calculation, while HTTP and some latest-value widgets provide custom metric calculation behavior
  - The stable layer is the KPI card shell: value typography, previous-period delta, sparkline, loading state, and accent-color model
- Decision:
  - Keep as a `Business/Log` shared component rather than promoting it to `Framework`
  - Formalize its public contract and document the calculation override pattern in Storybook
- Migration:
  - Promoted the implementation to `web/src/components/log-kpi-card/index.tsx`
  - Exported a named business contract from `LogKpiCard` (`LogKpiCardProps`, metric result type, and calculate-metric type)
  - Migrated the shared widget wrapper entry point plus all log KPI wrappers to the shared component path, avoiding duplicate KPI shells
  - Deferred any framework extraction until a second app demonstrates the same KPI shell and API needs
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/log-kpi-card.stories.tsx`
  - Stories cover the default sum variant, a latest-value variant, and a multi-query ratio calculator variant so the shared business contract is explicit

### Time-series composed chart
- Candidate: `time-series-composed-chart`
- Reason: log dashboard business widgets such as `apacheTrend`, `nginxTrend`, `rabbitmqTrend`, `mongodbTrend`, `fileIntegrityTrend`, `syslogTrend`, `postgresqlTrend`, `windowsEventTrend`, `elasticsearchTrend`, `kafkaTrend`, and `httpRequestTrend` all repeated the same framework shell: time-sorted category axis, shared tooltip/grid/legend chrome, bar+line series composition, optional dual y-axis, and `ChartSurface` handling
- Cross-app comparison:
  - The repeated structure appears across multiple log integration widgets, even when the business metrics differ
  - Some widgets are bar+line dual-axis charts, some are line-only, and HTTP latency uses a custom secondary-axis formatter
  - The stable layer is the chart composition contract itself; field semantics and labels remain business-owned
- Decision:
  - Promote the repeated ECharts shell to a shared `Framework/Charts` primitive
  - Keep data-field mapping, business labels, and domain-specific preprocessing inside thin app-local adapters
- Migration:
  - Added `web/src/components/time-series-composed-chart/index.tsx`
  - Migrated log dashboard trend widgets for apache, nginx, rabbitmq, mongodb, file-integrity, syslog, postgresql, windows-event, elasticsearch, kafka, and http request latency to the shared primitive
  - Left more custom trend widgets such as redis and grouped HTTP status aggregation local until their contracts stabilize further
- Storybook:
  - Added `web/src/stories/time-series-composed-chart.stories.tsx`
  - Stories cover line-only, bar+line dual-axis, and custom secondary-axis formatting variants

### Chart with sidebar legend
- Candidate: `chart-with-sidebar-legend`
- Reason: log dashboard `comLine/comBar/comPie/comBarLine` and ops-analysis dashboard `comLine/comBar/comPie` all repeated the same shell pattern: `ChartSurface` plus a chart pane plus a right-side legend pane, with log additionally repeating the same responsive legend-hiding behavior
- Cross-app comparison:
  - log uses a responsive sidebar legend with table-style rows and hides the legend when the chart area becomes too narrow
  - ops-analysis uses a fixed vertical sidebar legend with the same chart/legend split but different legend styling and no responsive hiding
  - The data transforms, ECharts options, and interactions still vary by widget, but the surrounding layout shell is stable and repeated
- Decision:
  - Promote the shell to a shared `Framework/Charts` component
  - Keep chart option logic local to each widget and only unify the layout boundary around chart content + sidebar legend
- Migration:
  - Added `web/src/components/chart-with-sidebar-legend/index.tsx`
  - Migrated log dashboard `comLine`, `comBar`, `comPie`, and `comBarLine`

  - log k8s uses the same shell with an extra preset/runtime hint, so the content density is higher even though the structure is unchanged
  - The component contract is stable; only the production-used content shapes were under-documented in Storybook

  - No API change needed; expand Storybook to cover the actual multi-app information variants

### Integration access complete

  - monitor k8s and log k8s use a two-action completion state, with log also overriding the success icon set
  - CMDB uses the same shell with no `subDescription` and a `Done` primary action instead of a navigation CTA

  - No component API change needed; the public props already cover the real variants
  - Expand Storybook so the shared contract documents all production-used variants instead of only the monitor-flavored ones

  - Shared contract verification now happens through broader story coverage rather than app-local assumptions

  - Added log k8s and CMDB k8s variants so icon overrides, missing `subDescription`, and alternative primary actions are part of the shared source of truth

### K8s common issues drawer
- Candidate: `k8s-common-issues-drawer`
- Reason: shared troubleshooting drawer already reused by monitor and log k8s onboarding, but its public API only exposed imperative ref control, so Storybook could not represent the open drawer state as an actual contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor and log both open the same drawer shell from page-owned wrappers with different issue copy
  - The shell structure is stable; only the issue content varies by app
  - Storybook needs declarative open-state control even when production wrappers prefer imperative refs
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Business/K8s` shared component
  - Support both imperative and declarative open control so runtime wrappers and Storybook use the same component contract
- Migration:
  - `K8sCommonIssuesDrawer` now supports optional `open`, `defaultOpen`, and `onOpenChange`
  - Existing monitor/log wrappers keep using `showDrawer()` through the forwarded ref with no page rewrite required
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/k8s-common-issues-drawer.stories.tsx`
  - Default story now renders the open drawer state directly, and a closed controlled variant documents the alternative control mode

### K8s collector install
- Candidate: `k8s-collector-install`
- Reason: shared onboarding component already reused by monitor, log, and CMDB k8s setup flows, but its Storybook contract still missed the CMDB-specific pre-generation state
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor and log both render the shared install shell with a ready command and verification flow
  - CMDB reuses the same shell but has an additional real state before command generation, plus verify must stay disabled until a command exists
  - The UI shell is stable; the variance is state sequencing and page-owned actions
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Business/K8s` shared component
  - Expand the shared verification-state contract instead of keeping CMDB-only coercion inside the page wrapper
- Migration:
  - `K8sCollectorInstall` now accepts `idle` as a first-class `verificationStatus`
  - CMDB collector setup now passes its real state directly instead of rewriting `idle` to `waiting`
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/k8s-collector-install.stories.tsx`
  - Added an `IdleBeforeGeneration` story so monitor/log/cmdb variants are all represented in the shared contract

### Declare incident
- Candidate: `declare-incident`
- Reason: shared business action used from alarm list/detail and related-alert flows, but its public API still exposed ad hoc `*Override` props that blurred the real component contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - Same create-or-link incident workflow is reused from alarm list, detail drawer, gantt detail, and related-alert flows
  - Stable contract is the row selection input, incident list fetch/create/update actions, assignee options, level options, and initial team/current user defaults
  - The remaining session/context/API fallbacks are runtime convenience, not the desired long-term contract shape
- Decision:
  - Keep as `Business/Alarm` shared component
  - Normalize its public API around real contract names and remove runtime fallbacks so Storybook owns the contract surface
- Migration:
  - Replaced `*Override` prop names with formal props like `fetchIncidentList`, `createIncident`, `updateIncident`, `assigneeOptions`, `levelOptions`, `initialTeamIds`, and `currentUsername`
  - Added local shared types for row data, option data, and incident summary data
  - Main consumers now pass explicit incident actions, assignee/level options, current user, and initial team defaults through `DeclareIncident`, `AlarmTable`, or `RelatedAlertsPanel` configuration
  - `AlarmTable` and `RelatedAlertsPanel` now render declare-incident affordances only when the declare contract is provided, instead of relying on component-internal alarm context fallbacks
- Storybook:
  - Updated `web/src/stories/declare-incident.stories.tsx`
  - Stories already exercise the explicit contract and now match production wiring

  - runtime usage is broad across monitor object dashboards, and the component already had a stable Storybook contract covering title, guide, empty state, center summary, and legend/info rows
  - keeping the implementation under the app tree would leave a duplicate non-business component boundary in place even though the system already treats it as shared monitor UI
- Cross-app comparison:
  - runtime use spans Apache, MySQL, MongoDB, Host, K8s Cluster, K8s Node, Nginx, Ping, Consul, RabbitMQ, Redis, Website, Tomcat, Zookeeper, and shared dashboard shells
  - these consumers vary in displayed metrics and card composition, but they keep the same semantic panel contract: donut distribution with a center value/caption and right-side breakdown rows
  - this is a governed monitor business panel with strong repeated usage, not a one-off chart wrapper
- Decision:
  - move `RingChartPanel` into the real shared family directory under [src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/ring-chart-panel.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/ring-chart-panel.tsx)
  - keep the public contract stable by continuing to expose it through the monitor widget family barrel
  - keep `TrendChartPanel` and `EChartsLineChart` for later slices, because those still represent the heavier remaining chart-runtime boundary
- Migration:
  - added [src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/ring-chart-panel.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/ring-chart-panel.tsx)

  - runtime consumers continue to use the stable family contract via `@/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets`, so no page-level API change was required
- Storybook:

  - the existing `Business/Monitor/MonitorRingChartPanel` story remains the contract source and now points at a real shared-family implementation
  - verification:
    - repo search shows the active `RingChartPanel` implementation now lives under `src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/`

### Monitor trend chart family extraction for TrendChartPanel and EChartsLineChart
- Candidate:
  - `TrendChartPanel`, exposed through [src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/index.ts](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/index.ts)
  - `EChartsLineChart`, also exposed through the same family barrel and covered by Storybook
  - their Storybook contracts:
    - [src/stories/monitor-trend-chart-panel.stories.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/stories/monitor-trend-chart-panel.stories.tsx)
    - [src/stories/monitor-echarts-line-chart.stories.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/stories/monitor-echarts-line-chart.stories.tsx)
- Reason:
  - `TrendChartPanel` has one of the broadest monitor reuse surfaces left in the app tree, but its implementation boundary was still tied to the app-local `EChartsLineChart`
  - `EChartsLineChart` already had a `Framework/Charts/*` Storybook contract, which meant we were publicly treating it as a shared chart primitive while still storing the implementation under app-local widgets
  - migrating only the panel would leave that public chart contract in an inconsistent state, so the two should move together as one chart family slice
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `TrendChartPanel` is reused across Apache, RabbitMQ, Elasticsearch, Console Server, Host, Website, Docker, Ping, Redis, Tomcat, Zookeeper, MySQL, MongoDB, K8s Cluster, common dashboard shells, and multiple network object dashboards
  - those pages vary in metric semantics, legend labels, body slots, and range-selection usage, but the panel contract remains stable: titled header, optional guide, legend row, line chart body, optional top/bottom content
  - `EChartsLineChart` itself is primarily a governed framework chart surface rather than a page-owned implementation detail: it handles tooltip rendering, empty/loading states, binary scaling, gap interval shading, and optional x-range selection in a single stable API
- Decision:
  - move `TrendChartPanel` and `EChartsLineChart` together into the real shared family directory
  - keep the public API stable by continuing to expose both through the monitor widget family barrel
  - allow the shared implementations to keep using existing monitor data/types and formatting utilities for now, because those are explicit module dependencies rather than hidden local contracts

  - added:
    - [src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/trend-chart-panel.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/trend-chart-panel.tsx)

    - [src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/echarts-line-chart.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/echarts-line-chart.tsx)

  - runtime consumers continue to use the stable family contract via `@/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets`, so no page-level API change was required

### OpsAnalysis single-value widget extraction into the shared family
- Candidate:

  - the contract is stable but richer than the log-analysis single-value shell: threshold-driven color, value mapping, compare percentage, unit-aware typography, and fallback sparkline behavior are all part of the business surface
  - this makes it a good convergence slice for shared ownership, while also proving that similar widget silhouettes across apps do not automatically justify cross-domain merging
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `OpsAnalysisSingle` and `LogAnalysisSingle` are visually adjacent, but their semantics and prop models diverge materially
  - `LogAnalysisSingle` is a lightweight KPI shell driven by a simple extracted number and optional color
  - `OpsAnalysisSingle` layers threshold interpretation, baseline comparison, fallback trend synthesis, value mapping, and ops-analysis-specific configuration shape on top of the single-value display
  - because of those differences, the correct move is to migrate it into the real ops-analysis shared family, not to collapse both widgets into one API
- Decision:
  - move `OpsAnalysisSingle` into the real shared `ops-analysis-widgets` family
  - keep it under `Business/OpsAnalysis/*` rather than promoting it to a framework primitive or forcing a premature merge with log-analysis
  - align its loading and empty states with the shared `ChartSurface` shell instead of preserving local ad hoc feedback handling

  - current evidence supports reusing shared tooltip and color grammar while keeping the business-specific graph contract local to the family

## Shared Components Already Standardized

These are already extracted and should remain the preferred source instead of creating app-local copies:

- Layout and shell:
  - `layout/sub-layout`
  - `page-layout`
  - `top-menu`
  - `top-section`
- Modal and form primitives:
  - `operate-modal`
  - `operate-drawer`
  - `single-file-upload-panel`
  - `multi-file-upload-panel`
  - `selection-preview-layout`
  - `content-drawer`
- Alarm business components:
  - `alarm-level-icon`
  - `alarm-search-filter`
  - `alarm-page-breadcrumb`
  - `alarm-event-table`
  - `alarm-filters`
  - `alarm-table`
  - `alarm-action`
  - `alarm-detail-drawer`
  - `declare-incident`
  - `related-alerts-panel`
- System manager business components:

  - `system-manager-role-transfer`
  - `system-manager-transfer-tree-panel`
  - `system-manager-group-edit-modal`
  - `system-manager-group-tree`
  - `system-manager-user-password-modal`

## Current Candidates

### 1. Chart primitives across monitor, log, mlops, ops-analysis
- Candidates:
  - `barChart`
  - `lineChart`
  - `chartLegend`
  - dimension filter/table helpers
- Why they are candidates:
  - Multiple apps have same-named chart wrappers and similar configuration surfaces
- Cross-app comparison:
  - Shared visual grammar exists
  - Data shape and interaction behavior still vary by domain
- Decision:
  - Do not merge blindly
  - First split out framework-level chart primitives where props are stable
  - `chart-with-sidebar-legend` and `time-series-composed-chart` now cover two validated shells; continue harvesting only similarly stable layers
  - Keep domain-specific query and aggregation wrappers local
- Migration path:
  - Build small chart surface primitives first
  - Replace repeated local legend, tooltip, empty-state, and dimension-selector wrappers
- Storybook plan:
  - Keep stories in `Framework/Charts/*`
  - Exclude domain dashboards from Storybook

### 2. Metric summary widget family
- Candidates:
  - `comSingle`
  - `comGauge`
  - `comTopN`
  - `comStateTimeline`
- Why they are candidates:
  - They all occupy the same dashboard summary tier and already share `ChartSurface` as a base shell
  - `log` and `ops-analysis` both render single-metric summary widgets, and ops-analysis adds adjacent gauge/topN/state-history patterns that look related at the page level
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `log/comSingle` is a lightweight centered value card with gradient styling and responsive typography
  - `ops-analysis/comSingle` adds compare text, value mappings, sparkline, and threshold-driven color logic
  - `comGauge`, `comTopN`, and `comStateTimeline` share summary-card placement but diverge strongly in semantics, layout density, and auxiliary visuals
- Decision:
  - Do not merge yet
  - Current evidence supports shared low-level surfaces like `ChartSurface`, but not a stable higher-level summary-widget abstraction
  - Revisit only if we see the same compare/meta/sparkline shell repeated across multiple apps or multiple widget types
- Migration path:
  - Keep auditing for repeated summary-card shells instead of forcing one abstraction from non-isomorphic widgets
  - Prefer extracting smaller typography, compare-indicator, or sparkline primitives if those repeat independently
- Storybook plan:
  - No new shared story yet
  - If a stable summary shell emerges, introduce it under `Framework/Charts/*` before migrating dashboard widgets

### 3. Pie distribution widgets across log and ops-analysis
- Candidates:
  - `log/comPie`
  - `ops-analysis/comPie`
  - thin wrappers such as `fileIntegrityPie`
- Why they are candidates:
  - Both apps render center-labeled categorical distribution charts with a shared sidebar-legend shell
  - At a glance they look similar enough that a forced merge would be tempting
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `log/comPie` supports `displayMaps` remapping, legend-driven selection wiring, localized legend title, and an automatic fallback to horizontal bar mode when category count grows
  - `ops-analysis/comPie` stays pie-only, uses a different theme token model and legend presentation, and does not carry the bar fallback behavior
  - `fileIntegrityPie` is only a thin log wrapper over `log/comPie`, not evidence of an independent shared contract
- Decision:
  - Do not merge yet
  - Keep `ChartWithSidebarLegend` and `ChartLegend` as the shared framework layers, but leave the higher-level pie distribution widgets app-local until behavior converges
  - Avoid introducing a misleading shared pie API that would either hide log-only bar fallback or overfit ops-analysis styling concerns
- Migration path:
  - No new shared component in this round
  - Continue watching for repeated category-to-bar fallback logic or repeated center-total donut behavior before extracting another layer
- Storybook plan:
  - No new shared Storybook artifact yet
  - If convergence appears later, add the shared abstraction under `Framework/Charts/*` first and migrate app widgets afterward

## Guardrails

- No new shared component without either:
  - cross-app repetition, or
  - strong evidence it is a stable shell/framework primitive
- No app-private page composite in Storybook
- No duplicate shared component with different APIs for the same job
- Business components may stay domain-specific, but must not depend on hidden app-only contracts once shared

    - external-app tag rendering in:

- Reason:
  - these surfaces were expressing the same origin semantics with different markup and different visual treatment:
    - Ant Design blue pill tag
    - Ant Design green pill tag
    - gray inline `(Built-in)` text
  - the semantics are stable across apps even though the surrounding business contexts differ
  - leaving them fragmented would keep a visible inconsistency in one of the repo's repeated metadata cues
- Cross-app comparison:
  - the true shared concept is not "blue tag" in general
  - it is specifically source origin:
    - built-in
    - external
  - repo search after the audit shows remaining blue/green tags are mostly other semantics:
    - event status
    - unified-filter metadata
    - generic card tags
  - that means source-origin is a valid family boundary, while "all colored tags" would be an over-abstraction
- Decision:
  - create a narrow shared component for source-origin semantics only:
    - pill mode for compact badges
    - inline mode for lighter tree/sidebar contexts
  - do not merge unrelated status/category tags into this family
