  - this slice is taxonomy convergence only; no duplicate component implementation exists and no new abstraction is needed
- Decision:
  - keep one canonical subtree for the CMDB CustomReporting badge family:
    - `Business/CMDB/CustomReporting/*`
  - do not duplicate the story under both the old standalone path and the family path
- Migration:
  - retitled story:
    - [src/stories/custom-reporting-mode-badge.stories.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/stories/custom-reporting-mode-badge.stories.tsx)

### CustomReportingReviewStatusSummary

  - they also duplicated the same localization keys, the same badge ordering, and the same neutral total badge styling
  - this is stable business semantics, not an incidental visual grouping

    - display semantics do not differ

### CustomReportingTargetModelValue
- Candidate:

    - [src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/customReporting/components/taskTable.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/customReporting/components/taskTable.tsx)

- Reason:
  - each location repeated the same business rule:
    - quick mode prefers `quick_model.model_name`
    - then falls back to `quick_model.model_id`
    - standard mode falls back to `model_id`
    - unresolved values render `--`
  - this is not just a string selection detail; it is the domain contract for how a CustomReporting task identifies its target model across list, detail, and review contexts
- Cross-app comparison:
  - all three call sites belong to the same CustomReporting workflow but represent different UI containers:
    - table cell
    - detail descriptions
    - drawer descriptions
  - difference analysis:
    - data source object is always `task.config`
    - surrounding layout differs, but the value-resolution semantics do not
  - no broader cross-domain reuse evidence exists yet, so this should stay a business component instead of becoming a generic field-value helper
- Decision:
  - extract one shared business display component:
    - [src/components/custom-reporting-target-model-value/index.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/custom-reporting-target-model-value/index.tsx)
  - keep the API narrow around `CustomReportingTaskConfig`

### LogMessagePreview
- Candidate:

    - MySQL slow log SQL preview
    - MySQL error log message preview
    - Redis log message preview
- Reason:
  - each implementation repeated the same behavior:
    - truncate long text inline
    - reveal full content in a tooltip

  - the only differences were preview length, monospace styling, and tooltip width
  - this is a stable log-analysis display contract, not one-off widget markup

### Shared K8s collector install API removed obsolete installContent override
- Candidate:
  - the shared business components:
    - [src/components/k8s-collector-install/index.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/k8s-collector-install/index.tsx)
    - [src/components/k8s-collector-install-step/index.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/k8s-collector-install-step/index.tsx)
  - still exposed an `installContent` escape hatch that let callers replace the governed command display shell
- Reason:
  - after converging CMDB back onto the shared command surface, no live app usage still needed that override
  - keeping the prop would leave a sanctioned escape hatch for future divergence and make Storybook examples contradict the intended contract
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor, log, and cmdb now all rely on the same generated-command display path
  - difference analysis:
    - callers still need `installActions` to render generate/regenerate controls
    - callers no longer need to replace the command reader itself
- Decision:
  - remove `installContent` from the shared API
  - keep `installActions` as the only extension point for command-generation controls
- Migration:
  - deleted `installContent` from both shared component prop interfaces and wiring
  - updated Storybook generated-command scenarios to reflect the governed shell instead of the legacy textarea override
- Storybook:
  - Storybook is now aligned with the live shared contract, rather than preserving a deprecated branch

  - the local serving-endpoint row in

  - the split `url` + `method` detail rows in
    [src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/detail/page.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/detail/page.tsx)
  - the local API info textarea + copy affordance in
    [src/app/opspilot/components/chatflow/components/nodeConfigs/ApiInfoNodeConfig.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/app/opspilot/components/chatflow/components/nodeConfigs/ApiInfoNodeConfig.tsx)
- Reason:
  - both apps implemented the same semantic unit: an HTTP request endpoint surface composed of a method badge, endpoint text, and copy affordance
  - this repeated UI pattern is small but meaningful, and leaving it duplicated would keep shared endpoint behavior and styling drifting across apps
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `monitor` custom API access guide rendered a bordered endpoint row with `HttpMethodBadge`, monospace endpoint text, and a dedicated copy button
  - `mlops` serving guide rendered the same trio with different spacing and a custom badge color override
  - `alarm` integration detail exposed the same endpoint information as two adjacent detail rows (`url` and `method`), which made the same request target harder to scan than the monitor/mlops variants
  - `opspilot` API info node config rendered the same endpoint target with a textarea shell and an overlaid copy icon
  - difference analysis:
    - monitor wants single-line truncation by default
    - mlops wants wrapped endpoint text inside a softer panel treatment
    - alarm wants the endpoint embedded inside a broader descriptions-based detail section
    - opspilot wants a compact inline card treatment inside a node settings panel
    - all four differences fit as visual props or surrounding layout choices instead of separate endpoint components
- Decision:
  - introduce one thin shared component:
    - [src/components/http-endpoint-display/index.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/http-endpoint-display/index.tsx)
  - keep the abstraction narrowly scoped to the repeated endpoint-display contract; do not fold in surrounding section titles, cards, or request examples
- Migration:
  - replaced the local endpoint row in `monitor/.../accessGuide/index.tsx` with `HttpEndpointDisplay`
  - replaced the local endpoint row in `mlops/components/ServingGuideDrawer.tsx` with `HttpEndpointDisplay`
  - replaced the separate `url` and `method` rows in `alarm/(pages)/integration/detail/page.tsx` with a single `endpoint` row powered by `HttpEndpointDisplay`
  - replaced the local textarea + overlay-copy pattern in `opspilot/.../ApiInfoNodeConfig.tsx` with `HttpEndpointDisplay`
  - preserved:
    - shared copy interaction through the central copy hook
    - per-surface copy success messaging where the original surface already exposed a specific success toast
    - per-app visual overrides such as multiline endpoint wrapping and badge color tuning
    - the surrounding business-specific guide/detail layout in each app
- Storybook:

    [src/stories/http-endpoint-display.stories.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/stories/http-endpoint-display.stories.tsx)
  - story coverage includes:

    - long multiline endpoint wrapping

### CopyableDetailList rich-value contract hardened and Alarm integration guide converged onto it
- Candidate:
  - the shared framework primitive
    [src/components/copyable-detail-list/index.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/copyable-detail-list/index.tsx)
    had already become the de facto detail-row contract across Alarm, CMDB, OpsPilot, and System Manager
  - meanwhile
    [src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/detail/page.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/detail/page.tsx)
    still kept its guide panels on local single-column `Descriptions` shells
- Reason:
  - several current consumers already pass rich `displayValue` nodes such as `StructuredDataPreview`, badge groups, status summaries, and inline semantic layouts
  - the old implementation still wrapped every value in `Typography.Text`, which is fine for strings but not a clean contract for block or structured content
  - leaving Alarm integration detail on local `Descriptions` would preserve another app-specific detail shell even though the page already depends on shared display primitives like `HttpEndpointDisplay`, `SecretValueDisplay`, `CodeSnippet`, and `StructuredDataPreview`
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `alarm/components/k8sGuide/index.tsx` already uses `CopyableDetailList` with mixed monospace/helper-text rich values
  - `cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/customReporting/components/taskDetail.tsx` and `batchReviewDrawer.tsx` already use it with badge groups, summaries, and `StructuredDataPreview`
  - `components/alarm-detail-drawer/index.tsx` already uses it for structured enrichment payload previews
  - difference analysis:
    - the shared pattern is stable: fixed label column, one semantic value slot per row, optional copy affordance
    - the real variance is whether the value slot is plain text or richer block content
- Decision:
  - reuse and harden the existing shared component instead of introducing another detail-shell abstraction
  - keep the existing API stable, but treat `displayValue` as a first-class rich-content slot
- Migration:
  - updated `CopyableDetailList` to:
    - render custom `displayValue` content in a block-safe wrapper
    - align rows to the top so multiline and structured values sit correctly
    - keep plain-text rows on the existing text path
  - replaced the local `Descriptions` guide shells in `alarm/(pages)/integration/detail/page.tsx` with `CopyableDetailList` for:
    - secret display
    - credentials/examples
    - base info
    - event field mapping
    - event field descriptions
  - preserved:
    - nested copy behavior where `CodeSnippet` and `HttpEndpointDisplay` already own copying
    - non-copyable row behavior where the original `Descriptions` surface exposed no copy action
    - the existing card structure and guide flow
- Storybook:
  - updated
    [src/stories/copyable-detail-list.stories.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/stories/copyable-detail-list.stories.tsx)
    with a rich-display story so Storybook remains the source of truth for the expanded shared contract

### Monitor trap message panel converged onto shared CopyableDetailList
- Candidate:
  - the local bordered `Descriptions` message panel in
    [src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/alert/information.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/alert/information.tsx)
    that renders SNMP trap payload rows from `trapData`
- Reason:
  - this panel is another dynamic key/value detail surface driven by runtime data rather than page-specific layout semantics
  - leaving it on local `Descriptions` would keep a Monitor-only variant of a pattern already standardized elsewhere as shared detail rows
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `alarm/(pages)/integration/detail/page.tsx` now uses `CopyableDetailList` for event field mappings and descriptions generated from dynamic object entries
  - `monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/flow/accessGuide.tsx` already uses `CopyableDetailList` for flat access details
  - `system-manager/components/security/operationLogs.tsx` and CustomReporting detail surfaces also use it for non-copyable informational rows
  - difference analysis:
    - the old Monitor panel used a two-column `Descriptions` layout
    - the underlying semantics still match the shared contract better than a Monitor-only presentation shell: one runtime label plus one value per row, no page-local behavior, no custom interaction
    - every row in this panel should remain non-copyable, matching the original behavior
- Decision:
  - reuse the existing shared detail-row contract:
    - [src/components/copyable-detail-list/index.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/components/copyable-detail-list/index.tsx)
  - accept convergence from the old two-column `Descriptions` style into the governed single-column detail-row style so Monitor aligns with the broader cross-app system
- Migration:
  - replaced the local trap-data `Descriptions` block with `CopyableDetailList`
  - preserved:
    - the existing runtime extraction logic for array and scalar trap values
    - placeholder fallback to `--`
    - non-copyable behavior for every row
    - the surrounding page structure and chart branch unchanged
- Storybook:
  - no new shared component was introduced
  - no story updates were needed because this slice only migrates another app surface onto the existing `CopyableDetailList` contract

### Shared copy interaction contract rolled out to remaining CMDB and OpsPilot local clipboard handlers
- Candidate:
  - the remaining page-local clipboard implementations in:
    - [src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/baseInfo/list.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/baseInfo/list.tsx)
    - [src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/configFiles/page.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/configFiles/page.tsx)
    - [src/app/opspilot/components/custom-chat-sse/RepairCommandsCard.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/app/opspilot/components/custom-chat-sse/RepairCommandsCard.tsx)
- Reason:
  - these surfaces still used direct `navigator.clipboard.writeText(...)` and page-local success messaging even though a shared copy interaction contract already exists in `useCopy`
  - leaving them local preserved duplicated clipboard fallback logic and inconsistent success-message behavior across apps
- Cross-app comparison:
  - the governed copy interaction contract is already used by:
    - `components/code-snippet/index.tsx`
    - `components/http-endpoint-display/index.tsx`
    - `components/secret-value-display/index.tsx`
    - `components/copyable-detail-list/index.tsx`
    - `system-manager`, `job`, `alarm`, `log`, and `node-manager` surfaces
  - difference analysis:
    - the remaining local handlers did not introduce unique business behavior
    - they only varied in the success message text and the source value being copied
  - that makes them a fit for shared interaction reuse rather than another app-local helper
- Decision:
  - reuse the existing shared hook contract:
    - [src/hooks/useCopy.ts](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/hooks/useCopy.ts)
  - keep page-level success text where needed via `successMessage`, but remove local clipboard implementations
- Migration:
  - replaced the local copy handler in `assetData/detail/baseInfo/list.tsx` with `useCopy`
  - replaced the config-file content copy handler in `assetData/detail/configFiles/page.tsx` with `useCopy`
  - replaced the OpsPilot repair-command bulk copy handler with `useCopy`
  - preserved:
    - existing success-message wording for CMDB and OpsPilot
    - the copied value sources and user-facing triggers
    - current page structure and business flow
- Storybook:
  - no Storybook update was needed because this slice converges onto an existing shared hook, not a visual component contract

### DataDisplay family overview updated to reflect the current governed shared surface
- Candidate:
  - the Storybook family entry
    [src/stories/data-display-family.stories.tsx](/Users/qiu/projects/bk-lite/.worktrees/web-component-governance/web/src/stories/data-display-family.stories.tsx)
    was still emphasizing older table/entity/badge primitives while omitting the newer shared detail and payload-display contracts that recent governance work has converged onto
- Reason:
  - Storybook is the component system's contract center, so its family overview should reflect what the current framework surface actually is
  - after recent migrations, key framework data-display primitives now include:
    - `CopyableDetailList`
    - `HttpEndpointDisplay`
    - `SecretValueDisplay`
    - `StructuredDataPreview`
  - leaving the family overview unchanged made those governed components harder to discover and weakened Storybook's role as the canonical index
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `CopyableDetailList` now governs detail rows across Alarm, Monitor, CMDB, OpsPilot, and System Manager
  - `HttpEndpointDisplay` now governs endpoint presentation across Monitor, Alarm, MLOps, and OpsPilot
  - `StructuredDataPreview` governs structured payload inspection across Alarm, CMDB, OpsPilot, and other detail surfaces
  - `SecretValueDisplay` is part of the same shared detail-display language in integration and credential flows
  - difference analysis:
    - these are framework-level display contracts, not app-specific business widgets
    - the missing piece was Storybook organization, not another component abstraction
- Decision:
  - keep the existing framework/business split
  - strengthen the `Framework/DataDisplay/FamilyOverview` story so it showcases the actual governed framework primitives instead of leaving recent shared contracts scattered across isolated leaf stories
- Migration:
  - added a new “Detail rows and payload previews” section to `data-display-family.stories.tsx`
  - showcased `CopyableDetailList`, `HttpEndpointDisplay`, `SecretValueDisplay`, and `StructuredDataPreview` together in one shared framework surface
  - preserved the existing table/entity/badge examples so the family overview still spans the broader data-display system
- Storybook:
  - updated the existing framework family overview instead of creating a parallel grouping story
  - this keeps the framework taxonomy cleaner and avoids adding another near-duplicate “overview” entry

  - the root leaf stories also still repeated the `OpsPilot` prefix inside the `Business/OpsPilot/*` subtree, which made the taxonomy noisier than other converged domains

  - `OpspilotAvailabilityBadge` is reused in entity-card browsing and studio settings
  - `OpspilotConfigSeverityBadge` is reused across config-analysis and diff-report surfaces
  - `ApprovalCard` is reused across chatflow execution preview and custom chat execution surfaces
  - difference analysis:

    - the shell, compact status semantics, and approval interaction contract remain stable

  - keep the child family subtrees (`Cards`, `Scheduler`, `ToolEditor`) as dedicated governed areas beneath the same domain
  - normalize the remaining root leaf stories to concise semantic names

    - `Business/OpsPilot/ApprovalCard`

  - this gives OpsPilot one governed top-level contract center while preserving the deeper child-family structure

  - `EventAlertInformationSummary` remains a business-scoped event-detail summary contract reused across monitor and log alert detail flows

    - both ownership boundaries stay unchanged
    - the inconsistency was only the Storybook leaf naming
- Decision:
  - keep both components in their current business scopes
  - normalize the leaf titles to concise contract names
- Migration:
  - retitled the existing leaf stories under:
    - `Business/Integrations/SNMP/GuidePanel`
    - `Business/Events/InformationSummary`
- Storybook:
  - no new story file added
  - no runtime component API changed
  - both singleton leaves now read more like governed contract surfaces and less like raw component class names

### Execution status badge leaf title normalized
- Candidate:
  - the execution domain currently exposes one governed shared business leaf:
    - `Business/Execution/ExecutionStatusBadge`
- Reason:
  - this was a Storybook taxonomy cleanup rather than a runtime abstraction change
  - the `Execution` parent path already carries the business context, so repeating `Execution` in the leaf added little value compared with the cleaner naming now used across other singleton business leaves
  - `TopMenu` under `Business/AppShell` was left unchanged in this slice because that label is already compact and semantically clear
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `ExecutionStatusBadge` is already the shared status grammar reused across Job, OpsPilot, MLOps, and other execution-adjacent surfaces
  - the underlying contract remains one stable business badge for run-state semantics, not several domain-specific variants
  - difference analysis:
    - the runtime ownership and usage surface stay unchanged
    - the inconsistency was only the verbose Storybook leaf naming
- Decision:
  - keep the component in `Business/Execution`
  - normalize the leaf title to the concise contract name:
    - `StatusBadge`
- Migration:
  - retitled the existing leaf story under:
    - `Business/Execution/StatusBadge`
- Storybook:
  - no new story file added
  - no runtime component API changed
  - the execution status contract now matches the same compact naming rule used by other converged singleton business leaves

### OpsAnalysis metric field selector leaf title normalized

  - adjacent config leaves already rely on concise contract-oriented names such as `GaugeSettingsSection`, `ThresholdColorConfigSection`, and `ValueFormatConfigSection`

    - the only inconsistency was the implementation-shaped Storybook leaf naming
- Decision:
  - keep the existing `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/SectionsOverview`
  - normalize the leaf title to the concise contract name:
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/MetricFieldSelector`

    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/MetricFieldSelector`

  - no new story file added
  - no runtime component API changed
  - the OpsAnalysis config subtree now reads more consistently as a set of governed configuration contracts

### OpsAnalysis config section leaves normalized
- Candidate:
  - the `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/*` subtree still had several leaves whose Storybook titles exposed layout implementation details:
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/ThresholdColorConfigSection`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/ValueFormatConfigSection`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/ValueMappingsConfigSection`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/GaugeSettingsSection`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/SingleValueSettingsSection`
- Reason:
  - this is a taxonomy convergence pass, not a runtime component split
  - inside the `Config` subtree, Storybook should govern the stable business configuration contracts, while `Section` only describes that the current render happens to be a section-shaped slice of a larger editor
- Cross-app comparison:
  - these stories all point at the shared OpsAnalysis config-section family that already powers multiple widget configuration flows in the same governed surface
  - the same subtree now already uses concise contract-oriented naming for adjacent leaves such as:
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/MetricFieldSelector`
  - difference analysis:
    - the components, exports, and ownership stay the same
    - the inconsistency was the implementation-shaped Storybook leaf naming across a cluster of related config leaves
- Decision:
  - keep the existing `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/SectionsOverview`
  - normalize the leaf titles to contract-first names:
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/ThresholdColorConfig`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/ValueFormatConfig`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/ValueMappingsConfig`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/GaugeSettings`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Config/SingleValueSettings`
- Migration:
  - retitled the existing stories in place under the normalized leaves above
  - no component consumers need code migration because this slice changes Storybook contract labeling only
- Storybook:
  - no new story files added
  - no runtime component API changed
  - the OpsAnalysis config subtree now reads as one consistent set of governed business configuration contracts instead of a mix of contracts and internal layout nouns

### Page header shell extracted as framework contract
- Candidate:
  - multiple business surfaces carried their own page-header skeletons with the same structural job:
    - title block
    - optional back affordance
    - optional subtitle or trailing actions
  - two concrete implementations already existed in different business domains:
    - `JobPageHeader`
    - `DashboardPageHeader`
- Reason:
  - this is a cross-app framework pattern rather than a business-only visual coincidence
  - keeping separate app-local header shells would continue the drift the user asked to stop, especially as more pages add title, back, and action rows with similar spacing and surface treatment
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `JobPageHeader` is the compact job-workspace variant with title, optional description, and optional back button
  - `DashboardPageHeader` is the monitor-detail variant with title, mode switch, time controls, and back action
  - difference analysis:
    - action payloads differ by business flow
    - typography level and trailing control density differ
    - the shared invariant is the header shell layout: content block on the left, optional assistive leading element, optional secondary row, and an optional action cluster on the right
- Decision:
  - extract the shared layout skeleton to:
    - `Framework/Layout/PageScaffold/HeaderShell`
  - keep the business wrappers:
    - `Business/Job/PageHeader`
    - `Business/Monitor/Widgets/PageHeader`
  - this preserves stable business contracts while removing duplicated framework structure from app-local components
- Migration:
  - `JobPageHeader` now renders through the shared shell
  - `DashboardPageHeader` now renders through the shared shell
  - follow-up migrations can move any future page-header variants to the same shell without changing their Storybook business entry points
- Storybook:
  - added a new framework contract story:
    - `Framework/Layout/PageScaffold/HeaderShell`
  - existing business stories remain the source of truth for their domain-specific variants

### Import modal shell reused by integration Excel import
- Candidate:
  - the repository already had a governed import container contract:
    - `Business/Imports/ModalShell`
  - but the integration Excel import flow still rebuilt the same shell locally with:
    - `OperateModal`
    - `SingleFileUploadPanel`
    - confirm/cancel footer wiring
- Reason:
  - this is a real cross-app workflow shell, not a one-off coincidence
  - leaving the integration Excel flow on a bespoke modal composition would keep import behavior fragmented even though the repo already has a shared import shell used by monitor and cmdb import surfaces
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `ImportFileModalShell` already owns the common import-modal structure: modal chrome, upload panel, and action footer
  - `IntegrationExcelImportModal` adds business-specific parsing, validation, and a template-download action
  - difference analysis:
    - the Excel import workflow has richer upload processing and an extra template action
    - the shell structure is the same import contract, so the business logic should sit above the shared shell instead of rebuilding it
- Decision:
  - keep `ImportFileModalShell` as the shared business import-shell contract under:
    - `Business/Imports/ModalShell`
  - extend it only where real reuse demanded it:
    - `width`
    - upload `maxCount`
  - migrate `IntegrationExcelImportModal` to consume the shared shell instead of composing its own modal/upload/footer stack
- Migration:
  - `IntegrationExcelImportModal` now renders through `ImportFileModalShell`
  - its Excel-specific parsing, validation, and template export remain local
  - future import modals with the same structure should start from `ImportFileModalShell` first
- Storybook:
  - no new business story was needed because the import shell already exists in Storybook
  - the shared contract behind `Business/Integrations/Editing/ExcelImportModal` is now aligned with the existing `Business/Imports/ModalShell` source of truth

### Knowledge QA pair upload modal aligned to import shell
- Candidate:
  - the knowledge document flow had an app-local upload modal:
    - `QAPairUploadModal`
  - its runtime structure matched the same import workflow shell already reused in other domains:
    - modal chrome
    - single-file upload panel
    - confirm/cancel action contract
- Reason:
  - this is another true cross-app pattern, not a knowledge-only visual accident
  - keeping the knowledge modal on its own `OperateModal + SingleFileUploadPanel` composition would continue the same duplication that was just removed from integration Excel import
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `QAPairUploadModal` adds knowledge-specific template download actions, file-status mapping, and import gating while uploads are in progress
  - `ImportFileModalShell` already owns the shared import-modal structure used by monitor, cmdb, and now integration Excel import flows
  - difference analysis:
    - the business logic and template choices remain knowledge-specific
    - the shell responsibilities are identical, so the app-local modal should sit on top of the governed import shell instead of rebuilding it
- Decision:
  - extend `ImportFileModalShell` just enough to cover a real existing requirement:
    - `uploadProps.onRemove`
  - migrate `QAPairUploadModal` to consume the shared shell
  - keep the business contract in the knowledge subtree:
    - `Business/Knowledge/QAPairUploadModal`
- Migration:
  - `QAPairUploadModal` now renders through `ImportFileModalShell`
  - template links, upload gating, and file-status behavior remain local to the knowledge workflow
- Storybook:
  - added a dedicated business story:
    - `Business/Knowledge/QAPairUploadModal`
  - the knowledge upload flow now has an explicit Storybook contract while sharing the same governed import shell used by other apps

### Job playbook upload modal extracted from page and aligned to import shell
- Candidate:
  - the job playbook library still kept its upload modal inline inside the page implementation
  - that inline surface repeated the same governed upload-modal skeleton already shared elsewhere:
    - modal shell
    - single-file upload panel
    - confirm/cancel footer
- Reason:
  - this was both a component-governance gap and a Storybook gap
  - leaving the upload flow embedded in page code made the job contract harder to reuse, harder to compare across apps, and invisible to Storybook as a first-class governed business component
- Cross-app comparison:
  - the playbook upload flow adds business-specific metadata fields such as version and organization ownership, plus a template download action
  - the underlying shell responsibilities match the same import workflow pattern already reused by cmdb, monitor, integration Excel import, and knowledge QA pair upload
  - difference analysis:
    - business fields and validation remain job-specific
    - the container and upload semantics are shared, so the job flow should compose the existing import shell instead of rebuilding it inline
- Decision:
  - keep the business contract under `Business/Job`
  - extract the page-local modal into:
    - `JobPlaybookUploadModal`
  - align its runtime shell to `ImportFileModalShell`
  - extend the shared shell with one real reuse requirement:
    - configurable footer button order via `primaryFirst`
- Migration:
  - the playbook library page now renders `JobPlaybookUploadModal`
  - upload validation, template download, version input, and organization selection remain in the job-specific component
  - future job upload flows can reuse this business component or drop to the shared import shell when they do not need the extra metadata fields
- Storybook:
  - added a dedicated business story:
    - `Business/Job/PlaybookUploadModal`
  - the upload flow is now part of the governed Job component surface instead of living only inside page code

### OpsPilot skill import modal extracted from page and aligned to import shell
- Candidate:
  - the OpsPilot tool page still kept its skill-package import modal inline inside page code
  - that modal repeated the same governed import structure already used across other domains:
    - modal shell
    - single-file upload surface
    - confirm/cancel action contract
- Reason:
  - the remaining differences were business-specific copy and an upload-intro block, not a fundamentally different UI pattern
  - leaving it inline would keep a reusable ToolEditor business flow outside Storybook and outside the shared import-shell governance path
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `SkillImportModal` needs a richer explanatory block before upload because the skill package format has contract details around `SKILL.md`, optional manifests, and derived metadata
  - `ImportFileModalShell` already governs the shared upload-modal behavior reused by imports, knowledge QA upload, integration Excel import, and job playbook upload
  - difference analysis:
    - the explanatory copy remains OpsPilot-specific
    - the shared shell contract is still the right container, so the business modal should compose it rather than hand-roll another page-local modal
- Decision:
  - extract the page-local modal into the ToolEditor business family:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/ToolEditor/SkillImportModal`
  - extend `ImportFileModalShell` only where a real business variant required it:
    - `beforeUploadPanel`
  - keep file parsing/import behavior local to the OpsPilot page workflow
- Migration:
  - the OpsPilot tool page now renders `SkillImportModal`
  - file-list ownership and import action stay in the page for now
  - future ToolEditor import flows can reuse this business component or compose the underlying import shell when they only need the container contract
- Storybook:
  - added a dedicated business story:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/ToolEditor/SkillImportModal`
  - the ToolEditor subtree now includes the governed skill-import business flow instead of only badges, empty states, and sidebars

### Job playbook upgrade modal extracted from page and aligned to import shell
- Candidate:
  - the job playbook library still kept its version-upgrade modal inline inside the page implementation
  - that modal repeated the same upload-shell structure as the governed job upload flow:
    - upload container
    - confirm/cancel footer
    - archive validation
- Reason:
  - the upgrade flow is a stable business capability in the same family as playbook upload, not page glue
  - keeping it inline would leave the Job component system with only the create/upload path governed while the parallel upgrade path continued to drift inside page code
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `JobPlaybookUpgradeModal` shares the same archive-upload mechanics and shared import shell as `JobPlaybookUploadModal`
  - it differs by adding:
    - current-version readout
    - upgrade warning
    - version override field
  - difference analysis:
    - these additions are business metadata layered around the same upload-shell contract
    - no separate framework shell is justified
- Decision:
  - keep the contract in `Business/Job`
  - extract the page-local upgrade flow into:
    - `JobPlaybookUpgradeModal`
  - reuse `ImportFileModalShell` as the shared upload container
- Migration:
  - the playbook library page now renders `JobPlaybookUpgradeModal`
  - archive validation, version placeholder derivation, and upgrade submit behavior remain in the page-owned business flow
  - future job upgrade surfaces can reuse this business component directly
- Storybook:
  - added a dedicated business story:
    - `Business/Job/PlaybookUpgradeModal`
  - the Job subtree now documents both upload and upgrade playbook flows as governed business contracts

### Job and OpsPilot family overviews strengthened with governed upload flows
- Candidate:
  - Storybook already had leaf stories for the newly extracted business modals:
    - `Business/Job/PlaybookUploadModal`
    - `Business/Job/PlaybookUpgradeModal`
    - `Business/OpsPilot/ToolEditor/SkillImportModal`
  - but the existing family overviews still described only the older shell and status surfaces
- Reason:
  - once a business family has governed modal flows, leaving them out of `FamilyOverview` weakens Storybook as the contract center because users must already know the leaf names to discover the workflow surfaces
- Cross-app comparison:
  - the `Job` family now includes two parallel playbook archive flows that share one upload-shell lineage
  - the `OpsPilot/ToolEditor` family now includes a concrete skill-import workflow on top of the same import-shell lineage
  - difference analysis:
    - the business semantics differ by domain
    - the governance need is identical: each family overview should expose the active contract surface, not just lower-level primitives
- Decision:
  - strengthen the existing family overviews rather than creating more overview stories:
    - `Business/Job/FamilyOverview`
    - `Business/OpsPilot/ToolEditor/FamilyOverview`
- Migration:
  - the Job family overview now showcases both playbook upload and playbook upgrade flows
  - the OpsPilot ToolEditor family overview now includes the governed skill-import modal
- Storybook:
  - no new family-level story files added
  - updated the existing family overviews so Storybook reflects the current governed business workflows, not only isolated leaf components

### MLOps family overview added while dataset upload stays business-scoped
- Candidate:
  - Storybook still had no root-level `Business/MLOps/FamilyOverview`
  - the only explicit MLOps business contract in Storybook was:
    - `Business/MLOps/Algorithm/TypeBadge`
  - I also reviewed the dataset upload modal under:
    - `src/app/mlops/components/algorithm-detail/uploadModal.tsx`
- Reason:
  - the MLOps upload flow is real and important, but it is not a clean fit for the generic import shell that now powers knowledge, integrations, job, and OpsPilot import surfaces
  - forcing reuse here would over-abstract across materially different behavior: multi-image packaging, algorithm-specific accepted file types, train/val/test labeling, and image-name validation
- Cross-app comparison:
  - current import-shell consumers mostly differ in copy, footer extras, and light metadata fields
  - the MLOps upload modal differs in runtime behavior:
    - switches file handling by algorithm type
    - may zip many image files before upload
    - adds dataset split semantics through checkbox state
    - validates image naming rules before submission
  - difference analysis:
    - this is still a business component candidate
    - but the right next boundary is an MLOps-scoped upload component, not another forced consumer of `ImportFileModalShell`
- Decision:
  - do not migrate the current MLOps upload modal onto the shared import shell
  - add a root business overview:
    - `Business/MLOps/FamilyOverview`
  - keep the current upload modal business-scoped until a stable MLOps upload contract is extracted
- Migration:
  - no runtime component moved in this slice
  - the next migration step for MLOps should extract a dedicated business upload component from the current app-local modal once its API boundary is defined
- Storybook:
  - added one new family overview:
    - `Business/MLOps/FamilyOverview`
  - this gives the MLOps subtree a proper contract entry point now, while honestly recording that dataset upload has not yet been standardized into a reusable business component

### MLOps dataset upload modal promoted to a governed business component
- Candidate:
  - the dataset upload flow lived only under:
    - `src/app/mlops/components/algorithm-detail/uploadModal.tsx`
  - it was already a reusable business modal within the MLOps dataset-detail flow, but it had no Storybook contract and no stable component-home outside the page directory
- Reason:
  - the earlier audit showed it should not be forced into the generic import shell
  - but leaving it only in `app/` code still violated the governance goal because an important business component had no explicit contract surface
- Cross-app comparison:
  - unlike the generic import-shell consumers, this modal owns:
    - algorithm-type-specific accepted file rules
    - train/validate/test labeling
    - multi-image packaging into ZIP
    - image filename validation
  - difference analysis:
    - these runtime behaviors remain MLOps-specific
    - the right move is therefore to promote it to a business component, not to flatten it into a framework/shared import primitive
- Decision:
  - extract the runtime component into:
    - `Business/MLOps/DatasetUploadModal`
  - keep the old app path as a thin re-export so existing page imports stay stable
  - keep the component business-scoped under MLOps instead of shared/framework scope
- Migration:
  - `AlgorithmDetail` continues to consume the modal through its existing import path, now backed by the governed business component
  - future MLOps dataset upload surfaces can reuse the same component directly from `src/components`
- Storybook:
  - added a dedicated business story:
    - `Business/MLOps/DatasetUploadModal`
  - strengthened `Business/MLOps/FamilyOverview` so the MLOps subtree now surfaces both algorithm taxonomy and the governed dataset-upload flow

### MLOps dataset release modal promoted to a governed business component
- Candidate:
  - the dataset release publishing flow lived only under:
    - `src/app/mlops/components/algorithm-detail/DatasetReleaseModal.tsx`
  - it is reused inside the dataset release drawer flow, but had no Storybook contract and no stable component-home outside the page-local MLOps detail directory
- Reason:
  - it is not a framework modal and should not be flattened into a generic publish primitive
  - but it does represent a real business-owned contract:
    - semantic version input
    - train/validation/test split selection
    - dataset-release submission semantics
  - that makes it a strong `Business/MLOps` candidate rather than another app-local leaf
- Cross-app comparison:
  - compared with the existing MLOps model `ReleaseModal`, both are publish flows under the same product family
  - difference analysis:
    - model release publishing depends on train jobs, model versions, team ownership, and serving metadata
    - dataset release publishing depends on dataset split files and dataset-version packaging
    - the family resemblance is structural, not enough to justify a fake shared publish abstraction
- Decision:
  - promote the dataset release flow into:
    - `Business/MLOps/DatasetReleaseModal`
  - keep it business-scoped, with optional injected file-fetch and submit hooks so Storybook can govern the contract without live API coupling
  - keep the old app path as a thin re-export for local stability
- Migration:
  - `DatasetReleaseList` now consumes the governed component from `src/components`
  - the old `app/mlops` modal path remains as a thin compatibility layer
  - no attempt was made to merge this component with model release publishing because the business payloads still diverge materially
- Storybook:
  - added a dedicated story:
    - `Business/MLOps/DatasetReleaseModal`
  - updated:
    - `Business/MLOps/FamilyOverview`
  - the MLOps family overview now exposes both upload and release workflow contracts, making Storybook the visible source of truth for both governed dataset lifecycle surfaces

### CMDB credential pool editor promoted to a governed business component
- Candidate:
  - the credential-pool editor lived only under:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/credentialPoolEditor.tsx`
  - it was already reused across multiple CMDB discovery task forms:
    - host
    - sql
    - snmp
    - config_file
    - cloud
    - vm
    - ipmi
- Reason:
  - this is a real business workflow surface, not a one-off page helper
  - it owns the shared card grammar for discovery credentials:
    - ordered fallback credentials
    - masked secret previews
    - shape-specific input sets
    - drag-sort behavior for retry order
  - leaving it inside one page subtree made Storybook incomplete and kept the CMDB discovery forms vulnerable to local style drift
- Cross-app comparison:
  - I checked other secret/password surfaces such as team secret management and password-policy modals
  - difference analysis:
    - those flows share lower-level secret-display and password-form primitives
    - the credential-pool editor is more specific: it models discovery credentials, fallback order, and collector-shape differences
    - that makes it a `Business/CMDB` component, not a framework secret-input primitive
- Decision:
  - extract the editor into:
    - `Business/CMDB/CredentialPoolEditor`
  - keep the former app-local path as a thin re-export for compatibility
  - migrate the CMDB discovery task forms to import the governed component directly
- Migration:
  - updated task-form consumers to use the governed component import:
    - `hostTask`
    - `sqlTask`
    - `snmpTask`
    - `configFileTask`
    - `cloudTask`
    - `vmTask`
    - `ipmiTask`
  - preserved the existing form-layout integration by keeping a stable `credentialPoolEditor` root class alongside the component-scoped module styles
- Storybook:
  - added:
    - `Business/CMDB/CredentialPoolEditor`
  - updated:
    - `Business/CMDB/FamilyOverview`
  - Storybook now exposes the CMDB discovery credential workflow as an explicit governed contract instead of leaving it implicit inside page-only forms

### NodeManager collector package modal promoted to a governed business component
- Candidate:
  - the package-management modal lived under:
    - `src/app/node-manager/components/sidecar/collectorModal.tsx`
  - and was consumed by multiple NodeManager package surfaces:
    - collector list
    - controller list
    - package detail views
- Reason:
  - it already acts as the shared package workflow contract for add, edit, and upload states
  - but keeping it inside one `sidecar` subtree made Storybook blind to an important package-management surface and obscured that multiple pages were already depending on it
- Cross-app comparison:
  - I also checked the separate modal at:
    - `src/app/node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/node/collectorOperation/collectorModal.tsx`
  - difference analysis:
    - the sidecar modal handles package metadata and upload lifecycle
    - the collector-operation modal handles node-side install/start/stop/restart flows
    - they share the product family and modal grammar, but their payloads and user intent are materially different
- Decision:
  - promote only the sidecar package modal into:
    - `Business/NodeManager/CollectorPackageModal`
  - do not merge it with the node-operation modal into a fake generic collector modal
  - keep the old sidecar path as a thin re-export while migrating direct consumers to the governed component import
- Migration:
  - updated direct consumers in NodeManager package-management flows to import the governed component
  - left the node-operation modal in place because it represents a different business workflow
- Storybook:
  - added:
    - `Business/NodeManager/CollectorPackageModal`
  - updated:
    - `Business/NodeManager/FamilyOverview`
  - Storybook now surfaces the package-management contract alongside the existing detail shell and runtime badges

### CMDB subscription drawer entered Storybook as an explicit business contract
- Candidate:
  - the subscription-management drawer already lived as a shared business component:
    - `src/app/cmdb/components/subscription/subscriptionDrawer.tsx`
  - and it is reused by at least two separate CMDB surfaces:
    - asset list
    - asset detail
- Reason:
  - this was not an extraction problem anymore; it was a governance gap
  - the drawer already represented a stable business contract:
    - rule search
    - rule list
    - enable/disable toggles
    - create/edit entry point
  - but Storybook still did not expose it, which meant one of the repo’s real shared CMDB workflows had no visible contract source of truth
- Cross-app comparison:
  - compared with other shared CMDB components already in Storybook, the subscription drawer sits at a broader workflow layer rather than a badge/value leaf
  - difference analysis:
    - it should stay business-scoped under CMDB
    - it did not need another extraction pass
    - it did need a controllable Storybook boundary so the workflow can be reviewed without live API coupling
- Decision:
  - keep `SubscriptionDrawer` where it already lives as a shared business component
  - add optional controller injection props so Storybook can render the rule-management contract with deterministic data
  - export `UserInfoContext` for controlled Storybook provider setup
- Migration:
  - no page consumer changed behavior
  - the runtime drawer API remains compatible
  - Storybook and family overview now render the shared drawer through injected controllers rather than production hooks
- Storybook:
  - added:
    - `Business/CMDB/SubscriptionDrawer`
  - updated:
    - `Business/CMDB/FamilyOverview`
  - this closes a governance hole where a genuinely shared CMDB workflow existed in production but not in Storybook

### Knowledge graph detail drawers promoted to governed business components
- Candidate:
  - the node and edge detail drawers lived only under:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/NodeDetailDrawer.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/EdgeDetailDrawer.tsx`
  - they were already reused across multiple knowledge entry points:

  - keeping them only in the `app/opspilot` subtree left shared knowledge-graph behavior under-governed and invisible to Storybook
- Cross-app comparison:
  - compared with the existing knowledge badge components already in Storybook, these drawers operate at a workflow/detail-view layer instead of a leaf status/value layer
  - difference analysis:
    - they should remain business-scoped under Knowledge
    - they are not framework drawers, because the payload language is specific to knowledge-graph entities and relations
- Decision:
  - extract them into:
    - `Business/Knowledge/NodeDetailDrawer`
    - `Business/Knowledge/EdgeDetailDrawer`
  - keep the old app paths as thin re-export compatibility layers
  - migrate direct consumers to the governed component imports

  - updated knowledge graph and testing consumers to import the governed components from `src/components`

  - added:

    - `Business/Knowledge/FamilyOverview`
  - Storybook now exposes graph detail behavior as a first-class knowledge contract rather than only badge-level semantics

### Knowledge QA drawers promoted to governed business components
- Candidate:
  - the QA edit/detail drawers lived only under:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/qaEditDrawer.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/qaDetailDrawer.tsx`
  - they were already reused across multiple knowledge authoring and review flows:
    - custom QA creation
    - QA result review
- Reason:
  - these are stable workflow components with clear business semantics:
    - create/edit a QA pair
    - inspect, update, and delete a QA pair
    - optionally inspect the source chunk behind a QA pair
  - leaving them buried inside the `app/opspilot` tree kept an active knowledge workflow outside Storybook’s source-of-truth boundary
- Cross-app comparison:
  - compared with the graph detail drawers, these are another knowledge-detail workflow family
  - difference analysis:
    - they remain business-scoped under Knowledge
    - they should not be flattened into a generic edit/detail drawer abstraction because the payload language and interaction model are QA-specific
- Decision:
  - extract them into:
    - `Business/Knowledge/QAEditDrawer`
    - `Business/Knowledge/QADetailDrawer`
  - keep the former app-local files as thin compatibility layers
  - add optional chunk-detail injection so Storybook can exercise the detail drawer without live API coupling
- Migration:
  - updated the custom QA and QA result flows to import the governed components from `src/components`
  - preserved compatibility shims for any remaining app-local imports
- Storybook:
  - added:
    - `Business/Knowledge/QAEditDrawer`
    - `Business/Knowledge/QADetailDrawer`
  - updated:
    - `Business/Knowledge/FamilyOverview`
  - the knowledge subtree now covers both graph-detail and QA-detail workflows as governed Storybook contracts

### OpsPilot knowledge-base selection workflow promoted to governed business components
- Candidate:
  - the knowledge-base selector and its selection modal lived only under:

    - `src/app/opspilot/components/skill/operateModal.tsx`
  - they were already reused across multiple skill-authoring surfaces:
    - skill rule editing
    - skill settings
    - the selection modal is also reused by the tool selector flow
- Reason:

    - choose one or more business entities
    - review the selected set in a compact surface with edit/remove actions
    - optionally tune retrieval scores
  - leaving it only inside `app/opspilot/components/skill` kept a stable multi-surface workflow outside Storybook and obscured the modal contract shared by knowledge and tool selection
- Cross-app comparison:
  - compared with the existing OpsPilot family stories, this workflow operates at a configuration/authoring layer rather than a detail-shell or status-badge layer
  - difference analysis:
    - the selector modal is shared within the OpsPilot skill-authoring domain
    - the knowledge-base selector is business-scoped under OpsPilot, not a framework primitive
- Decision:
  - extract the shared selector modal into:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/SelectorOperateModal`
  - extract the knowledge selector into:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/KnowledgeBaseSelector`
  - keep app-local files as thin compatibility re-exports
  - migrate direct consumers to the governed component imports
- Migration:
  - updated:
    - skill rule editing
    - skill settings
    - tool selector now imports the governed selector modal
  - preserved compatibility shims for remaining app-local imports
- Storybook:
  - added:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/SelectorOperateModal`
    - `Business/OpsPilot/KnowledgeBaseSelector`
  - updated:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/FamilyOverview`
  - Storybook now exposes the shared skill knowledge-routing workflow instead of leaving it implicit inside page-local skill editors

### CMDB subscription workflow moved under governed business-component ownership
- Candidate:
  - the CMDB subscription flow had already entered Storybook, but its runtime implementation still lived under:
    - `src/app/cmdb/components/subscription/subscriptionDrawer.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/components/subscription/subscriptionRuleForm.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/components/subscription/subscriptionRuleList.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/components/subscription/instanceSelector.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/components/subscription/recipientSelector.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/components/subscription/triggerTypeConfig.tsx`
  - the same workflow is consumed by:
    - CMDB asset list quick subscribe
    - CMDB asset detail quick subscribe
    - Storybook contract coverage
- Reason:
  - this is a single, stable business workflow rather than scattered page glue:
    - browse existing rules
    - create or edit a rule
    - scope target instances or conditions
    - configure trigger semantics
    - select recipients and channels
  - leaving the implementation under `src/app` while Storybook treated it as shared created a contract-ownership split, which weakens Storybook as the source of truth
- Cross-app comparison:
  - there is no evidence yet that this should become a framework primitive across unrelated domains
  - the repeated pattern is specifically inside CMDB subscription management, so the right boundary is a governed business family, not a generic drawer/form abstraction
  - difference analysis:
    - `SubscriptionDrawer` is the orchestration shell
    - `SubscriptionRuleForm`, `InstanceSelector`, `RecipientSelector`, and `TriggerTypeConfig` are tightly coupled business subcomponents inside the same flow
- Decision:
  - promote the whole workflow into `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/`
  - keep app-local compatibility re-exports for the legacy import paths
  - migrate direct consumers and Storybook stories to the governed `src/components` imports
- Migration:
  - moved runtime ownership to:
    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/subscriptionRuleForm.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/subscriptionRuleList.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/instanceSelector.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/recipientSelector.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/triggerTypeConfig.tsx`
  - updated direct consumers:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/baseInfo/page.tsx`
    - `src/stories/cmdb-subscription-drawer.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/cmdb-family.stories.tsx`
  - preserved compatibility shims:
    - `src/app/cmdb/components/subscription/subscriptionDrawer.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/components/subscription/subscriptionRuleForm.tsx`
- Storybook:
  - no story removed
  - existing governed contract remains:
    - `Business/CMDB/SubscriptionDrawer`
    - `Business/CMDB/FamilyOverview`
  - the difference now is ownership alignment: Storybook and runtime source both point at the shared business-component family

### Multi-action overlay footers converged on the shared framework contract
- Candidate:
  - several overlays and form footers still hand-authored the same multi-action row instead of consuming the governed footer contract:
    - CMDB subscription rule modal
    - CMDB quick-subscribe modal from asset list/detail
    - Job dangerous rule add/edit modal
    - Job cron task create/edit page footer
  - the existing framework contract already lived in:
    - `src/components/modal-action-footer/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/modal-action-footer.stories.tsx`
- Reason:
  - these surfaces all express the same interaction pattern:
    - one primary commit action
    - one alternate save path
    - one cancel/close escape hatch
  - before this pass, each surface rebuilt spacing, ordering, and button emphasis locally
  - that causes visual drift across apps even when the semantic contract is identical
- Cross-app comparison:
  - CMDB subscription uses:
    - `Save & Enable`
    - `Save Only`
    - `Cancel`
  - Job dangerous-rule and cron-task flows use the same three-part action semantics with different layout placement
  - difference analysis:
    - CMDB and cron-task prefer `primary -> alternate -> cancel`
    - dangerous-rule keeps `cancel -> alternate -> primary`
    - both are still one framework pattern: a governed multi-action footer with explicit ordering
- Decision:
  - extend `ModalActionFooter` instead of minting app-specific footer wrappers
  - add a small ordering control for alternate actions:
    - `secondaryActionsPosition="beforeConfirm" | "afterConfirm"`
  - keep business flows responsible only for labels, loading, and callbacks
- Migration:
  - updated framework contract:
    - `src/components/modal-action-footer/index.tsx`
  - migrated consumers:
    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/baseInfo/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/components/dangerous-rule-page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/cron-task/create/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/cron-task/edit/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/shieldStrategy/components/operateModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertEnrichment/components/operateModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertAssign/components/operateModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/settings/namespace/operateModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/settings/dataSource/operateModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/dashBoard/components/viewConfig.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/target/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/correlationRules/components/operateModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/modifyKnowledge.tsx`
    - `src/components/top-menu/user-info/userInformation.tsx`
- Storybook:
  - updated:
    - `Framework/Overlays/ModalActionFooter`
  - added explicit alternate-save documentation through the `WithAlternateSavePath` story so Storybook now captures both:
    - standard confirm/cancel flows
    - governed `Save & Enable / Save Only / Cancel` workflows

### Compact list-and-panel empty states converged on the shared framework contract
- Candidate:
  - several list or side-panel surfaces still rendered raw Ant Design simple empty states even though the repo already had a governed compact contract:
    - `src/components/tree-selector-panel/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/top-menu/notifications.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/incidents/detail/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/incidents/components/ganttChart/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/page.tsx`
  - the existing framework contract already lived in:
    - `src/components/compact-empty-state/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/compact-empty-state.stories.tsx`
- Reason:
  - these surfaces all express the same low-emphasis UI state:
    - a bounded list, tree, timeline, or panel has no current rows
    - the empty state should stay compact and non-heroic
  - leaving them on raw `Empty.PRESENTED_IMAGE_SIMPLE` keeps spacing, copy weight, and visual density inconsistent across apps
- Cross-app comparison:
  - true matches:
    - tree selector with no nodes
    - notification popover with no messages
    - incident timeline with no change records
    - gantt area with no incidents to render
    - CMDB left-side model tree with no filtered results
  - intentionally excluded in this pass:
    - full-page or business-explanatory empty states such as `notDeployed`, unsupported capabilities, or domain-specific onboarding hints
    - chart-empty states, because they already have a separate chart-focused framework contract
- Decision:
  - migrate only the compact no-data surfaces to `CompactEmptyState`
  - keep larger business-message empties on their own business contracts
- Migration:
  - migrated:
    - `src/components/tree-selector-panel/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/top-menu/notifications.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/incidents/detail/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/incidents/components/ganttChart/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/page.tsx`
- Storybook:
  - no new story added
  - existing governed contract remains:
    - `Framework/Feedback/CompactEmptyState`
  - this pass increases real runtime adoption so Storybook more accurately reflects the actual empty-state system in use

### Shared component fallbacks and selector/list empties aligned with CompactEmptyState
- Candidate:
  - several shared components still rendered raw Ant Design `Empty` for the same compact no-data scenario:

  - `entity-list` needed a compact “no results” card-grid empty state
  - `integration-config-edit-modal` needed a compact “no configurable fields” fallback
  - difference analysis:
    - all three are bounded panel/list surfaces
    - none of them require a full-page, chart-specific, or domain-explanatory empty state
- Decision:
  - move these shared fallbacks onto `CompactEmptyState`
  - keep specialized business empties such as chart surfaces and tool-editor-specific states separate
- Migration:
  - migrated:
    - `src/components/content-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/entity-list/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/integration-config-edit-modal/index.tsx`
- Storybook:
  - no new story added
  - existing contract remains:
    - `Framework/Feedback/CompactEmptyState`
  - this tightens the shared runtime base so more downstream screens inherit the governed empty-state contract automatically

### Storybook family overviews now expose the governed component taxonomy
- Candidate:
  - several newly-governed business components already had dedicated stories, but the domain `FamilyOverview` entries did not surface them:
    - `src/stories/cmdb-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/job-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/knowledge-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/opspilot-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/monitor-family.stories.tsx`
- Reason:
  - Storybook is our required contract center, so having isolated component stories without domain-level discoverability still leaves the taxonomy incomplete
  - reviewers should be able to enter a business domain overview and see both framework-level language and governed business contracts together
- Cross-app comparison:
  - CMDB gained collection-detail and visualization empties
  - Job gained playbook detail empties
  - Knowledge gained graph empties
  - OpsPilot gained provider-management empties
  - Monitor gained integration config states
  - difference analysis:
    - each domain has its own business semantics
    - all of them now fit the same Storybook information architecture pattern:
      - domain family overview
      - focused business component story
      - underlying framework primitives where appropriate
- Decision:
  - do not create more wrapper components here
  - instead, promote the existing governed components into the correct domain family overview stories so Storybook categories stay clean and complete
- Migration:
  - updated:
    - `src/stories/cmdb-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/job-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/knowledge-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/opspilot-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/monitor-family.stories.tsx`
- Storybook:
  - Family overview stories now explicitly include preview sections for the governed business components above
  - this keeps `Business/<Domain>/FamilyOverview` aligned with the dedicated component stories and avoids “hidden shared components” that only exist as isolated leaf entries

### Integration onboarding shells audit stayed converged with framework/business boundaries
- Candidate:
  - remaining cross-app onboarding files that still look similar at a glance:
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/automatic.tsx`
    - `src/app/log/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/automatic.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/k8s/accessConfig.tsx`
    - `src/app/log/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/k8s/accessConfig.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/k8s/collectorInstall.tsx`
    - `src/app/log/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/k8s/collectorInstall.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/k8s/collectorInstall.tsx`
- Reason:
  - these files are the highest-risk place for accidental duplicate framework components because monitor, log, and cmdb all expose similar onboarding flows
  - the governance requirement here is strict:
    - shared shells must live in Storybook as the contract source
    - app-local files should remain only as business compositions over those shells
- Cross-app comparison:
  - automatic configuration pages in monitor and log both render the same shared workspace shell:
    - `src/components/integration-automatic-configuration-shell/index.tsx`
  - monitor/log K8s access pages both render the same shared access-step shell:
    - `src/components/integration-k8s-access-config-shell/index.tsx`
  - monitor/log/cmdb collector-install pages all render the same shared install-step shell:
    - `src/components/k8s-collector-install-step/index.tsx`
  - difference analysis:
    - monitor and log automatic configuration pages diverge in data contracts, table-column generation, import semantics, and submit behavior
    - monitor and log K8s access pages diverge in extra business fields:
      - monitor adds upload interval controls
      - log adds runtime profile and path controls
    - cmdb collector install diverges in command generation and reporting APIs
    - the repeated structure is already abstracted; the remaining differences are business semantics, not framework duplication
- Decision:
  - no new shared component is needed in this slice
  - keep the app-local onboarding pages as business components
  - keep the shells as the governed shared contracts
  - align Storybook ownership with that decision by moving the automatic configuration shell into framework layout taxonomy
- Migration:
  - updated:
    - `src/stories/integration-automatic-configuration-shell.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/layout-family.stories.tsx`
  - no runtime component code changed
- Storybook:
  - moved:
    - `Business/Integrations/Editing/AutomaticConfigurationShell` -> `Framework/Layout/ConfigurationWorkspace`
  - family overview now documents `ConfigurationWorkspace` as part of the stable framework layout tree

### Integration onboarding structural primitives re-aligned with framework taxonomy
- Candidate:
  - the two onboarding leaves that had already been reclassified in governance reasoning, but whose Storybook titles were still parked under business onboarding paths:
    - `src/components/integration-step-callout/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/integration-setting-row/index.tsx`
- Reason:
  - both components are structural primitives rather than integration-domain contracts:
    - `IntegrationStepCallout` owns titled informational layout plus optional bullet guidance
    - `IntegrationSettingRow` owns the repeated `control + description` row pattern
  - leaving them under `Business/Integrations/Onboarding/*` would keep Storybook out of sync with the actual governance boundary and invite more app-local copies of the same shell behavior
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `IntegrationStepCallout` is reused in monitor and log K8s onboarding flows through copy presets
  - `IntegrationSettingRow` is reused across monitor flow onboarding, monitor K8s onboarding, log K8s onboarding, and shared K8s asset field composition
  - difference analysis:
    - app-specific variance is entirely in children, copy, and surrounding business workflow
    - the shared UI contract is the layout shell itself
- Decision:
  - keep both as shared framework components
  - keep business onboarding family overviews focused on actual flow contracts such as K8s shells, access fields, install steps, and completion states
- Migration:
  - updated:
    - `src/stories/integration-step-callout.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/integration-setting-row.stories.tsx`
  - no runtime component code changed

  - moved:

    - `Business/Integrations/Onboarding/SettingRow` -> `Framework/Layout/SettingRow`

### Async config overlay contract re-aligned with framework overlays taxonomy
- Candidate:
  - `src/components/integration-config-edit-modal/index.tsx`

  - governance had already settled that this component is a shared async overlay shell, but the focused story title had drifted back into business integration editing taxonomy
  - that drift is costly because it makes a generic async load/render/submit overlay look like an integration-only modal, which increases the chance of duplicate config-edit shells appearing elsewhere
- Cross-app comparison:
  - `IntegrationConfigEditModal` is still reused by monitor and log config editing flows
  - the business variance stays in schema loading, copy, and submit handlers
  - the stable contract is the overlay shell behavior:
    - async load
    - conditional empty state
    - configurable form body
    - submit lifecycle
- Decision:
  - keep the component in framework scope
  - keep the composed integrations family overview in business scope, but label the trigger copy so it is clear this modal is a framework-governed support shell

  - no runtime component code changed

### K8s family overview re-aligned with framework leaf ownership
- Candidate:
  - `src/stories/k8s-family.stories.tsx`
- Reason:
  - after the onboarding and completion leaf contracts moved into framework taxonomy, the K8s family overview still needed the same ownership clarification we applied to the broader integrations onboarding family
  - without that note, the family page could still make the terminal success surface and shared onboarding guidance look like K8s-owned leaf components instead of business composition over shared framework contracts
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor and log share the same K8s access-step shell and configuration-flow shell
  - monitor, log, and CMDB all compose the same terminal success/result surface through `IntegrationAccessComplete`
  - the business-specific layer is the K8s workflow and troubleshooting semantics, not the underlying content/layout/result primitives
- Decision:
  - keep `Business/Integrations/K8s/FamilyOverview` in business scope
  - keep focused leaf ownership split across:
    - `Business/Integrations/K8s/*` for K8s-specific shells and install/troubleshooting contracts
    - `Framework/Feedback/CompletionResult` for the terminal result shell
  - make that boundary explicit in the family overview copy instead of duplicating leaf ownership
- Migration:
  - updated:
    - `src/stories/k8s-family.stories.tsx`
  - no runtime component code changed
- Storybook:
  - K8s family overview now explicitly explains that it documents a business onboarding journey composed from both K8s-scoped contracts and shared framework leaf stories

    - editable table field wiring
    - bottom action row
  - leaving that page outside the shell would keep one more app-local copy of the same governed workspace composition alive
- Cross-app comparison:
  - monitor and log automatic configuration pages already render through `IntegrationAutomaticConfigurationShell`
  - node-manager controller install now shares the same structural contract:
    - top configuration fields
    - monitored-object install table
    - import and batch actions
    - primary confirm action
  - difference analysis:
    - node-manager adds OS / architecture / installation-method fields and a cancel action
    - monitor/log keep integration-specific plugin or collect-type fields
    - these are payload differences inside one shared workspace shell, not a separate layout contract
- Decision:
  - migrate node-manager controller install onto `ConfigurationWorkspace`
  - extend the shell only where a real existing consumer needed it:
    - optional secondary actions
    - optional confirm disabled state
    - nullable permission gate for flows that already manage access outside the shell
- Migration:
  - updated:
    - `src/components/integration-automatic-configuration-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/node/controllerInstall/installConfig/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/integration-automatic-configuration-shell.stories.tsx`
- Storybook:
  - no new story file added
  - updated the existing `Framework/Layout/ConfigurationWorkspace` story to document the secondary-action variant used by node-manager controller install
## 2026-07-01 Import file shell taxonomy realignment

- candidate component
  - `ImportFileModalShell`
- why it was reviewed
  - it is already reused across:
    - `cmdb` instance import
    - `cmdb` model-config import
    - `opspilot` QA pair upload
  - its implementation is framework-shaped:
    - `OperateFormModal` chrome
    - `SingleFileUploadPanel` body
    - consumer-supplied validation, upload state, and follow-up actions
  - the previous Storybook placement under `Business/Imports/ModalShell` made the ownership boundary look app-specific even though the runtime contract is shared and generic
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - single-file upload inside a governed modal shell
    - consumer-owned submit handler
    - consumer-owned template/help affordances
  - differing business behavior:
    - `cmdb` instance/model imports use Excel templates and API submission
    - `opspilot` QA upload supports JSON/CSV templates and domain-specific file preprocessing
    - `ops-analysis` import modal remains intentionally separate because it is a three-step business workflow with precheck and result stages, not a simple upload shell
- governance decision
  - classify `ImportFileModalShell` as a framework component
  - Storybook source of truth moves to:
    - `Framework/Forms/Upload/ImportFileModalShell`
  - keep `ops-analysis/components/importExport/importModal.tsx` as business-owned
    - it composes upload primitives but does not match the shared shell boundary
- migration and cleanup
  - align consumer usage so template/download affordances consistently flow through `afterUploadPanel` rather than mixed placement patterns
  - updated consumers:
    - `cmdb/(pages)/assetData/list/importInst.tsx`
    - `cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/management/list/importModelConfigModal.tsx`
    - `opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/detail/documents/components/QAPairUploadModal.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - keep the dedicated component story, now under the framework upload taxonomy
  - add the shell to `Framework/Forms/Upload/FamilyOverview` so upload surfaces show:
    - primitive panel
    - compatibility wrappers
    - governed modal import shell
## 2026-07-01 Side navigation extension hooks converged into framework menu shell

- candidate component
  - `components/layout/sub-layout/side-menu`
- why it was reviewed
  - `cmdb/(pages)/assetData/components/asset-data-layout/side-menu.tsx` was reimplementing framework side-navigation composition just to inject:
    - shortcut blocks before one menu item
    - relation blocks after one menu item
    - CMDB-specific active styling
  - the real reuse boundary was still framework side-navigation, not a CMDB-only shell
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - intro area
    - governed side-nav shell
    - active-item handling
    - parameter-preserving href generation
  - app-specific variation:
    - CMDB asset data needs `renderBeforeItem` and `renderAfterItem` hooks for relation shortcuts
    - CMDB asset data needs local style classes and nested click behavior for relation chips
- governance decision
  - extend framework `SideMenu` with pass-through extension hooks that already exist at the lower `SideMenuNavList` layer:
    - `renderBeforeItem`
    - `renderAfterItem`
    - style-class overrides for shell and list surfaces
  - do not create another shared side-menu variant
- migration
  - `asset-data-layout/side-menu.tsx` now renders through shared `SideMenu`
  - removed direct app-level composition of `SideMenuShell` + `SideMenuNavList`
- Storybook plan
  - add a dedicated framework story for `SideMenu`:
    - `Framework/Layout/SideNavigation/Menu`
  - demonstrate extension blocks so future app teams reuse the framework shell first instead of cloning it
