## 2026-07-01 Job playbook detail workflow converged on a governed business component

- candidate component
  - `JobPlaybookDetailDrawer`
- why it was reviewed
  - the remaining `job` audit surfaced one real repeated business pattern across two separate pages:
    - `execution/job-record/page.tsx`
    - `template/playbook-library/page.tsx`
  - both pages had near-identical playbook detail drawers:
    - same title + version presentation
    - same basic info / params / file list / README tabs
    - same empty states
    - same file-preview entry point from the file tree
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - tabbed playbook detail drawer body
    - summary fields for metadata
    - parameter table contract
    - file tree with preview affordance
    - README rendering contract
  - difference analysis:
    - `job-record` adds execution-context metadata such as executed version
    - `playbook-library` adds header actions for download and upgrade
    - both differences are extension points on the same business component, not separate components
  - non-candidate findings from the same audit:
    - `job` cron create/edit pages use page-level workspace footers, not overlay-shell duplication
    - `node-manager` installing guidance and uninstall flows are workflow surfaces with branch-heavy behavior, so they stay business-owned for now
- governance decision
  - extract one job-domain business shared component instead of leaving two app-local copies
  - keep it under business taxonomy, not framework taxonomy, because the semantics are playbook-specific
  - do not abstract the surrounding file preview modal in this pass; only the repeated drawer contract moved
- migration
  - added:
    - `src/components/job-playbook-detail-drawer/index.tsx`
  - updated:
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/job-record/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/template/playbook-library/page.tsx`
- Storybook plan

  - Storybook source of truth now lives under:
    - `Business/Job/PlaybookDetailDrawer`

## 2026-07-01 Log and monitor grouping modals converged on shared modal contract

- candidate component
  - `OperateFormModal`
- why they were reviewed
  - the remaining `log` and `monitor` hits in this slice included two older grouping-rule editors still hand-composing raw modal footer chrome:
    - `log/(pages)/integration/grouping/editInstance.tsx`
    - `monitor/(pages)/integration/group/ruleModal.tsx`
  - both already matched the governed confirm/cancel modal behavior used elsewhere
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - standard modal shell
    - title + confirm/cancel actions
    - loading state on confirm
    - body content with domain-specific condition editors
  - difference analysis:
    - `log` edits stream grouping rules with `field/op/value` conditions and term selection
    - `monitor` edits grouping rules with metric-aware `name/method/value` conditions and monitor-object context
    - the repeated boundary is still the modal shell contract, not a unified grouping-rule business component yet
  - non-candidate findings from the same audit:
    - `alarm` settings drawers already largely converge on `OperateFormDrawer`; the remaining correlation rule editor is a heavier business workflow
    - `monitor/(pages)/integration/asset/templateConfigDrawer.tsx` is a business workspace drawer with plugin/config-navigation behavior, so it stays business-owned for now
- governance decision
  - converge both modals onto `OperateFormModal`
  - keep the grouping-rule editors themselves local until a stronger cross-page business contract emerges
  - avoid creating another premature framework or business abstraction from only partially aligned rule-editing semantics
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/log/(pages)/integration/grouping/editInstance.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/group/ruleModal.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new story file required
  - existing `Framework/Feedback/OperateFormModal` stories remain the source of truth for this shell contract

## 2026-07-01 OpsPilot knowledge document selector converged on a governed business component

- candidate component
  - `KnowledgeDocumentSelectorDrawer`
- why it was reviewed
  - the OpsPilot knowledge audit found a repeated document-selection workflow across two separate editing surfaces:
    - `knowledge/detail/documents/modify/qaPairForm.tsx`
    - `knowledge/detail/documents/graph/edit/page.tsx`
  - both pages had near-identical drawers for:
    - choosing documents by source tab
    - paginating through a document table
    - keeping a temporary selection set before confirm
    - reviewing selected documents in a right-side pending panel
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - large content drawer shell with governed confirm/cancel footer
    - fixed file / web_page / manual document tabs
    - left-side selection table
    - right-side pending-document list with clear/remove actions
    - apply-on-confirm behavior
  - difference analysis:
    - QA pair generation shows chunk-size-oriented table columns and only the document-type badge in the pending list
    - knowledge graph editing shows status-oriented table columns and adds processing status badges in the pending list
    - these differences are presentational extensions on the same business workflow, not separate components
  - non-candidate findings from the same audit:
    - `testing/page.tsx` and `preprocessStep.tsx` share only a lightweight content-preview drawer state, which is still too small and local to justify a new business component right now
- governance decision
  - extract one knowledge-domain business shared component for document selection
  - keep it in business taxonomy rather than framework taxonomy because the interaction is tightly coupled to knowledge-document semantics
  - reuse `ContentFormDrawer` underneath instead of creating another bespoke drawer shell
- migration

    - `src/components/knowledge-document-selector-drawer/index.tsx`
  - updated:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/detail/documents/modify/qaPairForm.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/detail/documents/graph/edit/page.tsx`

  - Storybook source of truth now lives under:

## 2026-07-01 CMDB custom-reporting and auto-discovery drawers converged on shared drawer contracts

- candidate components
  - `ContentFormDrawer`
- why they were reviewed
  - the CMDB audit in this slice showed two different kinds of remaining drawers:
    - standard edit / selection / close-only drawers still hand-composing footer chrome

  - this pass targeted only the first group
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - `customReporting/components/taskWizard.tsx`
      is a standard content-form drawer with unsaved-change guarding, a governed confirm/cancel footer, and business-specific form content
    - `autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/baseTask.tsx`
      contains a standard selection drawer with confirm/cancel actions around a table-based asset picker
    - `autoDiscovery/collection/profess/page.tsx`
      contains two close-only detail drawers for node-management sync and task detail surfaces
  - difference analysis:
    - `taskWizard` keeps custom-reporting-specific model, identity-key, and cleanup-strategy logic local
    - `baseTask` keeps auto-discovery-specific asset selection and unsaved-selection guarding local
    - the two page-level detail drawers stay readonly domain surfaces, but their footer contract is still the shared close-only drawer pattern
  - non-candidate findings from the same audit:
    - `customReporting/taskDetail.tsx` and `batchReviewDrawer.tsx` form a business family, but they are not yet duplicate enough to justify another abstraction
    - `configFiles/components/contentDrawer.tsx` and `compareDrawer.tsx` are specialized file-content and diff viewers with distinct layout contracts, so they stay business-owned for now
- governance decision
  - converge the standard CMDB drawers onto `ContentFormDrawer`
  - keep custom-reporting review surfaces and config-file viewer surfaces local until a stronger repeated business contract emerges
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/customReporting/components/taskWizard.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/baseTask.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/page.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new story file required
  - existing `Framework/Feedback/ContentFormDrawer` stories remain the source of truth for these drawer contracts

## 2026-07-01 Ops analysis data-source controls converged into governed business components

- candidate components
  - `OpsAnalysisDataSourceSelect`
  - `OpsAnalysisDataSourceParamsConfig`
- why they were reviewed
  - the ops-analysis audit found the same data-source selection and parameter-rendering semantics reused in both dashboard view configuration and topology single-value node configuration
  - both pieces encode ops-analysis query semantics rather than generic form widgets
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - data-source option rendering with permission-aware disabled states
    - parameter form generation from `DatasourceItem.params`
    - support for string, number, boolean, date, and time-range query inputs
  - difference analysis:
    - consumers differ in surrounding layout, chart configuration, and follow-up behaviors after data-source changes
    - the selection and param rendering contracts themselves stay stable inside the ops-analysis domain
    - they do not belong at framework level because the API is shaped around ops-analysis datasource metadata
- governance decision
  - move both into governed business paths under `src/components`
  - keep the current runtime API intact so migration stays low-risk
  - add dedicated stories so Storybook becomes the visible contract instead of leaving behavior embedded inside page configuration flows
- migration
  - added:
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-data-source-select/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-data-source-params-config/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/ops-analysis-data-source-select.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/ops-analysis-data-source-params-config.stories.tsx`
  - updated:
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/topology/components/singleValueNodePanel.tsx`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/dashBoard/components/viewConfig.tsx`

    - `src/app/ops-analysis/components/dataSourceSelect.tsx`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/components/paramsConfig.tsx`

  - new business stories:
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/DataSource/Select`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/DataSource/ParamsConfig`

## 2026-07-01 App view fullscreen behavior moved into a governed framework layout primitive

  - `useAppViewFullscreen` with `AppViewFullscreenExit`

  - the ops-analysis audit found the same fullscreen interaction contract in architecture, dashboard, and topology views
  - the hook and exit affordance were already stable and detached from any one page's domain data
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - enter / exit fullscreen state handling
    - body and html scroll locking while fullscreen is active
    - Escape-key exit behavior
    - shared floating exit button
  - difference analysis:
    - consumers differ in the fullscreen content they render
    - the behavior contract itself is layout-level and domain-agnostic
- governance decision
  - promote the hook and exit affordance into `src/components/app-view-fullscreen`
  - document the interaction in Storybook under the framework layout taxonomy
  - keep page-specific fullscreen styling and content local to each consumer

  - difference analysis:

  - move the whole suite into `src/components/ops-analysis-unified-filter`

## 2026-07-01 Removed app-local wrapper components that duplicated shared component entrypoints

- candidate component
  - app-local wrapper entry files that only re-exported governed shared components
- why it was reviewed
  - the audit found several `src/app/**/components/*` files that no longer owned UI behavior and only forwarded imports into `src/components`
  - these wrappers create false component ownership inside app folders, make migration audits noisy, and weaken Storybook as the single source of truth because the same component appears to exist in two places
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - no local rendering logic
    - no app-specific props or styling
    - direct `export { default } from '@/components/...';` forwarding
  - difference analysis:
    - none at the UI-contract level; these files were path aliases, not true business variants
- governance decision
  - remove wrapper files when the shared component path is already the real contract and Storybook coverage already exists there
  - keep business components in `src/components`, not mirrored under app folders unless the app file still owns real domain behavior
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/mlops/components/algorithm-detail/AlgorithmDetail.tsx`
  - removed wrapper entry files:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/credentialPoolEditor.tsx`

    - `src/app/mlops/components/algorithm-detail/DatasetReleaseModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/mlops/components/algorithm-detail/uploadModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/dashboards/shared/widgets/index.ts`
    - `src/app/node-manager/components/sidecar/collectorModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/EdgeDetailDrawer.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/NodeDetailDrawer.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/qaDetailDrawer.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/qaEditDrawer.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/skill/knowledgeBaseSelector.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/skill/operateModal.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new stories required
  - existing governed stories remain the only visible contract for these shared business components

## 2026-07-01 Removed app-local widget runtime entrypoints that duplicated shared widget contracts

- candidate component
  - log-analysis and ops-analysis shared widget runtime entrypoints
- why it was reviewed
  - the audit found several `src/app/**/utils/*` files that looked like local chart helpers but actually only re-exported runtime helpers from governed shared widget packages
  - these files created a second import surface outside `src/components`, which weakens the idea that shared widget behavior lives behind one governed contract
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - `log` trend widgets imported `ChartDataTransformer` through `src/app/log/utils/chartDataTransform`
    - `ops-analysis` widget wrapper imported `ChartDataTransformer` and `getValueByPath` through app-local utility entrypoints
    - several ops-analysis utility files only forwarded exports from `src/components/ops-analysis-widgets` and `src/components/ops-analysis-config-sections`
  - difference analysis:
    - there was no app-specific behavior in these entrypoint files
    - the real implementation already belonged to the shared widget packages, so keeping app-local aliases only duplicated ownership
- governance decision
  - move consumers to the governed shared widget runtime/config-section entrypoints directly
  - delete app-local re-export files once they no longer own behavior
  - do not add new stories because widget behavior and visual variants stay unchanged; Storybook ownership already remains with the shared widget components

## 2026-07-01 Reduced app-local type and theme entrypoints that duplicated shared contracts

- candidate component
  - app-local type/theme entrypoints that only surfaced shared component contracts

  - the audit found several app-layer files still acting as type or theme aliases for shared components, even though the real ownership already lives in `src/components`
  - these aliases keep contract discovery split across app and shared layers, especially for menu configuration, chart theme resolution, search-combination field typing, and opspilot approval requests

    - opspilot execution flows consumed approval request types defined by `src/components/opspilot-cards/types`
  - difference analysis:
    - app-local files did not add new behavior to these shared contracts
    - local domain-specific types remained in place; only the duplicated shared aliases were removed
- governance decision
  - point consumers directly at the shared component type/runtime entrypoints
  - keep app-local files only for truly app-owned domain interfaces
  - remove the dedicated app-local chart theme alias once all consumers are migrated

## 2026-07-01 Moved shared monitor and ops-analysis runtime helpers out of app-local utility layers

- candidate component
  - shared chart/widget runtime helpers that were still implemented under app-local utility files
- why it was reviewed
  - the audit found governed shared components in `src/components` still importing foundational helper logic from `src/app`
  - this weakens Storybook-first ownership because the visible shared component contract depends on app-local runtime files instead of a shared package surface
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-widgets` and ops-analysis pages both relied on the same request/display time formatting rules
    - `src/components/monitor-line-chart`, monitor local chart siblings, and monitor dashboard helpers all relied on the same enum formatting, metric aggregation, time formatting, and random color helpers
  - difference analysis:
    - the helper behavior was identical across consumers
    - no app-specific branching justified keeping duplicate implementations inside `src/app/ops-analysis/utils/dateTime.ts` or inside the monitor app utility implementation surface
- governance decision
  - promote widget time formatting into `src/components/ops-analysis-widgets/date-time.ts`
  - promote monitor chart helper ownership into `src/components/monitor-chart-runtime`
  - keep `src/app/monitor/utils/common.tsx` as a compatibility facade for legacy monitor pages while the true implementation lives in the shared runtime package
  - remove the dedicated ops-analysis app-local time helper file once all consumers point to the shared widget runtime

  - removed app-local duplicate entrypoint:

  - no new stories required because this change keeps existing widget and chart behavior stable
  - existing stories under `src/stories/monitor-line-chart.stories.tsx` and the ops-analysis widget stories remain the verification center for these shared contracts

## 2026-07-01 Detached unified-filter shared components from app-local ops-analysis contracts

- candidate component
  - `src/components/ops-analysis-unified-filter` runtime and stories
- why it was reviewed
  - the unified-filter family had already been promoted into `src/components`, but its shared UI still depended on app-local ops-analysis types and helper functions
  - that meant the Storybook surface was not the real contract owner: the behavior still flowed through `src/app/ops-analysis/types/*` and `src/app/ops-analysis/utils/widgetDataTransform.ts`
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - filter bar, binding panel, config modal, and filter-options modal all consume the same filter definition model, bindable param rules, and input-mode normalization logic
    - dashboard view config and topology flows also consume the same binding helpers when wiring widget params to unified filters
  - difference analysis:
    - the shared UI only needs widget-level filter/data-source contracts plus a minimal layout shape that exposes `valueConfig.dataSource`
    - there was no app-only branching inside the binding helper logic; the behavior is shared across dashboard and topology workflows
- governance decision
  - move unified-filter helper ownership into `src/components/ops-analysis-unified-filter/runtime.ts`
  - keep `src/app/ops-analysis/utils/widgetDataTransform.ts` as a compatibility consumer/export surface for the broader request-building logic, not as the owner of shared unified-filter contracts
  - point Storybook stories at shared types so the stories verify the same contract the components actually own
- migration
  - added shared runtime entrypoint:
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-unified-filter/runtime.ts`
  - updated shared components:
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-unified-filter/filterBindingPanel.tsx`
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-unified-filter/filterOptionsModal.tsx`
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-unified-filter/unifiedFilterBar.tsx`
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-unified-filter/unifiedFilterConfigModal.tsx`
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-unified-filter/index.ts`
  - updated app consumers to use the shared unified-filter runtime:
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/dashBoard/components/viewConfig.tsx`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/dashBoard/hooks/useDashboardLayoutSync.ts`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/topology/hooks/useGraphNodeOperations.ts`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/topology/hooks/useNodeConfigFlow.ts`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/topology/hooks/useTopologyRefresh.ts`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/topology/hooks/useGraphData.ts`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/(pages)/view/topology/utils/namespaceUtils.ts`
    - `src/app/ops-analysis/utils/widgetDataTransform.ts`
  - updated Storybook contract fixtures to consume shared types:
    - `src/stories/ops-analysis-unified-filter-bar.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/ops-analysis-filter-binding-panel.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/ops-analysis-unified-filter-config-modal.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/ops-analysis-data-source-select.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/ops-analysis-data-source-params-config.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new stories were required because the visual contract already existed
  - the existing ops-analysis unified-filter and data-source stories now bind directly to shared component contracts instead of app-local type aliases, which makes Storybook the closer source of truth

## 2026-07-01 Moved CMDB subscription filter contract into component ownership

  - updated app runtime store:

- Storybook plan
  - no new stories required
  - existing `Business/CMDB/SubscriptionDrawer` stories now sit on a component-owned filter contract rather than a page-store-owned type

## 2026-07-01 Split MLOps dataset modals into governed business components plus runtime injection

  - `src/components/mlops-dataset-upload-modal`
  - `src/components/mlops-dataset-release-modal`

  - both dataset modals are already represented in Storybook as shared business components
  - the release modal had partial runtime injection, while the upload modal still imported app-local APIs directly, leaving the family with inconsistent governance boundaries
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - both modals expose the same imperative workflow boundary to pages and Storybook
    - both belong to the same dataset lifecycle family and should obey one ownership rule: component owns validation and UI flow, runtime container owns API wiring
  - difference analysis:
    - upload modal needed a new injected action contract
    - release modal already had the right shape and only needed the app-local fallback removed
- governance decision
  - make both modal components pure business components that receive runtime actions through props
  - move API selection back to MLOps page containers instead of leaving it inside Storybook-governed components
  - keep one component per workflow, without introducing duplicate wrapper components in multiple apps
- migration

    - `src/components/mlops-dataset-release-modal/index.tsx`

    - `src/stories/mlops-dataset-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/mlops-family.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new stories required
  - existing `Business/MLOps/Dataset/*` stories now exercise the governed components through injected runtime contracts rather than app-local API imports

## 2026-07-01 Split monitor chart unit-label runtime from governed chart components

- candidate component
  - `src/components/monitor-line-chart`
  - `src/components/monitor-lazy-metric-item`
- why it was reviewed
  - both components are already governed in Storybook but still reached into `app/monitor` runtime to resolve display units
  - the unit-label lookup is runtime context, while the chart and lazy-card rendering behavior belongs to the shared component system
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - Storybook uses both components without monitor page context
    - monitor pages, search views, and event detail surfaces all need the same unit-label resolution behavior when rendering chart values
  - difference analysis:
    - the governed components only need a resolver contract
    - monitor pages need the real unit resolver sourced from monitor context
    - non-monitor consumers such as Storybook or other modules can fall back to a pure shared formatter without monitor context
- governance decision
  - add a shared monitor unit-label helper under `src/components/monitor-shared`
  - make chart components accept an injected `resolveUnitLabel` contract instead of importing `useUnitTransform`
  - add monitor-side runtime wrappers that inject the resolver from `useUnitTransform` for page usage
- migration
  - added shared helper:
    - `src/components/monitor-shared/unit-label.ts`
  - updated governed business components:
    - `src/components/monitor-line-chart/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/monitor-lazy-metric-item/index.tsx`
  - added app runtime wrappers:
    - `src/app/monitor/components/monitorLineChartRuntime.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/components/monitorLazyMetricItemRuntime.tsx`
  - updated monitor page containers:
    - `src/app/monitor/components/metric-views/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/view/monitorView.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/strategy/detail/metricPreview.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/alert/information.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/view/detail/overview.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/search/page.tsx`
  - updated app compatibility helper:
    - `src/app/monitor/hooks/useUnitTransform.ts`
- Storybook plan
  - no new stories required
  - existing `Business/Monitor/Charts/LineChart` and `Business/Monitor/MetricViews/LazyMetricItem` stories continue using the governed components directly, now without hard dependency on monitor runtime context

## 2026-07-01 Split CMDB subscription runtime from the governed business workflow

- candidate component
  - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer`
  - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/subscriptionRuleForm.tsx`
  - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/instanceSelector.tsx`

  - asset list and asset detail pages share the same subscription workflow, but the governed business components still pulled model metadata, user data, instance search, channel APIs, and subscription hooks directly from `app/cmdb` and `app/system-manager`
  - this left the Storybook contract incomplete: the list drawer was mockable, but the form and nested selectors still depended on app runtime ownership
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - CMDB asset list and asset detail both reuse the same rule-management drawer and quick-subscribe form
    - Storybook and CMDB family overview both present the same workflow as a business-owned UI contract
  - difference analysis:
    - the governed workflow only needs explicit list/mutation/form runtime contracts
    - app pages need the real `useSubscription*`, model APIs, instance search, channel options, common user/model context, and cloud store wiring
- governance decision
  - keep the drawer/form/selectors as governed business components under `components`
  - move all runtime ownership into thin `app/cmdb/components/*Runtime.tsx` wrappers
  - require Storybook to pass an explicit `formRuntime` contract so the governed CMDB workflow is fully verifiable without app context

    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/runtime.ts`
  - updated governed business components:

    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/instanceSelector.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/recipientSelector.tsx`

    - `src/app/cmdb/components/cmdbSubscriptionRuleFormRuntime.tsx`
  - updated app consumers:

    - `src/stories/cmdb-family.stories.tsx`

  - existing `Business/CMDB/SubscriptionDrawer` and `Business/CMDB/FamilyOverview` now exercise the full governed workflow through explicit mocked runtime contracts
  - after this migration, `src/components` and `src/stories` no longer directly import `app/*` runtime modules for this CMDB workflow

## 2026-07-01 Normalized detail-shell Storybook taxonomy across business domains

- candidate component
  - `src/components/node-manager-detail-layout-shell`
  - `src/components/integration-detail-layout-shell`
  - `src/components/opspilot-detail-layout-shell`
- why it was reviewed
  - these three components all sit on the same `DetailLayoutShell` foundation, but Storybook still exposed them under inconsistent hierarchy paths
  - inconsistent naming makes it harder to audit whether a shell is a framework base, a routed framework variant, or a business-specific composition contract
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - all three are business detail-shell leaves sitting on top of the governed framework shell branch
    - each one is reused by multiple routes inside its domain
  - difference analysis:
    - integration already used the desired `Business/<Domain>/Detail/LayoutShell` taxonomy
    - node-manager and opspilot still used older shorter or inverted paths, which obscured the common ownership boundary
- governance decision
  - normalize business detail-shell story paths to `Business/<Domain>/Detail/LayoutShell`
  - keep `Framework/Layout/Detail/*` reserved for the generic shell branch
  - remove stray unsupported prop usage from `OpsPilotDetailLayoutShell` so the business shell stays aligned with the actual framework-shell API
- migration
  - updated Storybook taxonomy:
    - `src/stories/node-manager-detail-layout-shell.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/opspilot-detail-layout-shell.stories.tsx`
  - cleaned business wrapper API usage:
    - `src/components/opspilot-detail-layout-shell/index.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new stories required
  - detail-shell stories now line up under one business taxonomy branch:
    - `Business/NodeManager/Detail/LayoutShell`
    - `Business/Integrations/Detail/LayoutShell`
    - `Business/OpsPilot/Detail/LayoutShell`

## 2026-07-01 Promoted workspace panel ownership from Job business shell to framework layout

- candidate component
  - `src/components/job-workspace-panel`
  - `src/components/job-page-header`
  - `src/components/page-header-shell`
- why it was reviewed
  - job execution, template, target, and safety pages repeatedly used the same bordered content workbench shell next to a separate business header wrapper
  - the page shell and the header shell looked adjacent in Storybook, but only one of them was truly domain-neutral
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - `JobWorkspacePanel` only provided a rounded container plus an optional toolbar slot
    - its API was purely structural and matched framework layout ownership rather than job business semantics
    - `JobPageHeader` and monitor dashboard headers both relied on `PageHeaderShell`, but each still carried domain-specific interaction semantics on top
  - difference analysis:
    - the workspace panel has no job-specific data, wording, or behavior and should live under framework layout
    - the page-header wrappers should stay separate for now because job and monitor still diverge meaningfully in controls, back behavior, and visual treatment
- governance decision
  - extract the neutral shell as `src/components/workspace-panel`
  - migrate job pages and governed job stories to the framework-owned panel
  - remove the old `job-workspace-panel` implementation to avoid keeping two names for the same structural shell
  - keep `JobPageHeader` as a business wrapper on top of `PageHeaderShell`
- migration
  - added framework-owned component:
    - `src/components/workspace-panel/index.tsx`
  - removed duplicate business-only shell:
    - `src/components/job-workspace-panel/index.tsx`
  - updated business component and page consumers:
    - `src/components/job-dangerous-rule-page/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/file-dist/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/quick-exec/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/job-record/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/cron-task/create/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/cron-task/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/cron-task/edit/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/template/playbook-library/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/template/script-library/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/target/page.tsx`
    - `src/stories/job-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/job-workspace-panel.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - reclassified the shell story as `Framework/Layout/WorkspacePanel`
  - no new stories required
  - `JobPageHeader` remains under `Business/Job/PageHeader`, while `PageHeaderShell` continues to define the lower-level framework contract

## 2026-07-01 Removed duplicate business empty-state wrappers in favor of governed shared feedback contracts

- candidate component
  - `src/components/job-playbook-detail-empty-state`
  - `src/components/cmdb-collection-detail-empty-state`
  - `src/components/opspilot-provider-empty-state`
  - `src/components/knowledge-graph-empty-state`
  - `src/components/cmdb-visualization-empty-state`
- why it was reviewed
  - these components looked business-specific in Storybook, but several of them were only thin passthrough wrappers around `CompactEmptyState` or `ChartEmptyState`
  - the wrappers introduced extra component names and extra Storybook leaves without adding distinct behavior, which is exactly the kind of component drift this governance effort is meant to remove
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - three wrappers only injected `description` plus a default `py-8` class on top of `CompactEmptyState`
    - two visualization wrappers only centered `ChartEmptyState` with `compact` mode and optional height
    - family stories in Job, CMDB, OpsPilot, and Knowledge already demonstrated the domain examples; the standalone wrapper stories did not add new contract surface
  - difference analysis:
    - the compact empty wrappers carried no business semantics beyond default spacing
    - the visualization wrappers only needed a small framework enhancement so `ChartEmptyState` could accept direct container styling
- governance decision
  - delete the duplicate wrapper components instead of replacing them with another shared abstraction
  - expand `ChartEmptyState` slightly with direct `className` and `style` support
  - move all usages back to the true governed shared components: `CompactEmptyState` and `ChartEmptyState`
  - keep business examples in family stories rather than preserving standalone empty-state wrapper components
- migration
  - updated shared framework component:
    - `src/components/chart-empty-state/index.tsx`
  - removed duplicate business wrappers:
    - `src/components/job-playbook-detail-empty-state/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-collection-detail-empty-state/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/opspilot-provider-empty-state/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/knowledge-graph-empty-state/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-visualization-empty-state/index.tsx`
  - updated app and business component consumers:
    - `src/components/job-playbook-detail-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/nodeMgmtSyncDetail.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/taskDetail.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/modelManagement.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorCardGrid.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/grid.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/knowledgeGraphView.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/knowledge/knowledgeGraphPage.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/relationships/networkTopo.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/relationships/roomFloorPlan.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/relationships/rackElevation.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/topologyGraphModal.tsx`
  - updated family stories:
    - `src/stories/job-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/cmdb-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/opspilot-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/knowledge-family.stories.tsx`
  - removed duplicate standalone stories:
    - `src/stories/job-playbook-detail-empty-state.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/cmdb-collection-detail-empty-state.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/opspilot-provider-empty-state.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/knowledge-graph-empty-state.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/cmdb-visualization-empty-state.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new stories required
  - the source of truth is now clearer:
    - compact empty states live under `Framework/Feedback/CompactEmptyState`
    - chart visualization empties live under `Framework/Charts/State/EmptyState`
    - domain-specific examples stay in the relevant family stories instead of separate wrapper leaf stories

## 2026-07-01 Extracted a shared status-badge shell while preserving business status semantics

- candidate component
  - `src/components/execution-status-badge`
  - `src/components/node-manager-runtime-status-badge`
  - `src/components/knowledge-processing-status-badge`
  - `src/components/custom-reporting-review-status-badge`
  - `src/components/custom-reporting-runtime-status-badge`
  - `src/components/monitor-reporting-status-badge`
- why it was reviewed
  - these components belong to different business domains, but they all repeated the same rendering shell: resolve a palette and label, then render the same `SemanticBadge` wrapper
  - the repetition was small but systematic, which makes it good governance material: real shared mechanism, different domain semantics
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - each badge computes or receives a semantic label
    - each badge chooses a `textColor/backgroundColor` pair
    - each badge then renders the same outer `<span className>` and `SemanticBadge`
  - difference analysis:
    - the domains still need distinct status enums, fallback labels, numeric-to-semantic mapping, and translations
    - the shared part is the final rendering shell, not the business status dictionaries themselves
- governance decision
  - keep each domain badge as a business-owned semantic adapter
  - extract the repeated rendering layer into one framework helper component: `StatusBadgeShell`
  - register the new shared helper in Storybook so the contract is explicit and reusable
- migration
  - added framework-owned component:
    - `src/components/status-badge-shell/index.tsx`
  - added Storybook contract:
    - `src/stories/status-badge-shell.stories.tsx`
  - updated business badge adapters:
    - `src/components/execution-status-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/node-manager-runtime-status-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/knowledge-processing-status-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/custom-reporting-review-status-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/custom-reporting-runtime-status-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/monitor-reporting-status-badge/index.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no existing business stories needed removal
  - the shared rendering contract now lives under `Framework/DataDisplay/StatusBadgeShell`
  - domain stories remain the source of truth for business status semantics, while the framework story now owns the repeated badge rendering shell

## 2026-07-01 Reused the shared status-badge shell across type and availability badge families

- candidate component
  - `src/components/job-type-badge`
  - `src/components/job-driver-badge`
  - `src/components/job-trigger-source-badge`
  - `src/components/job-script-type-badge`
  - `src/components/knowledge-document-type-badge`
  - `src/components/opspilot-availability-badge`
  - `src/components/mlops-algorithm-type-badge`
  - `src/components/monitor-alert-type-badge`
- why it was reviewed
  - after extracting `StatusBadgeShell`, another family of business badge wrappers still repeated the same `span + SemanticBadge` shell pattern
  - these badges differ in semantics, but they all reduce to the same final rendering contract once label and palette are resolved
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - job badges map job-specific enums like type, driver, trigger source, and script type into palettes
    - knowledge, opspilot, mlops, and monitor badges do the same for document type, online/offline availability, algorithm type, and alert type
    - every component wrapped the exact same final `SemanticBadge` shell
  - difference analysis:
    - each domain still needs its own enum normalization, i18n, fallback label logic, and in one case an inline status dot
    - the reusable part is still the status-badge shell, not a cross-domain “universal type badge”
- governance decision
  - keep all domain badge components as business semantic adapters
  - migrate them to the already-governed `StatusBadgeShell` instead of rendering `SemanticBadge` directly
  - avoid creating a broader abstraction that would blur domain semantics or overfit unrelated badge taxonomies
- migration
  - updated business badge adapters:
    - `src/components/job-type-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/job-driver-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/job-trigger-source-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/job-script-type-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/knowledge-document-type-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/opspilot-availability-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/mlops-algorithm-type-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/monitor-alert-type-badge/index.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no story deletions required
  - the shared contract remains `Framework/DataDisplay/StatusBadgeShell`
  - existing business stories continue to define each domain’s semantic mapping while now rendering through the shared shell

## 2026-07-01 Folded remaining badge wrappers into the shared status-badge shell

- candidate component
  - `src/components/version-badge`
  - `src/components/event-status-tag`
  - `src/components/custom-reporting-mode-badge`
  - `src/components/monitor-integration-access-badge`
  - `src/components/opspilot-config-severity-badge`
- why it was reviewed
  - these components were still thin wrappers over `SemanticBadge` after the first badge-governance passes
  - they covered multiple apps and domains, which made them good final candidates for converging the shared badge rendering contract
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - every component resolved a label and a palette, then rendered the same `span + SemanticBadge` shell
    - `VersionBadge` and `EventStatusTag` represent framework-level data-display patterns reused across multiple apps
    - the other three badges are business semantic adapters for CMDB custom reporting, Monitor integrations, and OpsPilot config analysis
  - difference analysis:
    - domain-specific enums, i18n copy, and icon composition still belong in the local business component
    - only the rendering shell was common, so the right move was reuse rather than a new “universal badge” abstraction
- governance decision
  - migrate all five wrappers onto `StatusBadgeShell`
  - keep Storybook ownership split explicit:
    - framework stories remain under `Framework/*`
    - business semantic stories remain under `Business/*`
- migration
  - updated framework-aligned wrappers:

    - `src/components/event-status-tag/index.tsx`
  - updated business semantic wrappers:

    - `src/components/monitor-integration-access-badge/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/opspilot-config-severity-badge/index.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new stories were required because these components already had explicit contracts in Storybook
  - `Framework/DataDisplay/VersionBadge` and `Framework/DataDisplay/StatusTag` continue to own framework display semantics
  - `Business/CMDB/CustomReporting/ModeBadge`, `Business/Monitor/Integrations/AccessBadge`, and `Business/OpsPilot/ConfigSeverityBadge` continue to own business semantics while now rendering through the shared shell

## 2026-07-01 Extracted a shared auto-fit metric value shell for single-value widgets

- candidate component
  - `src/components/log-analysis-widgets/single`
  - `src/components/ops-analysis-widgets/single`
  - `src/components/log-kpi-card`
- why it was reviewed
  - these components lived in different business domains but repeated the same core display mechanism:
    - split a metric into value and unit
    - auto-fit typography to the available width
    - keep unit sizing visually subordinate
    - render inside the same chart-card surface pattern
  - the repeated work was structural UI behavior, not domain semantics, so it fits framework extraction well
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - all three surfaces render one dominant metric value with responsive sizing
    - `LogAnalysisSingle` and `LogKpiCard` render plain metric text with simple accent color control
    - `OpsAnalysisSingle` adds value mapping, threshold colors, optional compare text, and sparkline context
  - difference analysis:
    - business-owned value derivation, compare semantics, threshold logic, and sparkline behavior remain domain-specific
    - the reusable part is the auto-fitting metric text shell, not a universal “single-value widget”
- governance decision
  - extract a framework component for the typography contract only:
    - `src/components/auto-fit-metric-value/index.tsx`
  - keep each widget responsible for its own data extraction and surrounding presentation
  - avoid merging the full widgets, because their semantic responsibilities still differ materially
- migration
  - added shared framework component:
    - `src/components/auto-fit-metric-value/index.tsx`
  - migrated consumers:
    - `src/components/log-analysis-widgets/single.tsx`
    - `src/components/ops-analysis-widgets/single.tsx`
    - `src/components/log-kpi-card/index.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - added framework contract story:
    - `src/stories/auto-fit-metric-value.stories.tsx`
  - existing business stories continue to verify domain behavior:
    - `Business/Log/Analysis/Widgets/SingleValue`
    - `Business/OpsAnalysis/Widgets/SingleValue`
    - `Business/Log/Summary/KpiCard`

## 2026-07-01 Extended the shared metric-value shell into monitor and alarm KPI surfaces

- candidate component
  - `src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/stat-card`
  - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/components/SummaryStats`
- why it was reviewed
  - after introducing `AutoFitMetricValue`, monitor and alarm still had separate inline value+unit rendering blocks for KPI-style cards
  - both surfaces are high-visibility summary UI, so keeping them on different typography behavior would weaken visual convergence across apps
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - both cards render one dominant metric value with a smaller unit token beside it
    - both cards place the metric inside a business card surface with iconography and secondary metadata
  - difference analysis:
    - `Monitor StatCard` includes compare semantics and optional trend sparkline
    - `Alarm SummaryStats` uses a flatter inline card layout and subtitle-only trend text
    - the card skeletons remain business-specific, but the value rendering contract is shared
- governance decision
  - do not merge the full card components
  - reuse the framework-owned `AutoFitMetricValue` inside both business card shells so typography, unit scale, and width-fit behavior converge without collapsing different product semantics
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/monitor-dashboard-widgets/stat-card.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/components/SummaryStats.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new business story was required for `SummaryStats` because it remains page-local
  - existing `Business/Monitor/Widgets/StatCard` stories continue to verify monitor behavior
  - the shared typography contract remains owned by `Framework/DataDisplay/AutoFitMetricValue`

    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/collectionStats.tsx`

- Storybook plan
  - no new story was required because `PanelShell` already has a framework story under `Framework/Layout/PageScaffold/PanelShell`
  - this migration strengthens that existing contract by moving another page-local panel surface onto it

## 2026-07-01 Converged rich detail headers back onto the framework intro primitive

- candidate component
  - `src/components/detail-intro`
  - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/detail/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the alarm integration detail page still carried two hand-built header blocks:
    - generic integration header
    - Kubernetes summary left intro area
  - both repeated the same semantic pattern already present in shared detail layouts across OpsPilot and Node Manager:
    - visual identity area
    - title
    - supporting description
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - `DetailIntro` is already the framework-owned intro primitive for detail pages in OpsPilot and Node Manager
    - the integration detail headers express the same “entity intro” semantics, only with richer media instead of icon-font glyphs
  - difference analysis:
    - existing `DetailIntro` only supported icon-font visuals plus single-line truncation defaults
    - alarm integration detail needs custom media content and multiline descriptive copy
    - these are presentational extensions of the same intro primitive, not evidence for a second header component
- governance decision
  - extend `DetailIntro` to support:
    - custom `visual`
    - content/title/description class overrides
    - opt-out truncation for richer detail headers
  - migrate the alarm integration detail headers onto the broadened framework primitive instead of adding a new page-specific header component
- migration
  - updated framework component:
    - `src/components/detail-intro/index.tsx`
  - migrated business page:
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/detail/page.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - updated `src/stories/detail-intro.stories.tsx` with a rich-media detail-header variant documenting the broadened contract
  - existing business pages now validate the same intro primitive under real compositions instead of carrying parallel hand-built implementations

## 2026-07-01 Rolled remaining page-local overview headers back onto the shared page header shell

- candidate component
  - `src/components/page-header-shell`
  - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/page.tsx`
  - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/collect/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - both pages still hand-authored the same page-scaffold header structure even though the framework shell already exists:
    - title
    - descriptive supporting copy
    - page-level spacing contract
  - this is framework ownership, not app-specific business rendering
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - job pages already render list/detail workspace headers through `PageHeaderShell` via `JobPageHeader`
    - monitor dashboard pages already render title/action scaffolds through `PageHeaderShell` via `DashboardPageHeader`
    - the alarm integration overview and monitor collect editor matched the same scaffold semantics, just without actions
  - difference analysis:
    - alarm integration overview needs a simple one-line description
    - monitor collect needs a richer subtitle region with primary description plus a lower-emphasis explanatory note
    - both still fit the same `PageHeaderShell` contract because `subtitle` already accepts `ReactNode`
- governance decision
  - reuse `PageHeaderShell` directly instead of adding new app-local `OverviewHeader` or `CollectHeader` wrappers
  - document the richer subtitle composition in Storybook so multi-line explanatory copy is part of the governed contract
- migration
  - migrated page-local headers in:
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/collect/page.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - updated `src/stories/page-header-shell.stories.tsx` with a rich-subtitle variant documenting stacked explanatory copy
  - existing business wrappers such as `Business/Job/PageHeader` and `Business/Monitor/Widgets/PageHeader` remain business-owned compositions on top of the same framework shell

## 2026-07-01 Extended section-header contract for guide sections and migrated cross-app guide titles

- candidate component
  - `src/components/section-header`

  - these are framework-level content headers, not unique business widgets

  - difference analysis:

## 2026-07-01 Extended detail-list panel rollout into knowledge and security detail drawers

  - after the first extraction wave, the same detail-card shell still remained in adjacent read-only detail surfaces:

    - system-manager operation log detail drawer
  - these are exactly the kind of low-risk, high-signal consumers that prove whether a new framework container is actually coherent beyond its first migration set

    - the only meaningful differences are field sets and label widths

    - knowledge drawers use plain white drawer surfaces

  - continue rolling the same shared `DetailListPanel` outward instead of spawning drawer-specific detail wrappers
  - treat these consumers as evidence that the new framework contract is stable across multiple domains, not just inside integration flows

    - `src/components/knowledge-edge-detail-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/components/security/operationLogs.tsx`

  - these migrations strengthen that existing contract by expanding it into knowledge and security detail surfaces

## 2026-07-01 Re-centered family overview stories on governed business-component contracts

- candidate component
  - Storybook family overviews that were still showcasing legacy wrappers instead of the newly governed business shells:

    - `src/stories/alarm-integration-guides-family.stories.tsx`

  - difference analysis:
    - family overview stories still need to show domain semantics, not just isolated primitives
    - the fix is not another shared component; it is story composition that references the governed business components directly
- governance decision
  - keep the extracted business components as-is
  - update family overview stories so Storybook points to the governed contracts first, with page-local wrappers treated as adapters instead of the primary source of truth

    - `src/stories/alarm-integration-guides-family.stories.tsx`

  - custom-reporting family now demonstrates `PanelShell` plus `DetailListPanel` as the governed compact detail surface
  - alarm guide family now includes `AlarmIntegrationGuideSectionPanel` and `AlarmIntegrationGuideCredentialsPanel` before the source-specific full guides

## 2026-07-01 Formalized the boundary between CodeSnippet and the remaining genuine CodeEditor workflows

    - these are different business contexts, but they share the same true-editor contract rather than the read-only snippet contract

## 2026-07-01 Promoted integration completion presets into an explicit cross-app Storybook contract

- candidate component
  - `src/components/integration-access-complete/presets.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the shared completion surface itself was already documented in Storybook
  - but the cross-app preset helpers that actually stabilize monitor, log, CMDB, and flow onboarding variants were still only implicit in code
  - that left a governance gap: teams could see the shell, but not the approved app-specific action/title combinations they were supposed to reuse
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - all variants reuse `IntegrationAccessComplete`
    - each preset centralizes title, description, icon treatment, and action-set shape for a specific onboarding family
  - difference analysis:
    - monitor/log/CMDB K8s completion flows intentionally diverge in labels and action counts
    - those differences are stable business presets, not reasons to fork the completion component itself
- governance decision
  - keep `IntegrationAccessComplete` as the shared business surface
  - elevate the preset helpers into Storybook so the preset layer becomes part of the governed contract instead of hidden implementation detail
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/stories/integration-access-complete.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/integration-onboarding-family.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - `Framework/Feedback/CompletionResult` now includes preset-driven stories for:
    - monitor K8s
    - log K8s
    - CMDB K8s
    - monitor flow
  - `Business/Integrations/Onboarding/FamilyOverview` now shows the preset family side by side so app-specific completion variants remain discoverable and governed

## 2026-07-01 Elevated K8s onboarding entry presets into Storybook as governed cross-app contracts

  - preset/helper layers for the first K8s onboarding step:

    - `src/components/k8s-access-asset-fields/presets.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - monitor and log already reuse the same step-callout and access-asset field components
  - but the app-specific copy and prerequisite variants were still mostly visible only as implicit helper functions in runtime code
  - that created the same governance gap we had just closed for completion presets: the shell was documented, but the approved cross-app variants were not

  - shared structure:

    - only the copy/details vary by business domain
  - difference analysis:

    - log adds runtime-preset guidance and log-specific copy

  - keep `IntegrationStepCallout` and `K8sAccessAssetFields` as the shared building blocks
  - promote the monitor/log preset helpers into Storybook so the entry-step variant layer becomes part of the explicit contract

  - updated:

    - `src/stories/integration-onboarding-family.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan

  - `Business/Integrations/K8s/AccessAssetFields` now includes preset-driven monitor/log copy variants
  - `Business/Integrations/Onboarding/FamilyOverview` now shows the full K8s entry preset pair side by side so onboarding-first reuse stays discoverable and governed

  - `Business/Integrations/K8s/ConfigurationShell` now demonstrates governed completion presets directly, keeping the full K8s onboarding shell aligned with the preset-first contract

## 2026-07-01 Extracted event notification copy presets as a real cross-app business contract

- candidate component
  - `src/components/event-notification-form/index.tsx`
  - new preset layer:
    - `src/components/event-notification-form/presets.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - `EventNotificationForm` is reused in both:
    - monitor event strategy detail
    - log event strategy detail
  - but monitor, log, the focused Storybook story, and the events family overview were each maintaining their own near-duplicate notification copy objects
  - that made the notification wording contract fragmented across runtime pages and Storybook instead of being governed from one reusable layer
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - monitor and log both use the same notification form layout and behavior
    - the main differences are business copy and notifier-mode semantics
  - difference analysis:
    - copy varies predictably by app domain:
      - monitor uses multi-channel notification wording
      - log includes tag-recipient placeholder wording for single-channel flows
    - channel-type label mapping and notifier-mode rules also vary predictably by app domain while still remaining stable cross-app contracts
    - these are stable business presets, not separate component implementations
- governance decision
  - keep `EventNotificationForm` as the shared business component
  - extract monitor/log copy and notification-behavior semantics into explicit preset helpers so runtime pages and Storybook consume the same contract instead of cloning local objects and callback logic
- migration
  - added:
    - `src/components/event-notification-form/presets.tsx`
  - updated runtime usage:
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/strategy/detail/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/log/(pages)/event/strategy/detail/page.tsx`
  - updated Storybook usage:
    - `src/stories/event-notification-form.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/events-family.stories.tsx`
  - preset helpers now govern:
    - copy
    - channel-tag label resolution
    - notifier-mode resolution
    - notifier-clearing strategy
- Storybook plan
  - `Business/Events/Strategy/NotificationForm` now documents monitor/log variants through governed preset helpers rather than story-local copy
  - `Business/Events/FamilyOverview` now points to the same monitor notification preset contract used by runtime event strategy flows

## 2026-07-02 Consolidated Framework security password Storybook contract

- candidate component
  - Storybook support layer for:
    - `src/stories/security-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/password-form-modal.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/password-fields.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/password-policy-notice.stories.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the framework security stories still carried repeated password-field labels, policy lengths, required-char-type arrays, validation hints, and default password validator wiring across family and focused stories
  - that meant Storybook described the same password contract in several slightly separate local copies
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - `PasswordFields` and `PasswordFormModal` both depend on the same label vocabulary, icon affordance, and password-validator contract
    - `PasswordPolicyNotice` and `PasswordFormModal` both document the same min/max length and required-char-type policy
    - `security-family` composes those same framework contracts together
  - difference analysis:
    - the focused `PasswordFields` story intentionally overrides two copy strings for a narrower admin-style variant
    - the loading `PasswordPolicyNotice` story intentionally keeps the same policy length but expands required char types to include `special`
    - those are local variants on top of one governed framework password contract
- governance decision
  - do not create a new runtime abstraction
  - add one Storybook fixture file for the framework password contract, then let focused stories override only the few strings or policy branches that genuinely differ
- migration
  - added:
    - `src/stories/security-password.fixtures.tsx`
  - updated:
    - `src/stories/security-family.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/password-form-modal.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/password-fields.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/password-policy-notice.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - framework security stories now share one password contract fixture for labels, prefix icon, base policy, stricter policy, validation hint, and default validator wiring
  - Storybook therefore keeps a single source of truth for password UI semantics across family and focused framework stories

## 2026-07-02 Documented node-manager auth workflow in Storybook

    - both domains need password/private-key switching plus field-config snapshots for modal or import flows
  - difference analysis:

## 2026-07-02 Extracted shared guide step panels across alarm and node-manager flows

- candidate component
  - shared step-panel shell for guided operational instructions used by:
    - `src/components/alarm-k8s-guide/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/node/controllerInstall/installing/operationGuidanceSections.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - after earlier Storybook governance passes, two business domains still carried their own page-local step-card structures for operational guidance:
    - alarm integration guides rendered a timeline of numbered setup cards
    - node-manager operation guidance rendered numbered install instruction cards
  - both repeated the same semantic skeleton:
    - numbered step badge
    - step title
    - optional helper description
    - body content with code snippets, alerts, or detail blocks
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - guided setup/install content is expressed as ordered steps with strongly repeated typography and spacing
    - both domains embed business-specific bodies, but the shell around each step is the same UI contract
  - difference analysis:
    - alarm uses a timeline treatment with connector lines and optional eyebrow labels
    - node-manager uses compact filled cards without timeline connectors
    - those are visual variants of one shell, not separate components
- governance decision
  - do not force these flows into `StepWizardFlow`, because that component owns interactive step state and navigation
  - extract a smaller shared `GuideStepPanel` shell that only owns presentation for ordered instruction panels
- migration
  - added:
    - `src/components/guide-step-panel/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/guide-step-panel.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/node-manager-operation-guidance.stories.tsx`
  - updated:
    - `src/components/alarm-k8s-guide/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/node/controllerInstall/installing/operationGuidanceSections.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - `Framework/Content/GuideStepPanel` now defines the shared ordered-instruction shell contract with card and timeline variants
  - `Business/NodeManager/OperationGuidance/Sections` documents the node-manager business usage on top of that shared shell
  - existing alarm integration guide stories continue to validate the alarm-side business composition after migration

## 2026-07-02 Rejected false-positive convergence in monitor/log event strategy detail flows

- candidate component
  - same-named strategy detail components across:
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/strategy/detail/basicInfoForm.tsx`
    - `src/app/log/(pages)/event/strategy/detail/basicInfoForm.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/strategy/detail/conditionSelector.tsx`
    - `src/app/log/(pages)/event/strategy/detail/conditionSelector.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/strategy/detail/alertConditionsForm.tsx`
    - `src/app/log/(pages)/event/strategy/detail/alertConditionsForm.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the duplicated filenames suggested a possible cross-app component family that might still be split between monitor and log
  - this was a high-value audit target because the user explicitly asked to eliminate duplicated non-business components across apps
- cross-app comparison
  - shared surface signal:
    - both modules expose event-strategy detail pages with similarly named leaf files
  - difference analysis:
    - monitor `basicInfoForm` is a richer monitor-target configuration surface with scheduling, assets, and threshold-oriented setup
    - log `basicInfoForm` is a much smaller identity form built directly on `EventStrategyIdentityFields`
    - monitor `conditionSelector` models metric/group-by conditions, while log `conditionSelector` models field/function/operator filters for log queries
    - monitor `alertConditionsForm` centers on threshold and no-data alert semantics, while log `alertConditionsForm` centers on query/display field/grouping/trigger policy semantics
    - the page-level data contracts, copy, and operator workflows have already diverged too far for a stable shared component API
- governance decision
  - do not merge these monitor/log strategy detail files into a shared component family
  - treat the same filenames as a false-positive structural signal, not as proof of reusable UI
  - continue reusing smaller governed primitives under them when opportunities are real, but keep the strategy forms business-owned
- migration
  - no code migration performed
- Storybook plan
  - no new shared story should be introduced for this rejected convergence path
  - future strategy-form governance should target smaller proven primitives rather than the full page-local form assemblies

## 2026-07-02 Confirmed snmp trap runtime wrappers are valid business adapters, not framework duplication

- candidate component
  - runtime wrappers around `SnmpTrapGuidePanel` in:
    - `src/app/alarm/components/snmpTrapGuide/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/log/(pages)/integration/list/detail/configure/snmpTrapConfiguration.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - both flows fetch node data, manage selected-node state, and render the same shared `SnmpTrapGuidePanel`
  - this made them a tempting follow-up target after the shared panel itself had already been governed
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - both adapters wrap the same governed business panel
    - both own async node loading and selected-node state before passing data to the panel
  - difference analysis:
    - alarm resolves trap target data from alert-source node IP and adds a second MIB-path step
    - log resolves cloud-region proxy addresses and only exposes the target-address step
    - their API hooks, translated copy, and step semantics belong to different business flows even though the rendered panel shell is shared
- governance decision
  - keep these runtime wrappers as business adapters
  - do not force an extra shared runtime component above `SnmpTrapGuidePanel`, because that would only move divergent business loading logic into a muddy abstraction
  - treat `SnmpTrapGuidePanel` as the correct shared contract boundary
- migration
  - no code migration performed
- Storybook plan
  - existing `Business/Integrations/SNMP/SnmpTrapGuidePanel` remains the source of truth for the shared SNMP onboarding contract
  - no extra framework story is needed for the adapter layer because the wrappers are business-specific runtime glue

## 2026-07-02 Removed redundant custom-reporting detail-card wrapper in favor of PanelShell

- candidate component
  - `src/components/custom-reporting-detail-card/index.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the component had no custom-reporting-specific semantics beyond a compact border, background, and padding wrapper
  - the user explicitly asked to eliminate repeated non-business components across apps, and this wrapper duplicated framework framing responsibility under a business-specific name
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - custom-reporting batch review cards
    - custom-reporting token panel mode
    - layout-family compact framed surfaces
    - system-manager source-menu and alarm guide shells
  - difference analysis:
    - custom-reporting used a body-only compact frame
    - other domains sometimes add headers or footers, but those are already optional slots on `PanelShell`
    - the only real difference was styling parameters, not structure or semantics
- governance decision
  - remove `CustomReportingDetailCard`
  - reuse `PanelShell` directly for compact framed detail surfaces
  - keep custom-reporting business meaning in the content components and family stories, not in a parallel frame primitive
- migration
  - deleted:
    - `src/components/custom-reporting-detail-card/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/custom-reporting-detail-card.stories.tsx`
  - updated:

    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/customReporting/components/batchReviewDrawer.tsx`

    - `src/stories/panel-shell.stories.tsx`

  - `Business/CMDB/CustomReporting/FamilyOverview` continues to validate the business composition on top of that framework shell

    - node management sync cards only need the basic labeled metric

  - hand-rendered playbook metadata rows in:

  - this was a good follow-up because the same drawer had already been extracted as a shared job-domain business component, so its inner metadata rows should also align with the shared primitive layer
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - label/value metadata rows with a fixed label column
    - read-only informational content
    - no business-specific row interaction
  - difference analysis:
    - playbook detail only needs non-copyable display values
    - `DetailListPanel` already supports that through `displayValue` and `copyable: false`
    - no extra job-specific row component is justified
- governance decision

  - keep `JobPlaybookDetailDrawer` as the business wrapper that owns tab structure, file tree, params table, and README rendering

    - `src/stories/job-playbook-detail-drawer.stories.tsx`

## 2026-07-02 Converged AlarmBaseInfo internals onto DetailListPanel

  - internal read-only summary rows in:
    - `src/components/alarm-base-info/index.tsx`

  - this was a good convergence target because it improves style consistency without changing the alarm-domain field contract
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - one-column label/value informational rows
    - fixed label width
    - placeholder fallback for missing values
  - difference analysis:
    - `AlarmBaseInfo` needs a scrollable content row for long alarm text
    - `DetailListPanel` already supports that through `displayValue`, a custom wrapper, and `copyable: false`
    - there is no remaining reason to keep a separate `Descriptions`-based rendering path
- governance decision
  - keep `AlarmBaseInfo` as the business contract
  - replace its internal row rendering with `DetailListPanel` so alarm detail surfaces align with the shared detail primitive layer

## 2026-07-02 Converged profess raw-data row shells onto DetailListPanel

  - the paginated raw-data detail rows in:

    - placeholder fallback behavior

## 2026-07-02 Rejected false-positive convergence between event summaries and incident detail descriptions

- candidate component
  - two-column information-summary grids and editable description layouts across:
    - `src/components/event-alert-information-summary/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/incidents/detail/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - both surfaces still use `Descriptions`, which makes them look like obvious follow-up candidates for one more shared summary-grid abstraction
  - this was worth checking because the current governance direction is to eliminate duplicated non-business shells, especially when a structural pattern appears in more than one app
- cross-app comparison
  - shared surface signal:
    - both render labeled information rows in a multi-column `Descriptions` layout
    - both appear inside high-visibility alert or incident detail flows
  - difference analysis:
    - `EventAlertInformationSummary` is a read-only event-summary contract with a fixed action slot below the summary and caller-owned row content, including optional `span` handling for monitor/log alert metadata
    - incident detail mixes status actions, inline assignee editing, inline team editing, note editing, permission-gated controls, and responsive multi-column page layout inside the `Descriptions` grid itself
    - the incident page treats the grid as an editable operational workspace, not a passive summary block
    - forcing both onto one shared summary-grid abstraction would blur the boundary between read-only event summary semantics and inline-edit incident workflow semantics
- governance decision
  - keep `EventAlertInformationSummary` as the business summary contract for monitor/log alert detail flows
  - keep incident detail descriptions business-owned
  - do not introduce a generic shared summary-grid shell from these two surfaces
- migration
  - no code migration performed
- Storybook plan
  - existing `Business/Events/DetailDrawer/InformationSummary` remains the source of truth for the read-only event-summary contract
  - no new framework summary-grid story should be introduced for this rejected convergence path

## 2026-07-02 Removed redundant knowledge graph detail drawer wrapper in favor of DetailListDrawerShell

  - it no longer owned any graph-specific layout, data shaping, or interaction, so keeping it added one more component name without adding a real contract
  - this matched the same "garbage wrapper" smell that previously led to removing `CustomReportingDetailCard`

  - shared structure:
    - title + close handling
    - read-only detail rows
    - fixed drawer shell behavior
  - difference analysis:
    - knowledge node and edge drawers still have valid business semantics because they map graph-domain entities into labeled detail items
    - the intermediate `KnowledgeGraphDetailDrawer` added no extra business semantics between those business adapters and the shared framework shell
    - `DetailListDrawerShell` already has framework Storybook coverage, and node/edge drawers already have business Storybook coverage
- governance decision
  - remove `KnowledgeGraphDetailDrawer`
  - let `KnowledgeNodeDetailDrawer` and `KnowledgeEdgeDetailDrawer` compose `DetailListDrawerShell` directly
  - keep the node and edge drawers as business components because they still own graph-domain field mapping

    - `src/components/knowledge-edge-detail-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/knowledge-family.stories.tsx`
  - removed:
