    - `src/stories/knowledge-graph-detail-drawer.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - `Framework/Content/DetailListDrawerShell` remains the only framework source of truth for this drawer shell contract
  - `Business/Knowledge/Graph/NodeDetailDrawer`, `Business/Knowledge/Graph/EdgeDetailDrawer`, and `Business/Knowledge/FamilyOverview` continue to cover the knowledge-domain usage paths without a duplicate intermediate story

## 2026-07-02 Renamed CMDB config-file content drawer wrapper to keep framework and business boundaries explicit

- candidate component
  - page-local business wrapper:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/configFiles/components/contentDrawer.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - this component was not a shared primitive candidate, but it reused the same generic name as the framework `src/components/content-drawer/index.tsx`
  - keeping a page-private business adapter named `ContentDrawer` next to the governed framework `ContentDrawer` made code search, imports, and Storybook-first component classification noisier than necessary
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - both render a right-side content drawer
    - the page-local wrapper composes the framework drawer and injects config-file-specific title, metadata, encoding controls, and code preview content
  - difference analysis:
    - the framework drawer owns generic drawer behavior, loading, and fallback handling
    - the CMDB wrapper owns config-file business semantics and should remain business-scoped
    - the problem was naming overlap, not missing abstraction
- governance decision
  - keep the business wrapper
  - rename it to `ConfigFileContentDrawer` so the shared framework drawer remains the only generic `ContentDrawer` contract in the repo
  - do not add a separate Storybook entry, because this remains a page-local business adapter rather than a shared component
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/configFiles/page.tsx`
  - renamed:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/configFiles/components/contentDrawer.tsx`
    - to `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/configFiles/components/configFileContentDrawer.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - `Framework/Overlay/ContentDrawer` continues to be the only shared drawer contract for this interaction pattern
  - no new Storybook node is needed for the page-local CMDB wrapper

## 2026-07-02 Converged CMDB K8s guided wizard onto IntegrationK8sConfigurationShell

- candidate component
  - CMDB guided K8s onboarding container:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/k8s/k8sGuidedTask.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - monitor and log K8s onboarding flows had already converged on the shared `IntegrationK8sConfigurationShell`, while CMDB still assembled the same three-step sequence manually with `StepWizardFlow`
  - CMDB had already adopted the shared collector-install leaf and shared access-complete preset, so the remaining divergence was the outer wizard shell
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - step 1 access configuration
    - step 2 collector installation / verification
    - step 3 completion state with restart action
  - difference analysis:
    - CMDB step 1 still uses its own `K8sTaskForm`, because it saves a discovery task instead of creating a monitor/log integration instance
    - CMDB step 2 still uses its own runtime adapter around the shared `K8sCollectorInstallStep`
    - these business differences fit naturally inside `IntegrationK8sConfigurationShell` through `renderAccessConfig` and `renderCollectorInstall`
    - no CMDB-specific outer wizard shell is needed anymore
- governance decision
  - keep CMDB-specific form and runtime adapters
  - replace the local `StepWizardFlow` composition in `K8sGuidedTask` with the shared `IntegrationK8sConfigurationShell`
  - keep `StepWizardFlow` as the lower-level framework primitive and `IntegrationK8sConfigurationShell` as the governed K8s onboarding contract
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/components/k8s/k8sGuidedTask.tsx`
    - `src/stories/integration-k8s-configuration-shell.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - `Business/Integrations/K8s/ConfigurationShell` now includes a CMDB-guided-flow variant alongside monitor/log variants
  - this keeps the shared K8s onboarding shell contract centralized in one Storybook node while preserving app-specific injected content

## 2026-07-02 Deduplicated form-footer wiring across shared modal and drawer wrappers

- candidate component
  - framework wrappers with duplicated footer wiring:
    - `src/components/content-form-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/operate-form-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/operate-form-modal/index.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - these three shared wrappers all repeated the same `ModalActionFooter` prop-picking and rendering logic
  - this was framework-layer duplication rather than business variation, so leaving it in place would keep three almost-identical implementations drifting independently
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - all three expose the same action-footer API surface
    - all three only differ in the underlying shell they attach the footer to (`ContentDrawer`, `OperateDrawer`, `OperateModal`)
  - difference analysis:
    - the public component contracts should stay separate because the underlying shells still carry different overlay semantics
    - the duplicated footer-adapter implementation does not need to stay separate
- governance decision
  - keep the three public wrapper contracts unchanged
  - extract the repeated footer-rendering logic into one internal helper instead of introducing a new public shared component
  - this removes framework duplication without forcing any call-site migration
- migration
  - added:
    - `src/components/_internal/form-feedback-footer.tsx`
  - updated:
    - `src/components/content-form-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/operate-form-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/operate-form-modal/index.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - no new public Storybook node is needed because this change only consolidates internal implementation
  - existing stories for `Framework/Feedback/ContentFormDrawer` and `Framework/Feedback/OperateFormDrawer` remain the source of truth for the public contracts exercised by the refactored wrappers
  - those framework stories now document that both wrappers share the same governed action-footer wiring beneath their different overlay shells

## 2026-07-02 Rejected false-positive convergence between CMDB and monitor group modals, but normalized shared naming hygiene

- candidate component
  - similarly named business modals:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/management/list/groupModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/list/detail/metric/groupModal.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - both files exposed a `groupModal` concept and even repeated the same internal typo `GroupMoadal`, which made them look like a possible missed shared abstraction
  - this was worth checking because repeated naming and repeated implementation smell often travel together in cross-app governance work
- cross-app comparison
  - shared surface signal:
    - both use `OperateFormModal`
    - both create or edit a "group"-like entity
  - difference analysis:
    - CMDB group modal manages model classifications with `classification_id` and `classification_name`
    - monitor group modal manages metric groups with monitor-object-scoped API payloads and a different field contract
    - the overlap is only at the modal shell level, which is already governed by `OperateFormModal`
    - no additional shared business group-modal abstraction is justified

  - keep the two business modals separate
  - do not introduce a cross-app shared `GroupModal`
  - still clean up repeated internal naming errors so code search, DevTools display names, and future governance passes are less noisy

    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/management/list/groupModal.tsx`

    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/relationships/selectInstance.tsx`
  - changes:
    - renamed internal component identifiers from typo forms like `GroupMoadal` / `FieldMoadal`
    - normalized `displayName` values to `GroupModal`, `FieldModal`, and `SelectInstance`
- Storybook plan
  - no Storybook node changes are required because public component contracts did not change
  - this is naming hygiene in support of the existing governed modal contracts rather than a new shared abstraction

## 2026-07-02 Converged second-confirmation modal footers onto OperateFormModal

- candidate component
  - repeated modal footer pattern with a second confirmation step:
    - `src/app/node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/node/collectorOperation/collectorModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/node/controllerUninstall/index.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - both business modals already shared the same shell semantics: form content inside `OperateModal`, cancel button first, and a confirm button that sometimes needs an inline `Popconfirm`
  - the difference was not business structure, but duplicated framework-level footer assembly that sat outside the governed `OperateFormModal` contract
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - both are form-like operation modals
    - both need the same confirm/cancel footer ordering
    - both optionally gate the final confirm action behind a `Popconfirm` prompt
  - difference analysis:
    - the business body content remains different: collector package/config selection versus controller credential table
    - the safety-check footer interaction is identical enough to belong in the shared modal contract
- governance decision
  - extend `ModalActionFooter` and `OperateFormModal` with an optional shared `confirmPopconfirm` capability
  - migrate the two Node Manager business modals onto `OperateFormModal`
  - keep the business modal bodies separate and avoid introducing a fake cross-domain "operation modal" component

    - `src/components/modal-action-footer/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/_internal/form-feedback-footer.tsx`

    - `src/app/node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/node/controllerUninstall/index.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - added confirm-prompt coverage to:
    - `Framework/Overlays/ModalActionFooter`
    - `Framework/Feedback/OperateFormModal`
  - this makes the second-confirmation footer behavior part of the governed shared contract instead of an app-local reimplementation

- candidate component
  - repeated single-file drag-upload surfaces:
    - `src/components/node-manager-collector-package-modal/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/target/page.tsx`

  - both Node Manager and Job still rendered `Upload.Dragger` directly for one-file upload workflows even though the governed framework layer already exposed `SingleFileUploadPanel`
  - this was a cross-app framework repetition rather than a business-only special case
- cross-app comparison

    - one-file upload only
    - drag-and-drop affordance with icon and helper copy
    - consumer-owned validation and submit behavior
  - difference analysis:
    - Node Manager uploads collector packages inside a modal form
    - Job uploads an SSH key file inside a target-creation form
    - the upload shell is shared, while accepted file types, form binding, and submit semantics stay business-specific
- governance decision
  - reuse the existing `SingleFileUploadPanel` contract instead of keeping new app-local `Dragger` markup
  - keep business validation and payload assembly in place
  - do not merge multi-file or directory-upload flows into this contract

    - `src/components/node-manager-collector-package-modal/index.tsx`

  - changes:
    - fixed the shared panel's default icon import
    - replaced Node Manager collector package upload markup with `SingleFileUploadPanel`
    - replaced Job SSH key upload markup with `SingleFileUploadPanel` and normalized the form binding to file-list semantics
- Storybook plan

  - these migrations move app surfaces onto that existing Storybook-owned upload shell instead of expanding the business component surface

## 2026-07-02 Converged MLOps dataset upload shell onto shared upload and modal contracts

  - MLOps dataset upload modal:

  - after the previous single-file migrations, this became the last remaining app-level `Dragger` implementation outside the governed upload shell
  - it also still assembled its own modal footer even though the shared modal contract already supported left-side contextual content plus confirm/cancel actions

## 2026-07-02 Converged remaining footerless content drawers onto ContentFormDrawer

- candidate component
  - read-only or header-action-only drawers that still mounted the low-level `ContentDrawer` directly:
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/job-record/page.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-config-file-compare-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/cmdb-subscription-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/customReporting/components/batchReviewDrawer.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/customReporting/components/taskDetail.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/detail/documents/result/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - these flows belonged to different domains, but their drawer contract had already converged:
    - standard header
    - shared close lifecycle
    - no footer action row
    - optional header-side actions
  - keeping them on the raw drawer would preserve another layer of repeated shell decisions across Job, CMDB, and OpsPilot
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - page or feature-local state owns visibility
    - the body is domain-specific and remains local
    - drawer shell is read-only or uses nested inner modals instead of a drawer footer
  - difference analysis:
    - `job-record` shows script metadata and code content
    - `cmdb-config-file-compare-drawer` renders a synchronized diff workspace
    - `cmdb-subscription-drawer` hosts a rule list and launches an inner edit modal
    - `batchReviewDrawer` and `taskDetail` present reporting summaries and review data
    - `documents/result` opens chunk detail browsing
    - only `taskDetail` required header actions, so the governed shell needed an explicit header-extra hook
- governance decision
  - reuse `ContentFormDrawer` with `hideFooter` for all of these flows
  - extend the shared contract with `headerExtra` so header-side actions stay governed without overloading the footer `extra` slot
  - do not create new domain wrappers because the drawer body semantics remain app-specific while the overlay contract is now shared

## 2026-07-02 Eliminated remaining app-level raw Drawer usage behind ContentFormDrawer

- candidate component
  - app-level drawers that still depended on raw antd `Drawer` only because the governed shared shell was missing a few passthrough capabilities:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/custom-chat-sse/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/relationships/roomFloorPlan.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/relationships/deviceDetailDrawer.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - these flows no longer needed unique overlay primitives; they were blocked only by shell details such as nested-container mounting, local root positioning, custom z-index, closable toggles, and mask control
  - leaving them on raw antd `Drawer` would keep a second ungoverned drawer contract alive across OpsPilot and CMDB
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - right-side drawer presentation
    - body content remains feature-owned
    - no custom footer contract beyond close/read-only usage
  - difference analysis:
    - `custom-chat-sse` opens read-only reference details and graph previews, sometimes inside fullscreen local containers
    - `collection/profess` uses one edit-task drawer plus one side-by-side plugin document drawer
    - `roomFloorPlan` and `deviceDetailDrawer` keep a more stylized tech-detail visual treatment, but still use the same read-only drawer lifecycle
    - the differences are about mounting/styling, not about needing separate base components
- governance decision
  - extend `ContentDrawer` passthrough support for:
    - `rootClassName`
    - `rootStyle`
    - `getContainer`
    - `mask`
    - `keyboard`
    - `closable`
    - `zIndex`
  - keep using `ContentFormDrawer` as the governed business-facing shell
  - avoid introducing a new CMDB- or OpsPilot-specific drawer base because the existing shared shell now covers the real variation points
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/content-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/content-form-drawer.stories.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/custom-chat-sse/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/autoDiscovery/collection/profess/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/relationships/roomFloorPlan.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/detail/relationships/deviceDetailDrawer.tsx`
  - post-migration audit shows raw antd `Drawer` imports remain only inside:
    - `src/components/content-drawer/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/operate-drawer/index.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - updated `Framework/Feedback/ContentFormDrawer` with a nested-container story so side-by-side or locally mounted drawers are documented in the shared contract
  - no separate Storybook business story is added for the CMDB tech-detail visuals because this change governs the shell contract, not a new cross-app business component

  - updated `Framework/Feedback/OperateFormModal` with a wide footerless viewer story so graph/canvas-style read-only modal shells are explicitly documented

## 2026-07-02 Converged account and asset-save modals onto OperateFormModal

- candidate component
  - page and shared-business modals that already relied on standard form-footer semantics but still mounted raw modal shells:
    - `src/components/top-menu/user-info/userInformation.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/list/filterBar.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - these flows already behaved like governed form modals:
    - explicit confirm/cancel actions
    - optional disabled/loading button states
    - form submission owned by the modal body
  - some of them were still wiring `ModalActionFooter` manually inside raw antd `Modal`, which is exactly the kind of duplicated shell logic this governance pass is meant to remove
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - local open/close state
    - form body remains domain-specific
    - footer semantics match `OperateFormModal`
  - difference analysis:
    - `userInformation` contains identity verification and email change flows
    - asset data quick subscribe is a richer multi-action form modal with an extra secondary action
    - filter-bar save is a compact naming modal
    - they differ in body content and button states, not in modal-shell contract
- governance decision
  - reuse `OperateFormModal` for all three
  - map form submission directly to `onConfirm`
  - keep quick subscribe on the shared secondary-action contract instead of a page-local footer subtree
  - avoid leaving manual `ModalActionFooter` composition inside raw modal shells when the shared form modal already expresses the same contract
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/top-menu/user-info/userInformation.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetData/list/filterBar.tsx`
  - targeted audit confirms these files no longer import raw antd `Modal`
- Storybook plan
  - no new story node was needed because existing `Framework/Feedback/OperateFormModal` stories already cover:
    - compact field entry
    - multi-action footer
    - standard form submission modal
  - these migrations increase real usage coverage of that existing contract across account settings and CMDB asset flows

## 2026-07-02 Converged multi-step bulk-action modal onto OperateFormModal

- candidate component
  - a remaining raw antd `Modal` that represented a reusable shell pattern rather than a one-off exception:
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/template/bulkApplyModal.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - this modal is a canonical multi-step bulk workflow:
    - next / previous actions
    - final submit action
    - completion state that collapses to a single finish action
  - the complexity lives in step state and body content, not in needing a custom modal primitive
  - leaving it on raw antd `Modal` would keep a high-value workflow pattern outside the shared contract
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - modal shell is generic
    - body owns step content
    - footer actions are state-driven but still map to shared footer semantics
  - difference analysis:
    - unlike compact forms, this flow swaps button labels and secondary actions as the current step changes
    - once complete, it degrades to a single finish action
    - these are state transitions within the same governed footer contract, not reasons for a separate modal base
- governance decision
  - reuse `OperateFormModal`
  - represent previous-step action with `secondaryActionsPosition="beforeConfirm"`
  - switch `confirmText` / `cancelText` / `secondaryActions` by workflow state instead of preserving a page-local footer subtree
  - avoid inventing a special monitor-specific workflow modal base because the existing shared shell now covers this pattern
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/event/template/bulkApplyModal.tsx`
    - `src/stories/operate-form-modal.stories.tsx`
  - targeted audit confirms `bulkApplyModal` no longer imports raw antd `Modal`
- Storybook plan
  - updated `Framework/Feedback/OperateFormModal` with a stepper-actions story so previous/next workflow footers are documented in Storybook
  - no new business Storybook node is needed because the abstraction is still the shared modal-shell contract

## 2026-07-02 Converged card-selection and collaborator-picker modals onto OperateFormModal

  - difference analysis:

    - cron-task create/edit are scheduling forms with footer actions and host-selection modal flows
    - the field content differs substantially, so only the outer form workspace shell was promoted in this slice

  - keep all field groups, validation logic, footer actions, and host-selection modals page-local
  - explicitly do not abstract the form fields yet because the duplication only proves a shared shell, not a stable shared field model across all four pages

    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/cron-task/create/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/cron-task/edit/page.tsx`
  - targeted audit confirms Job-domain form pages now converge on one governed shell instead of duplicating the same header + workspace composition
- Storybook plan
  - added `Business/Job/Workspace/FormShell`
  - this story becomes the source of truth for job form-page framing while deeper field families remain page-local until stronger reuse evidence emerges

    - opspilot quota management adds a `TopSection` above the panel and keeps its quota modal outside

  - keep `system-manager-application-manage-list-shell` as a thin business wrapper so existing business stories and page semantics remain intact
  - migrate opspilot quota-management page to the shared shell directly

  - added:

  - updated:
    - `src/components/system-manager-application-manage-list-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/settings/manage/page.tsx`
  - targeted audit confirms the repeated search + table + action management surface now converges on one shared shell instead of separate system-manager and opspilot implementations
- Storybook plan
  - added `Framework/Layouts/ManagementTableShell`
  - existing `Business/SystemManager/ApplicationManage/ListShell` continues to document the business wrapper while delegating to the shared framework shell underneath

## 2026-07-02 Expanded management table shell adoption into OpsPilot skill rules

  - after promoting the shell from system-manager and opspilot quota management, we needed a third real page to verify the API was not overfit to one modal pattern
  - skill-rules provided a stronger validation case because it combines:

    - table state toggles

    - page-local modify modal outside the shell

    - search + action row
    - governed table framing
    - page-local business modal lifecycle
  - difference analysis:
    - skill-rules previously used raw `Table` plus separate fixed `Pagination`
    - system-manager application management uses modal-in-shell
    - opspilot quota management uses topSection plus external modal
  - these differences confirm the shell API is broad enough to support multiple admin page variants without app-specific forks
- governance decision
  - reuse the shared framework shell directly in skill-rules instead of inventing a new opspilot-specific list wrapper
  - normalize pagination back onto `CustomTable` through the shared shell to keep list-page behavior and styling aligned
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/skill/detail/rules/page.tsx`
  - targeted audit confirms a third real management page now uses the shared shell, strengthening confidence that the abstraction is governed rather than incidental

  - no new story required
  - `Framework/Layouts/ManagementTableShell` already covers the shared contract that skill-rules now consumes

## 2026-07-02 Extracted system-manager audit table shell across security and error logs

- candidate component
  - added shared business shell:
    - `src/components/system-manager-audit-table-shell/index.tsx`
  - validated pages:
    - `src/app/system-manager/components/security/loginLogs.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/components/security/operationLogs.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/settings/error-logs/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the three pages all repeated the same audit workspace frame:
    - right-aligned audit filter bar
    - dense table with standard pagination
    - optional page-local follow-up surfaces such as export flows or detail drawers
  - the repeated structure was business-specific to system-manager audit workflows, so leaving it as page-local markup would keep duplication alive across the same domain
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - `SystemManagerAuditFilterBar`
    - `CustomTable`
    - standard pagination wiring
    - optional trailing surfaces rendered after the table shell
  - difference analysis:
    - login logs adds row selection plus export modal and export progress
    - operation logs adds detail drawer with diff viewer
    - error logs wraps the audit shell in a settings-page card treatment and uses a taller vertical scroll window
  - these differences belong in shell props and trailing children, not in three separate page-level layouts
- governance decision
  - extract a business component, not a framework shell
  - keep the contract scoped to system-manager audit pages because the audit filter-bar semantics and follow-up workflows are tighter than the generic management-table family
  - do not merge this into `ManagementTableShell`; the overlap is structural, but the audit pages carry a distinct business vocabulary and composition pattern
- migration
  - added:
    - `src/components/system-manager-audit-table-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/system-manager-audit-table-shell.stories.tsx`
  - updated:
    - `src/app/system-manager/components/security/loginLogs.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/components/security/operationLogs.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/settings/error-logs/page.tsx`
  - targeted audit confirms the repeated audit list frame is now governed from one business shell while preserving each page's local export or detail behavior
- Storybook plan
  - added `Business/SystemManager/Audit/TableShell`
  - included a default variant and a card-wrapped settings-page variant so future audit pages can align with the same shell contract instead of cloning layout markup

## 2026-07-02 Converged OpsPilot knowledge cards onto the shared entity-list contract

- candidate component
  - existing shared framework component:
    - `src/components/entity-list/index.tsx`
  - newly validated page:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the knowledge list page still hand-authored its own add-card tile, card grid, menu trigger, title row, description block, and organization footer even though the repo already has a governed shared entity-card gallery shell
  - that duplication was visual and structural rather than business-semantic, which made it a good fit for direct convergence on the shared framework component
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - search-driven entity cards
    - add-new action tile
    - per-card overflow menu
    - click-through card navigation
    - bottom-right metadata slot
  - difference analysis:
    - the knowledge page uses a rotating icon family and organization text in the footer
    - those differences already fit within the shared `EntityList` extension points:
      - item-level `icon`
      - `menuActions`
      - `descSlot`
      - page-owned click routing
- governance decision
  - reuse `EntityList` directly instead of keeping a page-local card-grid implementation
  - keep infinite-scroll loading and mutation orchestration inside the page, because those are page behaviors rather than shared gallery-shell responsibilities
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/knowledge/page.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - replaced page-local grid markup with `EntityList`
    - kept page-owned load-more behavior, modal lifecycle, and delete/update flows intact
    - mapped knowledge cards onto the shared shell through `description`, `icon`, `menuActions`, and `descSlot`
- Storybook plan
  - no new story required
  - the shared contract already lives in:
    - `src/stories/entity-list.stories.tsx`
  - this migration strengthens that Storybook node as the source of truth for card-gallery pages rather than leaving OpsPilot knowledge as a visual fork

## 2026-07-02 Converged OpsPilot memory spaces onto the shared entity-list contract

- candidate component
  - existing shared framework component:
    - `src/components/entity-list/index.tsx`
  - newly validated page:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/memory/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the memory space page still carried another page-local asset-card grid with its own title row, search input, add action, overflow menu, description area, and footer metadata
  - after converging the knowledge page, this became a confirmed second implementation of the same shared "asset catalog card gallery" pattern rather than a one-off page
- cross-app comparison
  - shared structure:
    - title + supporting copy in the toolbar
    - search-driven card gallery
    - add-new entry tile
    - per-card overflow menu
    - click-through card navigation
    - footer metadata row
  - difference analysis:
    - memory spaces expose scope semantics (`team` / `personal`) instead of knowledge-base organization text
    - memory cards also show count and creator in the footer
    - those differences already fit inside the existing shared extension points:
      - `toolbarPrefix`
      - item-level `tagList`
      - `menuActions`
      - `descSlot`
      - page-owned click routing
- governance decision
  - reuse `EntityList` directly instead of keeping a second OpsPilot-only card grid
  - keep modal lifecycle, CRUD orchestration, and form schema inside the page because they are business behaviors, not framework-card concerns
  - keep `provider/vendorCardGrid` app-local for now because its brand visuals, enabled switch, and model-count behaviors are business-specific and do not match the generic catalog-card contract yet
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/memory/page.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - replaced page-local card-grid markup with `EntityList`
    - mapped memory scope into `tagList`
    - moved count and creator into the shared footer slot via `descSlot`
    - preserved page-owned modal editing, deletion flow, and detail routing
- Storybook plan
  - updated:
    - `src/stories/entity-list.stories.tsx`
  - added a `MemoryCatalog` variant under `Framework/DataDisplay/EntityList`
  - this keeps memory-style metadata cards inside the framework story node instead of letting the contract drift back into app-local markup

## 2026-07-02 Promoted OpsPilot entity management grid into a governed business shell

- candidate component
  - formerly app-local shared implementation:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/entity-management-grid/index.tsx`
  - current governed business component:
    - `src/components/opspilot-entity-management-grid/index.tsx`
  - current consumers:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/studio/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/skill/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - audit of the remaining OpsPilot card pages showed that `studio` and `skill` were already sharing one list-workflow shell, but that shell still lived under `app/` and had no Storybook contract
  - leaving it there would keep a real cross-page business component outside the governed component system even though it already controls search, type filtering, add-entry affordances, modal injection, pin toggles, and infinite-scroll behavior
- cross-app comparison
  - shared business structure:
    - right-aligned search/filter controls
    - add-entry tile in the same card grid
    - card-level edit/delete/pin actions
    - infinite-scroll loading
    - page-injected modify modal
  - difference analysis:
    - `skill` adds multi-type filtering and a two-action add tile
    - `studio` keeps a simpler search-only toolbar and custom delete/pin behavior
    - these differences belong to configurable business-shell props, not page-local forks
- governance decision
  - promote the shell into `src/components` as an explicit OpsPilot business component
  - keep it in the business layer rather than the framework layer because it bundles OpsPilot-specific CRUD and catalog behaviors
  - add an injectable `dataSource` contract so Storybook can drive the real shell without depending on live page APIs
  - keep `provider/vendorCardGrid` and `provider/grid` separate for now; they are provider-domain-specific card workflows rather than this generic OpsPilot asset-management shell
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/opspilot-entity-management-grid/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/studio/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/skill/page.tsx`
  - removed:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/entity-management-grid/index.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - moved the shared business shell out of `app/` to eliminate duplicate ownership
    - preserved existing page behavior by keeping `endpoint`-based mode for runtime pages
    - added `dataSource` injection so Storybook and future non-HTTP consumers can exercise the same contract
- Storybook plan
  - added:
    - `src/stories/opspilot-entity-management-grid.stories.tsx`
  - classification:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/EntityManagementGrid`
  - the stories cover both:
    - skill-style catalog management
    - studio-style catalog management
  - this makes the business shell an explicit governed contract instead of an app-private implementation detail

## 2026-07-02 Converged OpsPilot provider empty states onto one business contract

- candidate component
  - new business component:
    - `src/components/opspilot-provider-empty-state/index.tsx`
  - validated consumers:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorCardGrid.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/modelManagement.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/grid.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - provider-related surfaces were already described in Storybook family docs as sharing one empty-state contract, but runtime code still mixed direct `CompactEmptyState` calls with different spacing and copy ownership
  - this left the governance story ahead of the actual implementation, which is exactly the kind of drift we want Storybook-first governance to prevent
- cross-app comparison
  - shared business structure:
    - provider-domain empty feedback for vendor catalogs
    - provider-domain empty feedback for model sections and model grids
    - compact, low-noise messaging inside already-framed provider panels
  - difference analysis:
    - vendor catalogs need slightly roomier spacing and vendor-specific copy
    - model sections use a tighter vertical rhythm and model-specific copy
    - the visual contract is the same; only semantic variant and spacing differ
- governance decision
  - keep `CompactEmptyState` as the framework primitive
  - add `OpspilotProviderEmptyState` as the business semantic layer so provider pages stop inlining raw primitive usage
  - do not force `vendorCardGrid` and `provider/grid` into one card-shell yet; their item structures still differ materially even though their empty feedback now converges
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorCardGrid.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/modelManagement.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/grid.tsx`
    - `src/stories/opspilot-family.stories.tsx`
  - added:
    - `src/components/opspilot-provider-empty-state/index.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - replaced direct `CompactEmptyState` calls with one provider-domain empty-state component
    - kept provider-specific copy and spacing aligned through semantic variants instead of ad hoc page-local props
- Storybook plan
  - added:
    - `src/stories/opspilot-provider-empty-state.stories.tsx`
  - classification:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/ProviderEmptyState`
  - this makes the provider empty-state contract real in Storybook instead of merely implied by the family overview

## 2026-07-02 Promoted OpsPilot provider loading skeletons into governed business components

- candidate component
  - formerly app-local loading helpers:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/skeleton.tsx`
  - current governed business components:
    - `src/components/opspilot-provider-skeletons/index.tsx`
  - validated consumers:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorCardGrid.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/modelTree.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the provider catalog and provider model tree already shared a real loading-state language, but that contract still lived under `app/` and had no Storybook entry
  - once empty states were governed, the loading state became the next obvious gap in the provider feedback surface
- cross-app comparison
  - shared business structure:
    - lightweight provider-card placeholders for vendor catalogs
    - compact provider-side navigation skeleton for model-group trees
    - both are tightly coupled to provider-domain layouts rather than generic framework loading primitives
  - difference analysis:
    - grid skeleton mirrors vendor-card density and icon/header balance
    - tree skeleton mirrors left-rail search-and-group navigation
    - both should stay together in one provider loading family, but not be collapsed into a single over-generic skeleton API
- governance decision
  - promote the existing loading helpers into `src/components` as explicit OpsPilot business components
  - keep Ant Design `Skeleton` as the framework primitive underneath
  - avoid inventing a parameter-heavy universal loading shell just to merge visually related but structurally different placeholders

    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorCardGrid.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/modelTree.tsx`
  - added:
    - `src/components/opspilot-provider-skeletons/index.tsx`
  - removed:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/skeleton.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - moved provider loading contracts out of `app/`
    - preserved existing loading behavior while aligning ownership with other governed business components
- Storybook plan
  - added:
    - `src/stories/opspilot-provider-skeletons.stories.tsx`
  - classification:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/ProviderSkeletons`
  - the stories now document both provider loading modes:
    - vendor grid loading
    - model tree loading

- candidate component
  - dead app-local components:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/grid.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/modelTree.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/modelGroupModal.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the provider audit showed these files no longer had any imports, JSX consumers, or Storybook coverage
  - keeping them around would violate the no-garbage-components rule and create a false impression that provider still had an alternate group-management UI path

  - current live provider flows are:
    - vendor catalog cards via `vendorCardGrid`
    - vendor detail and model sections via `modelManagement` and `vendorBasicInfo`
  - difference analysis:
    - the removed files belonged to an older grouped-model browsing flow that is no longer reachable from current provider pages
    - there is no second live consumer pattern to justify extracting or preserving that branch
- governance decision
  - remove the dead provider-group branch instead of migrating it into shared space
  - retain current provider live components as the authoritative business path
  - remove dead supporting types and API methods so the remaining provider contracts match the actual UI surface area
- migration
  - removed:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/grid.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/modelTree.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/modelGroupModal.tsx`
  - cleaned:
    - unused provider types in `src/app/opspilot/types/provider.ts`
    - unused provider group-management API helpers in `src/app/opspilot/api/provider.ts`

  - no new story added
  - decision rationale:
    - dead components should be deleted, not legitimized by Storybook entries

  - formerly app-local business component:

  - current consumer:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/provider/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - after removing the dead provider forks, the active provider catalog path was clearly centered on one stable vendor-card grid
  - that grid already encapsulated card visuals, vendor-type badge semantics, enable/disable interactions, loading state, and empty state, but it still lived under `app/` and had no direct Storybook contract
- cross-app comparison
  - shared business structure:
    - card-grid browsing for provider vendors
    - vendor-type badge semantics
    - top-glow card visual treatment
    - per-card edit/delete actions
    - enable switch at the metadata edge
  - difference analysis:
    - these cards do not match the generic `EntityCard` contract used by studio and skill
    - vendor cards depend on provider brand icons, model counts, and enable switches, so they should remain a provider-domain business component rather than being forced into the generic OpsPilot card family
- governance decision
  - promote the vendor grid into `src/components` as an explicit provider business component
  - keep it separate from the generic OpsPilot card family because the interaction and metadata model differ materially
  - add direct Storybook coverage so the active provider catalog contract is documented at the component level instead of only through page screenshots or family overviews
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/provider/page.tsx`
  - added:
    - `src/components/opspilot-provider-vendor-grid/index.tsx`
  - removed:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorCardGrid.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - moved the live provider vendor-grid contract out of `app/`
    - preserved existing page behavior and callbacks while aligning ownership with other governed business components
- Storybook plan
  - added:
    - `src/stories/opspilot-provider-vendor-grid.stories.tsx`
  - classification:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/ProviderVendorGrid`
  - stories cover:
    - default data state
    - loading state
    - empty state

## 2026-07-02 Extracted shared provider vendor configuration fields across modal and detail flows

- candidate component
  - new business component:
    - `src/components/opspilot-provider-vendor-config-fields/index.tsx`
  - validated consumers:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorBasicInfo.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - audit of the active provider path showed that vendor creation/editing and vendor detail maintenance were each carrying their own copies of the same vendor configuration fields:
    - name
    - API base
    - API key editing
    - organization binding
    - enabled state
    - description
  - both flows also shared the same masked-password interaction pattern, which made this a real cross-surface business form contract rather than incidental similarity
- cross-app comparison
  - shared business structure:
    - vendor connection basics
    - masked API key edit/reset behavior
    - group binding
    - status toggle
    - description field
  - difference analysis:
    - the modal keeps provider-type cards and protocol selection above the shared fields
    - the detail page uses a simpler vendor-type selector and places test/save actions in the page footer
    - these differences belong to the surrounding container, while the repeated config-field block is stable and reusable
- governance decision
  - extract only the repeated vendor configuration field block as a business component
  - keep vendor-type selection and page/modal action layout local to each container
  - avoid over-abstracting the entire vendor form into one monolith with too many mode branches
- migration
  - added:
    - `src/components/opspilot-provider-vendor-config-fields/index.tsx`
  - updated:
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorModal.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/components/provider/vendorBasicInfo.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - moved shared field rendering into one business component
    - preserved modal-specific and detail-specific shells while removing duplicate field markup and password-edit handling
- Storybook plan
  - added:
    - `src/stories/opspilot-provider-vendor-config-fields.stories.tsx`
  - classification:
    - `Business/OpsPilot/ProviderVendorConfigFields`
  - stories cover:
    - modal-style field presentation
    - detail-style field presentation

  - difference analysis:

    - visual treatment and interaction contract remain stable across all four live sections
    - this is a real repeated business pattern inside the authoritative provider management surface, not a one-off cosmetic duplication

  - extract the repeated section shell as a provider business component
  - keep fetch orchestration and modal lifecycle in `modelManagement`
  - avoid turning this into a global framework table shell because the interaction and visual language are provider-domain-specific

## 2026-07-02 Removed duplicate detail-shell wrappers in favor of one framework contract

## 2026-07-02 Promoted PageLayout from app-local usage to a governed cross-app workspace shell

- candidate component
  - existing framework component, upgraded:
    - `src/components/page-layout/index.tsx`
  - migrated pages:
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/settings/quota/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/settings/key/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the repository already had a `PageLayout` shell used in system-manager user-management pages, but other routes were still hand-authoring the same page workspace frame:
    - top descriptive section
    - one rounded primary work area
    - route-specific height and overflow behavior
  - `opspilot/settings/quota` and `system-manager/settings/key` both had manual copies of that workspace container contract instead of reusing the existing shell
- cross-app comparison
  - shared framework structure:
    - optional `TopSection`
    - one primary workspace surface
    - fixed desktop-height body area
    - optional left-side navigator and main content split
  - difference analysis:
    - system-manager user pages use both left and right panes
    - quota and secret-key pages only need the right workspace
    - quota needs scrollable content, while secret-key keeps a standard table workspace
    - these are layout variants, not separate page-shell components
- governance decision
  - keep `PageLayout` as the single framework shell for this workspace pattern
  - enrich it with controlled styling hooks:
    - `className`
    - `topSectionClassName`
    - `bodyClassName`
    - `leftSectionClassName`
    - `rightSectionClassName`
  - avoid creating another `settings-page-layout` or app-branded wrapper around the same frame
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/page-layout/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/opspilot/(pages)/settings/quota/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/settings/key/page.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - removed page-local top-section + rounded-workspace wrappers
    - moved both pages onto the shared `PageLayout` contract
    - kept route-local loading, toolbar, and table/progress content inside `rightSection`
- Storybook plan
  - updated:
    - `src/stories/page-layout.stories.tsx`
  - classification:
    - `Framework/Layout/PageScaffold/Layout`
  - stories now show:
    - right-workspace-only usage
    - top-section + workspace usage
    - left/right split usage
    - full page workspace usage

    - monitor metric page uses a wider search field and two adjacent add actions

  - strengthen Storybook examples so the shared contracts explicitly cover these monitor variants

## 2026-07-02 Converged job dashboard card headings onto shared header contracts

- cross-app comparison
  - shared framework structure:
    - `SectionHeader` for card/section titles without actions
    - `PageHeaderShell` for title rows with trailing actions

    - execution trend, execution status distribution, and job type distribution only need a stable section title
    - recent jobs needs a title plus a trailing navigation action
    - both cases are already covered by shared framework components without needing a job-specific heading wrapper
- governance decision
  - reuse `SectionHeader` for passive card headings
  - reuse `PageHeaderShell` for the recent-jobs title row with action
  - avoid creating a new `job-dashboard-card-header` wrapper for one page

  - updated:
    - `src/app/job/(pages)/home/page.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - replaced repeated `<h3>` card headings with `SectionHeader`
    - replaced the recent-jobs title/action row with `PageHeaderShell`

  - no new story required
  - current source-of-truth stories already cover the required semantics:
    - `Framework/Content/SectionHeader`
    - `Framework/Layout/PageScaffold/HeaderShell`

  - migrated framework usage:
    - system-manager security and audit pages via `SystemManagerAuditTableShell`
    - alarm integration filter row via `app/alarm/(pages)/integration/components/FilterBar.tsx`
  - difference analysis:
    - system-manager needs right-aligned, wrap-capable filter rows
    - alarm integration needs left-aligned labeled filters
    - both differences are layout variants of the same toolbar primitive and fit class-based composition without a second API
- governance decision
  - promote the layout primitive into framework scope as `FilterToolbar`
  - delete `SystemManagerAuditFilterBar`
  - keep `SystemManagerAuditTableShell` as a business shell for now, but make it depend on the shared toolbar contract instead of a system-manager-only layout helper
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/system-manager-audit-table-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/integration/components/FilterBar.tsx`
    - `src/stories/system-manager-family.stories.tsx`
  - added:
    - `src/components/filter-toolbar/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/filter-toolbar.stories.tsx`
  - removed:
    - `src/components/system-manager-audit-filter-bar/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/system-manager-audit-filter-bar.stories.tsx`

    - `Framework/Toolbar/FilterToolbar`
  - covered variants:

## 2026-07-02 Removed the system-manager audit table shell by extending the shared management table contract

- candidate component
  - expanded framework component:
    - `src/components/management-table-shell/index.tsx`
  - removed business wrapper:
    - `src/components/system-manager-audit-table-shell/index.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - after `FilterToolbar` became the shared filter-row contract, `SystemManagerAuditTableShell` no longer owned any unique business behavior
  - it had become a thin composition of:
    - `FilterToolbar`
    - `CustomTable`
    - optional page-local children such as drawers or export modals
  - those responsibilities belong naturally in the shared table scaffold layer, not in a system-manager-specific shell
- cross-app comparison
  - migrated pages:
    - `system-manager/components/security/operationLogs.tsx`
    - `system-manager/components/security/loginLogs.tsx`
    - `system-manager/(pages)/settings/error-logs/page.tsx`
  - framework comparison:
    - `ManagementTableShell` was already governing search-based tables, top sections, actions, modals, and selection flows across system-manager and opspilot
    - audit pages added one more proven need: arbitrary toolbar content plus page-local child surfaces after the table
  - difference analysis:
    - operation logs need a custom filter toolbar and a detail drawer
    - login logs need a custom filter toolbar, export modal, and export progress surface
    - error logs need a custom filter toolbar plus a looser panel wrapper
    - all of these fit one expanded framework contract better than a dedicated business shell
- governance decision
  - extend `ManagementTableShell` with:
    - `toolbar`
    - `toolbarContainerClassName`
    - `tableContainerClassName`
    - `children`
  - migrate all audit pages directly onto `ManagementTableShell`
  - delete `SystemManagerAuditTableShell`
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/management-table-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/components/security/operationLogs.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/components/security/loginLogs.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/settings/error-logs/page.tsx`
    - `src/stories/management-table-shell.stories.tsx`
  - removed:
    - `src/components/system-manager-audit-table-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/system-manager-audit-table-shell.stories.tsx`
- Storybook plan
  - updated framework source of truth:
    - `Framework/Layouts/ManagementTableShell`
  - added variant:
    - `WithCustomToolbar`

## 2026-07-02 Converged alarm settings list pages onto the shared management table scaffold

- candidate components
  - existing framework components, reused directly:
    - `src/components/management-table-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/filter-toolbar/index.tsx` (for the operation-log variant)
  - migrated pages:
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertAssign/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/shieldStrategy/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/correlationRules/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertEnrichment/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/operationLog/page.tsx`
- why they were reviewed
  - these settings pages were each hand-authoring the same panel structure:
    - shared `Introduction`
    - search or filter toolbar
    - `CustomTable`
    - optional page-local modal or drawer
  - the pages differed in business content, but not in the framing contract
  - leaving each page on local toolbar/table markup would keep the same visual pattern fragmented inside one app
- cross-app comparison
  - matching framework patterns:
    - `ManagementTableShell` already governs search/action/table layouts across system-manager and opspilot
    - `FilterToolbar` already governs complex multi-control filter rows across system-manager, alarm integration, and other filter surfaces
  - difference analysis:
    - alert-assign, shield-strategy, correlation-rules, and alert-enrichment all fit the standard search-plus-primary-action table contract
    - operation-log needs a richer multi-field toolbar, but still fits the same table shell once `toolbar` is used instead of `searchProps`
    - modals and drawers remain page-local, which is the correct boundary
- governance decision
  - migrate the five pages directly onto `ManagementTableShell`
  - use `searchProps + actions` for the four simple list pages
  - use `FilterToolbar` plus `toolbar` for operation log
  - avoid creating a new alarm-specific settings list shell until cross-app evidence shows a stable business contract beyond the shared scaffold
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertAssign/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/shieldStrategy/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/correlationRules/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertEnrichment/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/operationLog/page.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - replaced page-local search rows and table wrappers with `ManagementTableShell`
    - preserved local operate modals, drawers, pagination, and filter behavior
    - aligned the operation-log filter row to `FilterToolbar`
- Storybook plan
  - no new story required
  - current framework source of truth already covers the needed contracts:
    - `Framework/Layouts/ManagementTableShell`
    - `Framework/Toolbar/FilterToolbar`

## 2026-07-02 Extended the shared management table shell for advanced CustomTable capabilities

- candidate component
  - expanded framework component:
    - `src/components/management-table-shell/index.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - after migrating richer monitor pages, we had hard evidence that some shared table surfaces need more than the base table props:
    - `fieldSetting`
    - `rowDraggable`
    - `expandable`
    - drag callbacks and other advanced `CustomTable` features
  - without a controlled passthrough, pages would either stay on hand-authored wrappers or force more business-specific shells back into the codebase
- cross-app comparison
  - motivating pages:
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/view/viewList.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/object/page.tsx`
  - difference analysis:
    - both pages still fit the same shared scaffold
    - their extra complexity lives in table behavior, not in layout semantics
    - a typed `tableProps` passthrough is more governable than spinning up new per-page shells
- governance decision
  - extend `ManagementTableShell` with a constrained `tableProps` passthrough for advanced `CustomTable` features
  - keep core scaffold inputs (`columns`, `dataSource`, `rowKey`, `pagination`, `scroll`, `loading`, `rowSelection`, `locale`) owned by the shell
  - use the new capability to migrate richer monitor pages instead of introducing new local wrappers
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/management-table-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/view/viewList.tsx`
    - `src/app/monitor/(pages)/integration/object/page.tsx`
    - `src/stories/management-table-shell.stories.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - `viewList` now passes `fieldSetting` through `tableProps`
    - `integration/object` now passes draggable and expandable table behavior through `tableProps`
    - both pages now sit fully on shared toolbar/table scaffolds
- Storybook plan
  - updated framework source of truth:
    - `Framework/Layouts/ManagementTableShell`
  - added variant:
    - `WithAdvancedTableProps`

    - `src/app/job/(pages)/template/script-library/page.tsx`

  - migration shape:

    - kept `children` as a compatibility path for non-table content

  - added coverage for the shell-owned table contract so future job library changes are validated in Storybook

    - `src/app/job/(pages)/template/script-library/page.tsx`
  - difference analysis:

    - the table mounting contract itself is the same and now belongs to the shell
- governance decision
  - migrate the remaining standard job list pages onto the `JobListWorkspaceShell` internal table contract
  - keep richer page-local drawers, modals, and action semantics outside the shell
  - avoid a split world where some job pages use the shared workspace contract and others still hand-wire tables

    - `src/app/job/(pages)/execution/job-record/page.tsx`

  - no new story required

## 2026-07-03 Extracted alarm settings workspace shell

- candidate component
  - new shared business component:
    - `src/components/alarm-settings-workspace-shell/index.tsx`
  - migrated pages:
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertAssign/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/shieldStrategy/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertEnrichment/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/correlationRules/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/operationLog/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the alarm settings area had already converged on a stable page-level business skeleton above `ManagementTableShell`:
    - `Introduction` guidance card
    - neutral settings surface container
    - search-or-filter toolbar
    - settings table
    - page-local operate modal or drawer
  - three pages also already shared the same `useSettingsTable` behavior contract:
    - `alertAssign`
    - `shieldStrategy`
    - `alertEnrichment`
  - `correlationRules` and `operationLog` then confirmed the same workspace framing pattern exists even when the inner workflow differs
- cross-app comparison
  - matching pages:
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertAssign/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/shieldStrategy/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertEnrichment/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/correlationRules/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/operationLog/page.tsx`
  - difference analysis:
    - assign, shield, and enrichment share nearly identical search, toggle, and modal rhythms
    - correlation rules adds an effective-alert drawer and a different table schema
    - operation log swaps the simple search bar for a denser filter toolbar
    - those differences are still variants inside one alarm-settings workspace family, not evidence for five separate page-local shells
  - non-matching pages reviewed indirectly:
    - `system-manager`, `opspilot`, and `log` pages also reuse `ManagementTableShell`, but they do not share the same introduction copy, settings-domain semantics, or toolbar rhythm strongly enough to justify a cross-domain page shell
- governance decision
  - extract a business component for the alarm settings domain rather than a new framework layout
  - keep `ManagementTableShell` as the framework source of truth for generic table framing
  - let `AlarmSettingsWorkspaceShell` own only:
    - the introduction card
    - the shared settings workspace container treatment
    - the standard search/action alignment defaults
    - the toolbar-variant entry point for denser settings filters
  - keep columns, fetching hooks, toggles, permissions, modals, and drawers page-local
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/alarm-settings-workspace-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertAssign/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/shieldStrategy/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/alertEnrichment/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/correlationRules/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/operationLog/page.tsx`
  - removed:
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/operationLog/index.module.scss`
  - migration shape:
    - replaced repeated `Introduction + ManagementTableShell` assembly with the shared business shell
    - standardized the settings surface styling across the migrated alarm pages
    - preserved page-local drawers, modals, columns, filters, and table behaviors
- Storybook plan
  - added business source of truth:
    - `Business/Alarm/Settings/Workspace`
  - added coverage for:
    - standard search-and-primary-action settings pages
    - dense filter-toolbar settings pages such as operation logs

## 2026-07-03 System-manager management list workspace review (no new shared component)

- candidate component
  - reviewed pages:
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/data/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/menu/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/channel/detail/settings/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - these pages all present a superficially similar list-management shell:
    - keyword search
    - primary add action
    - management table
    - page-local modal
  - this is exactly the kind of visual similarity that can lead to a weak duplicate wrapper if we do not verify the business contract carefully
  - the repo also no longer has a live `system-manager-application-manage-list-shell` implementation, which made it worth explicitly deciding whether to recreate that layer
- cross-app comparison
  - `application/manage/data` is a data-permission rule editor with group selection and nested permission-rule form state
  - `application/manage/menu` is a custom-menu list with enable/disable, copy, redirect-to-config edit flow, and built-in menu protection semantics
  - `channel/detail/settings` is a channel-instance list with back navigation, subtype-specific columns, and transport-specific edit modal behavior
  - all three reuse the same framework table shell correctly, but they do not share one stable higher-level business workflow beyond that
- governance decision
  - do not create or resurrect a new shared business shell for this family at this time
  - keep them on `ManagementTableShell` directly
  - avoid reintroducing a thin wrapper whose real abstraction would only be "search + add + table + modal", because that is already the framework contract
  - wait for stronger evidence such as:
    - a shared top-level application-management workflow
    - a stable domain-specific props contract
    - a third page with the same business rhythm rather than only visual resemblance
- migration
  - no code migration in this slice
  - this is an explicit governance hold to prevent a low-value duplicate component from coming back
- Storybook plan
  - no new story required
  - current source of truth remains:
    - `Framework/Layouts/ManagementTableShell`

## 2026-07-03 Migrated system-manager secret key list onto the framework table shell

- candidate component
  - existing framework component, reused directly:
    - `src/components/management-table-shell/index.tsx`
  - migrated page:
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/settings/key/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the secret-key page was still hand-mounting antd `Table` plus a page-local action bar even though the same app already uses `ManagementTableShell` for comparable management lists

  - matching reference pages:

    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/application/manage/data/page.tsx`
  - difference analysis:
    - secret-key management keeps its own create-success modal and warning treatment
    - administrator and application-management pages use pagination while secret keys do not
    - the shared concern is still the same table workspace framing, so the right move is to broaden the framework contract rather than invent a secret-key-specific shell
- governance decision
  - migrate the page to `ManagementTableShell`
  - extend the framework contract to treat `pagination={false}` as a first-class supported case
  - avoid creating a new system-manager business shell for a case already covered by the framework layer

  - updated:
    - `src/app/system-manager/(pages)/settings/key/page.tsx`
    - `src/components/management-table-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/stories/management-table-shell.stories.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - replaced the page-local action-bar-plus-`Table` assembly with `ManagementTableShell`
    - kept the page-level success modal, delete confirmation, and secret-copy flow unchanged
    - removed the old need for a special-case type cast by promoting no-pagination usage into the framework shell API
- Storybook plan
  - updated framework source of truth:
    - `Framework/Layouts/ManagementTableShell`
  - added explicit coverage for a non-paginated management list variant

## 2026-07-03 Extracted cross-app introduction table workspace shell

    - CMDB operation log adds scenario filtering and export action

      - framed management table

  - boundary check:

    - that is why the right target is a framework workspace shell rather than forcing CMDB onto an alarm-named business component

  - migration shape:
    - moved the repeated `Introduction + ManagementTableShell` assembly into the framework shell
    - kept alarm settings on their business shell while changing only the implementation underneath
    - migrated CMDB operation log from page-local `CustomTable` assembly to the shared shell with page-local filters, export, and query logic preserved
- Storybook plan
  - added framework source of truth:
    - `Framework/Layouts/IntroductionTableWorkspaceShell`
  - current business source of truth remains:
    - `Business/Alarm/Settings/Workspace`
  - added coverage for:

    - denser filter-toolbar table pages with trailing actions

## 2026-07-03 CMDB asset management workspace review (no new shared component)

- candidate component
  - reviewed page:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/management/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - after extracting `IntroductionTableWorkspaceShell`, this page looked like a possible follow-up because it also starts with:
    - `Introduction`
    - top-level search
    - action cluster
    - data presentation region
  - it needed an explicit audit so we would not incorrectly treat every introduction-plus-content page as the same workspace family
- cross-app comparison
  - compared against:
    - `src/app/alarm/(pages)/settings/operationLog/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/operationLog/page.tsx`
  - difference analysis:
    - asset management is not a framed table workspace at its core
    - the primary workflow is a two-mode page:
      - normal mode uses grouped model cards and classification operations
      - manage mode switches into classification ordering, visibility control, and draggable model layout editing
    - the embedded `CustomTable` only appears inside one branch of manage mode and is not the dominant page contract
  - that means the page does not share the same stable scaffold as the operation-log family, even though all of them happen to start with an `Introduction`
- governance decision
  - do not migrate this page onto `IntroductionTableWorkspaceShell`
  - do not create a new shared CMDB asset-management shell yet
  - keep the page local until there is evidence of a second page sharing the same:
    - grouped model-card browsing
    - dual normal/manage mode contract
    - classification-and-model drag layout workflow
- migration
  - no code migration in this slice
  - this is an explicit boundary decision to prevent overextending the new framework shell
- Storybook plan
  - no new story required from this review
  - current source of truth remains split correctly:
    - `Framework/Layouts/IntroductionTableWorkspaceShell` for introduction-plus-table pages
    - page-local CMDB asset-management composition for the model-layout workflow

## 2026-07-03 Extracted a governed MLOps algorithm workspace shell

- candidate component
  - new shared business component:
    - `src/components/mlops-algorithm-workspace-shell/index.tsx`
  - migrated pages:
    - `src/app/mlops/(pages)/[algorithmType]/(main)/algorithm-config/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/mlops/(pages)/[algorithmType]/(main)/training/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/mlops/(pages)/[algorithmType]/(main)/serving/page.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - the three MLOps pages were still each hand-assembling the same workspace scaffold:
    - algorithm-type badge on the left
    - one-line description copy
    - right-aligned search box
    - primary action button
    - refresh affordance
    - paginated `CustomTable`
    - page-local modal or drawer mounts after the table
  - the repeated code had already started drifting visually:
    - training used extra button rounding, text sizing, and shadow classes
    - serving and algorithm-config used slightly different spacing around the same controls
  - that is exactly the kind of multi-page business pattern Storybook should govern instead of leaving page-local
- cross-app comparison
  - matching pages:
    - `src/app/mlops/(pages)/[algorithmType]/(main)/algorithm-config/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/mlops/(pages)/[algorithmType]/(main)/training/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/mlops/(pages)/[algorithmType]/(main)/serving/page.tsx`
  - existing shared references considered:
    - `src/components/management-table-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/components/mlops-algorithm-type-badge/index.tsx`
  - difference analysis:
    - all three pages share one stable MLOps list-workspace contract:
      - business-owned algorithm taxonomy badge
      - single-line page description
      - search plus primary action plus refresh controls
      - table-driven list management
      - page-local dialogs and drawers mounted outside the table shell
    - their differences stay where they should:
      - table columns
      - API calls and mutation flows
      - modal and drawer business content
  - that makes this a real business-shell candidate rather than a one-off coincidence
- governance decision
  - extract a new business component:
    - `MlopsAlgorithmWorkspaceShell`
  - compose the existing framework `ManagementTableShell` underneath it instead of creating another table framework branch
  - keep business modals and drawers page-local through `modal` and `children`
  - standardize the top workspace styling for this MLOps family so the three pages stop drifting independently
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/components/mlops-algorithm-workspace-shell/index.tsx`
    - `src/app/mlops/(pages)/[algorithmType]/(main)/algorithm-config/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/mlops/(pages)/[algorithmType]/(main)/training/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/mlops/(pages)/[algorithmType]/(main)/serving/page.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - moved the shared header contract into the business shell
    - reused `ManagementTableShell` for the governed table container
    - preserved each page's columns, pagination, refresh behavior, and modal or drawer wiring
    - removed page-local visual drift in the primary action area by converging on one shared shell style
- Storybook plan
  - added new business source of truth:
    - `Business/MLOps/Algorithm/Workspace`
  - story coverage now includes:
    - search plus primary-action plus refresh header contract
    - table workspace contract
    - page-local modal and drawer attachment points

    - `uniqueRules` and `autoAssociationRules` both use an alert banner plus one primary action and a non-paginated table
    - `associations` adds search and batch-delete selection on top of the same table workspace contract
    - their business differences remain in columns, deletion logic, selection behavior, and local modals
  - that means the shared part is still the framework shell, not a new CMDB-specific rule shell
- governance decision
  - migrate all three pages to `ManagementTableShell`
  - do not create a new `cmdb-model-detail-rule-*` shared component yet
  - keep alerts, batch actions, row selection, and modal implementations page-local through the framework shell API
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/management/detail/uniqueRules/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/management/detail/autoAssociationRules/page.tsx`
    - `src/app/cmdb/(pages)/assetManage/management/detail/associations/page.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - replaced page-local action-bar-plus-table assembly with `ManagementTableShell`
    - mapped alert guidance into `topSection`
    - mapped search and batch actions into `searchProps` / `actions`
    - preserved all existing modal, delete, toggle, and row-selection workflows
- Storybook plan
  - no new story required
  - current framework source of truth already covers the adopted contract:
    - `Framework/Layouts/ManagementTableShell`

## 2026-07-03 Audited node-manager node workspace and explicitly did not extract a shared shell

  - reviewed page:
    - `src/app/node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/node/page.tsx`

    - `RefreshIconButton`

    - `src/app/node-manager/(pages)/cloudregion/variable/page.tsx`

    - node management is a materially heavier workflow surface with:
      - dynamic column insertion
      - bulk controller and collector operations
      - row selection constraints based on OS and architecture
      - detail drawers and edit modals
      - mode switching into controller-install and collector-operation step flows
      - page state that temporarily replaces the table with operation subflows
  - there is no second page sharing that exact multi-mode node-operation workspace contract
- governance decision
  - do not extract a new shared node-management workspace shell yet
  - keep this page composed from smaller governed primitives
  - revisit only if another page emerges with the same:
    - multi-mode table/step-flow switching
    - bulk component operation grammar
    - node-operation selection constraints
- migration
  - no code migration in this slice
  - this is an explicit no-extract decision to avoid creating a garbage component around one highly specific workflow
- Storybook plan
  - no new story required from this review
  - current source of truth remains split correctly across the smaller node-manager components already in Storybook

## 2026-07-03 Normalized Storybook framework-shell taxonomy for the shared layout layer

- candidate component
  - audited Storybook entries for framework shell components:
    - `src/stories/management-table-shell.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/introduction-table-workspace-shell.stories.tsx`
- why it was reviewed
  - Storybook is the required contract center, so shell components cannot live under inconsistent namespaces
  - the current taxonomy had a drift between:
    - `Framework/Layout/...`
    - `Framework/Layouts/...`
  - that weakens discoverability and makes framework-vs-business classification less trustworthy
- cross-app comparison
  - existing framework-shell stories already converge on singular `Layout`, for example:
    - `src/stories/job-workspace-panel.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/tree-workspace-shell.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/page-header-shell.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/summary-detail-layout-shell.stories.tsx`
  - difference analysis:
    - `ManagementTableShell` and `IntroductionTableWorkspaceShell` were the outliers using `Layouts`
    - both are framework shells, not business stories, so they should follow the same top-level taxonomy as the rest of the layout layer
- governance decision
  - keep both stories in the `Framework` tree
  - normalize them into the singular `Framework/Layout/...` namespace
  - do not create duplicate entries or parallel aliases in Storybook
- migration
  - updated:
    - `src/stories/management-table-shell.stories.tsx`
    - `src/stories/introduction-table-workspace-shell.stories.tsx`
  - migration shape:
    - title namespace only; no component API changes
- Storybook plan
  - continue enforcing:
    - framework primitives and shells under `Framework/...`
    - cross-page business components under `Business/...`
  - next audits should treat taxonomy drift as a governance issue, not a cosmetic one
